channel.title: Iterative Thinking
channel.description: Design, AI, and systems thinking by Ulises Arvizu

item.title: Are you a designer... only when you design?
item.link: https://iterativethinking.substack.com/p/Are you a designer... only when you design?
item.guid: https://iterativethinking.substack.com/p/Are you a designer... only when you design?
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: A few weeks ago, this article by Lowell Stevens motivated my friend Adrián Solca to make this great video, explaining why UX Design has more to do with other things than just designing a screen. His video reminded me of the paradigm from my university years, where people believed that to study graph…
item.content:encoded:
Ed. 01

A few weeks ago, this article by Lowell Stevens motivated my friend Adrián Solca to make this great video, explaining why UX Design has more to do with other things than just designing a screen. His video reminded me of the paradigm from my university years, where people believed that to study graphic design you needed to know how to draw.

Solca's conclusions resonated in my head, because even though I now agree with him, I myself believed something different in my early years learning this discipline; somehow I saw the screen output as what gave meaning to what I did. Coming from a graphic background (my degree is in Design and Visual Communication) it was very easy for me to want to situate design from the Craftsman perspective, the one who produces something. But do you need to produce something tangible to say you design?

Here are my first thoughts in a Twitter thread, after watching Solca's video

Applying a similar analogy: Is the architect the one who makes the architectural project or the one who's at the construction site and knows how to lay bricks? Is the one who knows how to use software and goes through an ideation process to deliver a solution more of a designer, or the one who, through a research process, reframes the problem and finds insights that give us clearer understanding and therefore we can see a more obvious solution?

In art schools, we appropriated the word design but it doesn't really belong to us, not in a totalitarian way, and we forget that our training, however authorial we may be, responds to a process; sometimes ignored, sometimes misunderstood and subjected to the professor's tastes, and later to the client's, but you only need to look at Bruno Munari and the green rice, to understand that designing is always a process, things aren't pretty by whim in graphic design, that's been misunderstood, underneath there's a real decision-making process. Without process there is no design.

Also the concept of design isn't the same, it has changed. An industry with more complex problems and solutions, with bigger teams; design becomes a collaborative activity. The solitary designer, the old west cowboy or the MadMen-style genius in creative or advertising agencies are stereotypes that don't work today. If the design activity doesn't fall on a single person, why do we assume that only the person(s) who produce(s) can be called designer(s).

If we add to this that the father of Design Thinking studied Mechanical Engineering, the founders of Stanford's d.School are mostly engineers, the authors of Lean UX are anything but pencil-and-paper designers, the founders of Nielsen Norman Group come from Engineering and Psychology, and we could mention other cases like Jared Spool, Alan Cooper, none of them designers from art schools. They formed the foundations of the discipline before it was called UX (UX Design as we know it came after 2005) before thinking about Flat design, Skeuomorphism, or Brutalist trends (which by the way, is borrowed from architecture) before systems were sophisticated enough to allow us and demand more compelling and visually refined metaphors. These engineers were doing design, because they applied a process to make decisions.

Finally, graphic design references like Massimo and Lella Vignelli, Lance Wyman, Paul Rand, Paula Scher or Michael Bierut from Pentagram, some of them came from architecture, industrial design and the arts; because graphic design didn't even exist, but they transcended because they saw design as a mindset applicable to solving communication problems, graphics was their medium, but in some cases they even went beyond the graphic. The process of understanding the WHAT and the exploration to get the HOW.

Personally, the craftsman perspective in their workshop designing and producing falls short, it's not wrong, but it's not enough, as short as assuming that doing UX design is just sitting at the screen and knowing about visual organization rules. It's a part, but let's not try to measure the whole by looking at a small fraction.

For me, design is a process, that results in something (the designed), and that is practiced by people with that mindset (designers) in a collaborative way. The outputs are as variable as the scope that the process itself has.

I can understand the good faith of the stance that Lowell's article was looking for, Facebook and some other companies look for Product Designer profiles with a solid process, but pixel perfect execution. Maybe saying that to be a product designer, you need to know UI, or that an effective way to understand the visual configuration of interfaces is by doing design exercises, would have been more accurate.

And you… What do you think? What makes you a designer? What's your definition of design?

To Read or Watch 👀

Spatial Design

This video explains the functionalities that iOS provides as a guide for creating Spatial Design experiences. It helps us understand what we need to consider to create a great experience by making the user see, hear and feel those interactions between physical products. A whole range of new possibilities opens up with this type of physical interactions including VR and AR.

To Listen 🎧

Simulation - Virtual Riot

Contrary to the proposal you received in your welcome email, this time it's a very energizing album, Bass music (EDM) with chip-tune undertones, definitely not music to relax, but to move your feet and head. I recommend the track It could be us

item.title: Communicate and Share
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: I've always found the debate between designers who like to create hype and be protagonists, and those who prefer to stay at the edge of their work.
item.content:encoded:
Ed. 02
Photo by Alexandre Pellaes on Unsplash

I've always found the debate between designers who like to create hype and be protagonists, and those who prefer to stay at the edge of their work.

This newsletter is a test and an attempt at networking, maybe an attempt at visibility, creating hype, why not? But above all, it's about sharing my ideas, so I think my stance is more than clear.

And being honest, writing helps me have clarity in my ideas (or at least I try). The first step before having good ideas is just having ideas - says a famous saying. The first step to having ideas is having clarity about what you think - I would add. When you write your ideas you can look at them differently, you can contrast them with what they are and what they theoretically would be.

Starting from the basics, having eloquence when explaining your process, your project or what you do, is having clarity in your ideas, and when you have that clarity you don't doubt as much about what you're going to say. If you add to this that you can also read your audience, you're empathetic, you understand them and can align their motivations with what you want to communicate to achieve engagement, the power this has is undeniable.

And this is craft. You can't improve if you don't practice, if you don't apply it, if there's no trial and error. Having clarity in your ideas to explain what you've done means facing, even at a minimal level: the vulnerability of being exposed. The fear of being criticized and judged never goes away, that voice that helps you stay humble and not let yourself be devoured by the demon of self-worship, but it never disappears. Communicating effectively should be a soft skill that every designer must develop and cultivate. Even if you don't need to talk much with your stakeholder, or with the client; simply because your clarity in communicating is a reflection of your mental process when designing, of your consciousness in decision-making; that's part of the design process.

This is an open invitation to improve your soft skill of communicating, try writing or drawing your ideas if everything in your head is overwhelming, get them out of there and observe them from outside. That's the beginning.

If this is not your case, and you're good at talking about your progress or presenting a project with your boss, client or stakeholder, then move to the next circle: share what you know.

Yes, I know. Your impostor syndrome will tell you that you don't know enough, that you have nothing interesting to share, that the spotlight isn't made for you. But don't lose sight that what you could share isn't for your peers or those you admire, it's for those who come behind you, those generations who see novelty in your everyday. Sharing your experience is another way to improve that soft skill of communicating.

Writing, commenting on design posts on social media, did someone say making a podcast?, content on instagram or twitter. The simple act of sharing those articles you've read (because you add a small explanation of what you liked and why you share them and find them interesting) don't let the vulnerability of being exposed stop you. Here the only one who won't screw up is the one who does nothing, if your ideas never leave your head, how can you be sure they're correct or that they can be iterated. Share what you know, that's what this is about.

I hope you practice, a lot, no matter what level you're at, no matter if you're an introvert who wants to be heard or a protagonist of conversations. The design community needs to know and learn from those who've been at this for years, at least from those who've been at it longer than the newcomers.

To read or watch 👀

Work and Meta-work

I discovered Jorge Arango's Blog by accident (like most things I find on the internet) author of the famous book Information Architecture: For the Web and Beyond (the O'Reilly polar bear one). I really liked this post; above all, because it talks about a book that personally changed my way of seeing the organization of my productivity: Get Things Done. The idea of Meta-Work. That which you do to be able to do your work, and it's as important as the work itself.

To listen 🎧

Form By Firelight- Jon Hopkins

From 2013. Jon Hopkins with this track makes me feel déjà vu and nostalgia. Glitches to the rhythm of techno that wants to be minimal, piano keys seeking balance between 4x4 and the organic. I hope you enjoy it.

item.title: When Designers Become Robots
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: Lately in my inbox, the multiple newsletters I'm subscribed to have been talking about artificial intelligence, automation, etc. How far does the algorithm know you and suggest things to you, or does it mold you, pulling you into a tunnel of bias?
item.content:encoded:
Automation is knocking at your door. Are you ready to answer?

Ed. 03

Lately in my inbox, the multiple newsletters I'm subscribed to have been talking about artificial intelligence, automation, etc. How far does the algorithm know you and suggest things to you, or does it mold you, pulling you into a tunnel of bias?

And although we could debate ideas like Anticipatory Design, maybe in another edition, the topic that's been echoing in my head is: how far can design be automated.

Naief Yehya, in The Transformed Body said: when humans can transfer their consciousness to hardware, we'll face the problem of defining what makes us human, when the physical body is no longer the obvious answer.

If design were automated, how would our concept of being designers mutate, what direction would it take? And maybe I'm getting ahead of myself because automation will first take over functions that don't necessarily have to do with creativity, but just look at the Tricycle project, their slogan says it: Save time with tools that help you design products powered by Artificial Intelligence.

Here's a tweet from Jordan Singer who's behind Tricycle, opening a door to the possibilities of automation when it comes to making UI

The truth is this happened a long time ago in graphic design with the arrival of computers, many roles had to change and others disappeared, the production chain shortened, because many processes were automated with the arrival of those gray boxes, or the blue boxes called iMac. In fact, this step made it possible for us to venture into the web.

Automation can be a great ally in our work as designers. Although looking at it in a Machiavellian way with a dystopian perspective, maybe one day software could take our jobs…

Although I'm sure the first things to be automated could be screen design or wireframes, making the ideation process perhaps much more effective in time and results; I believe there will still be a human behind it making the final decision. On the other hand, I like to imagine the day we could simulate journeys or use cases like we simulate maps to understand how a disease spreads in real time.

What is experience design (I'm oversimplifying) if not the search for an optimal journey. Those paths—couldn't they be simulated, in real time, all at once?

Doctor Strange is reviewing the multiple possibilities to make an optimal journey and only in one does the user make the decision: yes, I'll subscribe to your service.

That's where things could get interesting. Would we be behind that data, creating or running the algorithms? Our work would change drastically. Would designing mean something else? Would we design an experience, or would we design use cases to be validated in simulations to understand which is more effective? OK, maybe I'm already future-tripping too much…

Leaving the future-tripping aside a bit, I'd like you to keep this in your head: What is your value as designers? The tool doesn't define the designer, that's clear, but then what does? Are you conscious of this in your daily work, what makes you irreplaceable for when automation knocks at the door, so that its purpose is to help you and not replace you.

I said it in another edition: design is not a static concept, so why should our work be. Where do we want to or need to evolve?

Maybe this idea ended with more questions than reflection itself, but if you come up with or have any answers let me know, I'd like to hear your points of view.

To Watch

#MiEmpleoMiFuturo: a documentary about robots, economy, middle class... and the end of the world

A two-part documentary where they talk about the future, technological advances, and how this can impact how the world develops for the following generations.

To Listen

Elastic Heart - Live from The Village

There's not much to say about Sia. Elastic Heart has a very interesting production, here's the original song, but I think this live version does justice to Sia's vocal power.

item.title: Uncertainty and trust in the design process
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: I can't deny the fear I feel sitting in front of this blank page every week after sending the newsletter. What's the topic I'll cover in the next edition? — I ask myself. There's an internal voice telling me I should trust, the words and ideas will connect, it's just a matter of dealing with that un…
item.content:encoded:
How to deal with uncertainty and how the lack of it can be a sign of bias.

ed. 04

I can't deny the fear I feel sitting in front of this blank page every week after sending the newsletter. What's the topic I'll cover in the next edition? — I ask myself. There's an internal voice telling me I should trust, the words and ideas will connect, it's just a matter of dealing with that uncertainty.

Something similar happens in the design process. We feel certain anxiety when we don't have a clear direction, or when there's an open door of multiple possibilities. I've felt that anxiety after receiving feedback or realizing that what I assumed wasn't actually the case.

But uncertainty is inherent to design. When you're going to improve something, you know at least how you'll find the solution (because you'll follow a process) but not the final state of that solution. Innovation in its most essential sense is that: embracing uncertainty to think out of the box. Understanding that the puzzle isn't complete, and maybe never will be, but this is a continuous approach, being something better than what came before.

The designer must explore, develop, and present concepts and ideas toward what could be, but doesn't yet exist.

Continuous experiments during validation help us rule out scenarios and refine that direction. Don't try to solve everything in one stroke — solve one problem at a time. Every time we learn and answer questions, we're iterating. Uncertainty leads us there. It's the yin and yang, design and uncertainty. Better understand that uncertainty (usually there's more than one) and try to solve it, and therefore iterate.

In a less macro sense, how many times have you felt lost? Maybe the more experienced ones have learned to deal with this feeling, which at a minimal level still creates some anxiety. The truth is it never disappears completely.

That feeling can lead us down the easy path of finding quick answers, and our own expertise can play tricks on us. Having quick design solutions can happen because we don't have enough space for ideation, or because past experiences create biases. When you're not scared and you feel comfortable in your design practice, reflect on whether it's one of these two options.

Although we can find similar problems, we shouldn't forget the context. When we go into detail, we'll see that things can be different. Our process can be more or less the same, but the outcome is so variable because we deal with human issues and the ramifications of singularities are infinite. It can be fuzzy, it can be overwhelming — that's why you must trust the process.

As I mentioned paragraphs above, what's important isn't the answer, since it will come naturally and emerge as a great insight once we have all the necessary information. What's important here are the questions, understanding the uncertainty, fragmenting it. So enjoy this journey. When you trust the process, you're conscious of it and you focus on the elements that compose it, not on the solution. Eat the elephant, one bite at a time, because immaturely, we treasure the goal of the solution so much that we forget to enjoy the landscape while we learn, while we solve one by one those questions that give us a better understanding.

What do you think?

To Read or Watch 👀

You Need Tough Feedback—Here's How to Get It | Christine vs. Work

It seems like something cliché, but asking for feedback assertively has its trick. This video is one of those gems you should share, and I think in many cases it will work because the coming months will be a time for retrospective.

To Listen 🎧

Origamibiro - Morel

The project of Thomas William Hill. I discovered Origamibiro through the track Tinder about 3 years ago. Honestly, I was fascinated. Morel is his latest release: samples of string instruments, very dreamlike. I'm leaving the track on Spotify but also his Bandcamp.

item.title: Productivity in Remote Times
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: A few hours ago I had an interesting reflection with my team that made it clear what I needed to write about in this edition. The reality for a little over a year and a half has pushed us toward remote workplaces (in most cases). Celebrated by some; since in Mexico City, where I'm writing this post …
item.content:encoded:
or how to live without guilt when you procrastinate.

Ed. 05

A few hours ago I had an interesting reflection with my team that made it clear what I needed to write about in this edition. The reality for a little over a year and a half has pushed us toward remote workplaces (in most cases). Celebrated by some; since in Mexico City, where I'm writing this post from, there are people who in regular situations could spend up to 4 hours daily commuting from home to office. Others adapting, with whatever they can; a space that was perhaps relegated to social life, or at least to sleeping, into their virtual office. Now we spend hours connected without noticing how the line between life outside work and work itself blurs.

Yes, we know, this isn't new. It happens that without wanting to, the physical boundary between feeling at home and being at work can wreak havoc on us. Colleagues won't deny it, procrastination can be a temptation that roams our now home offices; distractions, for those of us who had to juggle kids and meetings or even pets. Psychologically I think sometimes it's harder to understand when some discomfort is about work or about something in your life or in your home.

A few weeks ago, a family situation; I'm a husband and father of a 4-year-old daughter, led me to adapt my routine. My wife spent a few weeks with limited mobility due to a muscle tear. This pushed me to make double effort to maintain stability in the household routine: breakfast, preparing things for my daughter's school and picking her up. It's not that my routine changed too much, but mentally I entered a phase of mental fatigue, that caused office hours to become complicated, and although I managed to pull through, I reflected on whether I needed to rest, on why I couldn't be as productive as before, and on a certain guilt I had about this.

Routines and organization

I've spent a lot of time reading about productivity, and although I'm by no means the top of organization, I still have much to learn, I have developed strategies that help me have some control over my routines in the home office. I'm a fan of the GTD Method, of starting my day between 5-6 AM and although I follow the popcorn method (dedicate 5 minutes to the task, because that's the minimum time to get hooked and keep going with the task, without realizing it until you finish. Like when without noticing, you eat a whole bag of popcorn). This method is great for when inspiration strikes on a task to complete, but the truth is this wasn't enough for me.

Making yourself present

One of the most obvious things, but very easy to overlook is that we live tormented by the past and the future, the exercise of being present means being conscious of what you're doing in that moment. The past cannot be changed and the future is a fantasy we don't know will happen.

I realized I had days where I spent procrastinating partly because I felt guilty about taking a break, just resting. This kept me in a halfway loop, I wasn't resting because of guilt and when I rested I couldn't stop thinking about work pending (again thinking about the future).

When I talk about making yourself present, I don't mean meditating (although it helps a lot) but being fully in the moment, take a break, but a real one, one that's worth it, a couple of hours resting the mind is worth more, to be recharged; than half a day where you're not even at 20%. When you're at work focus, but don't stress if you don't accomplish everything planned, it rarely happens, but do focus on moving forward, it's not about measuring how much you advance, it's simply about not stopping, advancing a little is better than nothing.

Pareto's Law

Pareto's Law indicates an 80-20 relationship in what you do, 80% of your results will be a reflection of 20% of your effort. So the trick is to focus on that 20% that will give you phenomenal progress, maybe two hours of work in the zone in the purest Joe Gardner in Soul style will give you those advances that were causing you so much anxiety. Productivity isn't about sitting without rest for 8 hours, it's about focusing, and delivering results, sometimes a couple of hours of good ideation work.

This is what I reflected on, right now, things at home are returning to normal, but now I try to be present and focus on that 20.

To Read or Watch 👀

Championing Low-Ego Leadership at Figma

An interesting interview with Dylan Field (CEO and co-founder of Figma) about leadership, fun fact, Dylan took on the CEO role when he was just 20 years old, Figma doubled its market share in the last year.

To Listen… and a bit more 🎧

Mister Lies - False Astronomy

A track I use to clear my mind. Nothing more to say. Oh!, it's not on Spotify that's why I'm sharing the YouTube video

item.title: Metaverse and NFTs, things that make me realize I'm getting old.
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: The hype around the latest news about Facebook (now Meta) and their new approach to what the next generation of internet should be, an immersive internet, makes me feel old. On one hand, I'm excited about a ton of possibilities, but on the other it makes me think about the risks these types of exper…
item.content:encoded:
Old... but I like talking about what's Hype

Ed. 06

The hype around the latest news about Facebook (now Meta) and their new approach to what the next generation of internet should be, an immersive internet, makes me feel old. On one hand, I'm excited about a ton of possibilities, but on the other it makes me think about the risks these types of experiences carry; see, I'm already old. I look back and I'd like to believe I can avoid repeating mistakes from the past; however, when you're young (I'm referring to generations that come after me), you have to find your own path, your own voice, you have to make your own mistakes.

Accepting this fact doesn't exclude me, as someone compulsively driven to understand things, from doing some research and letting my imagination fly. What good would our creative capacity be if we couldn't sketch, at least mentally, the possibilities of something that doesn't exist yet.

First, while what Meta is looking for is very possible to make real, it's estimated that in 2021 Meta's investment in research and development could reach 28.5 billion dollars. I think a lot of the hype is also a response from the culture of immediacy; we're chronic anxious types and we satisfy that anxiety by putting our eyes on what's next and not on the now. The new is shiny and motivating, we want to be in what's coming next, as a guarantee of not losing relevance, we promote any argument that brings us closer to the future. Many of us who are now watching expectantly and those who are actively participating in the NFT movement don't want to repeat the mistake of staying outside a monetary decentralization movement like Bitcoin (yes I know, I'm generalizing). I think the Metaverse is something that will lay its foundation in the coming years, but I don't know if my generation will be able to see the culmination of these efforts. The internet was born in 1969 with ARPANET. But it wasn't until 1990 when the World Wide Web was born with the structure we know today.

But setting aside the discussion of how long it'll take to have a Metaverse (maybe it's sooner than I imagine and it won't even end up being called that) what's a fact is that immersive experiences are permeating more and more. Maybe my 4-year-old daughter will be a native of these technologies. What I see with suspicion today, she'll see as natural, telling me: "Dad, this has always been like this." For now we see the first advances in VR and AR, from other companies besides Meta. I think designers' and developers' work will have great advancement and demand in these types of environments. Immersive experiences require much more precise knowledge about Motion and 3D. Surely our discipline will be enriched by understanding psychological factors of perception and sensation, AI or Machine Learning, as I expressed in edition 03: Our definition of experience designer would have to be redefined.

This brought to mind the premise of the series Upload, a comedy set in 2033 where people who are close to death can be "uploaded" to a virtual afterlife of their choice. The Metaverse is where we'll go to "escape" reality, but it could be where we spend the rest of eternity. Pete Sena describes it as posterity 3.0 in his Medium post. But while one line of thinking makes us think that going for a digital immersive environment would free us from the constraints we have in the physical world; in this Metaverse we can move freely and be who we want to be; the other path, portrayed in both Upload and even movies like Ready Player One, presents a dystopian scenario, where the demons of current society are carried into this digital world. You have to work for resources and few manage to achieve that social mobility in this digital world.

Now, an idea that comes to mind when talking about immersive experiences is: How will we consume multimedia? Today, photographs, videos, and GIFs are the multimedia forms par excellence for sharing memories we treasure like the printed photo albums your grandmother has on her bookshelf. If the next step is reliving moments, today we long for a photograph or video of a loved one; What if we have the possibility to relive moments of our lives, enclosed in VR files I can play back, or better said, live over and over again? This could be the best NFT to sell. What price would the memories of someone like Tesla or Gandhi have, with the possibility of living them firsthand, through a VR interface? Now, a more corporate application: Analyzing reconstructed facts with AR video, in pure Minority Report style, audits and processes could have unprecedented clarity. Without a doubt, the technologies that building a Metaverse can unleash could lead us to scenarios and use cases we can't see today.

Part of me has some resistance and weariness, but the other part is excited, like that teenager who browsed for the first time in Netscape, like when I downloaded an MP3 for the first time in Napster, met people from other countries thanks to IRC chats, or first used an iPod and then an iPhone. Those moments evoke freedom for me. Maybe for many the Metaverse and NFTs evoke the same thing. My goal is to be able to understand the possibilities in a general way. You can't be 100% prepared to evolve with the labor demands of the next 10 years, but you can stay informed, and maybe keep being part of it, perhaps in a way we can't imagine today.

To Read or Watch 👀

Why I Don't Get Distracted - 4 Principles To Control Your Attention and Your Life

Connected to the previous edition, if you're new and want to know what it was about you can read it here. This video gives us the basics for not getting distracted, very general rules and for the more expert a good reminder to stay focused and guilt-free.

To Listen... and a bit more 🎧

# Rone - Bye Bye Macadam

This time I share the official video which is amazing, black and white motion graphics that fill this playful track with mysticism. I use this one to get my second wind, hope you like it.

Note: the previous edition came out with more writing errors than usual, apologies, we now run the grammar checker three times

item.title: Things I've learned leading a design team.
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: Disclaimer: I'm no longer leading a team, I'm now an individual contributor but I want to add to the conversation.
item.content:encoded:
After almost a year and a half of going from Senior designer to Lead, these are the things I've learned.

Ed.07

Disclaimer: I'm no longer leading a team, I'm now an individual contributor but I want to add to the conversation.

If your goal is to climb the ladder and manage design teams, you need to be prepared. In my case, even though I had been looking for it for a while, the truth is it happened overnight. I went from being a team member to overseeing my colleagues' work.

Being a team manager is a role. I think leadership is a soft skill you can cultivate without having people under you. However, the truth is that neither companies nor designers themselves prepare to get there, whether because of a lack of a clear career path, or because you need to look at skills that go beyond what designing implies. You're going to deal with people and your job won't be execution, at least not design execution. Your job is to align the motivations of the people on your team with the objectives that, as a team and at a more macro level, as a company you need to achieve.

So dear reader, if you're aspiring to be a manager—whether because it's the only path to promotion (sorry, life is like that), because they threw the challenge at you at your office or job, or because you're genuinely drawn to it and have at least noticed that leadership comes easily to you—I ask that at the very least, you seek information, cultivate that part that has more to do with relationships between humans. Leading a design team will put you in front of challenges that have little to do with execution. You'll have to deal more with negotiation and with helping those who collaborate on your team grow.

Paying close attention to team dynamics, reading several books, and above all learning quickly from my mistakes—which were many—these are some of the things I want to share with you.

Trust in people that fosters radical transparency.

First, you need to understand that beyond reviewing tasks, measuring productivity, and sticking to a plan called strategy; you work with people.

That's why it's important to understand their motivations, because as a manager whether you like it or not, you're going to deal with their moods. Believe me, no matter how much effort you put in, the productivity graph will never be perfect. People tend to be more productive when their moods are positive. The context in which team members find themselves will influence things for better and worse, and that's inevitable. That's why you need to know your team in every sense if you want to take advantage of their potential and help them grow. Because it's not about the work they're currently doing, it's about the work they could potentially do when motivated.

The pandemic taught me that I had to replace spaces like the "coffee moment" or "informal hallway chats" with other dynamics in a fully remote environment. It's very easy to lock yourself into a circle of only meetings, talking only about work. That space to talk about life and friends no longer exists, there's no going out for lunch. These are the spaces that would help you understand the motivations and context of the people on your team.

So facilitate spaces where your team can connect beyond work. In my case, we organized virtual lunches, free afternoons to get together and chat (beer included) as if it were a bar, where the rule is not to talk about work, because we want to talk about ourselves, tell jokes, share stories, break the cycle of work meetings: to know each other as individuals.

And why do we want this? Very simple, by creating bonds with members, they'll feel confident enough to express how they feel to you. Sometimes they just need to be heard; but if they can trust you with how life is going, they'll be able to trust you when something isn't right in a project, when they see something you can't see, even when that goes against your ideas. Collaboration and support are mutual—if you give trust, you'll receive radical transparency.

This is one of the hardest parts to learn. By default, I'm someone who likes to have a dominant or very proactive role when discussing ideas. When you're a manager, you need to understand that this personality can make people lose motivation to express their ideas. You need to sometimes take a step back and try to be the last one to speak.

I confess this has been something that's cost me a lot of work (even without people under me I try to practice active listening). The interesting thing about this exercise is what you can achieve. On one hand, your team expresses wild ideas (when in ideation processes this is fundamental), ideas contrary to what you think. Otherwise, you can fall into biases, since everyone is looking in the same direction, or worse, in the direction you set. On the other hand, your own team will build confidence to come up with solutions and maybe won't even need your intervention. Better yet, they propose something that can only be the result of teamwork, instead of a proposal from a single person.

Be consistent: practice by example and find allies on your team

You can't expect the team to do the work automatically. You need to be consistent. It's easy to align goals, but it's equally easy for them to be forgotten and end up in a file in Mural, Miro, or Figma. It's not easy to be the one always reminding everyone of the objectives, reminding them of the agreements. I'm convinced that building habits and good practices is not a simple exercise, however, that can define the culture your team has.

You're not alone. Find allies who help you with this task. I call them ambassadors, who help ensure you're not the crazy one always reminding them that they need to follow a process, methods, etc. Ambassadors are catalysts who will help you develop good practices on the team. It will be your job to identify them and motivate them to exercise that leadership on the team.

However, don't be inflexible either, and always ask yourself about the objective of what you're asking for. Flow with the waves, because otherwise what you're seeking as a good practice can become a meaningless checklist to fill out. Seek consistency in understanding the why. The consequence of understanding it, externalizing it, and even receiving feedback from your team, will lead you to develop strategies and processes that were validated in practice and that you didn't just adopt from reading the trendy book on Design Ops.

Listening is not enough, you need to follow up on what was agreed.

Listening is not enough; you need to be attentive to the concerns of your team members. I'm not saying you should do everything they say, I'm saying you need to listen to them and help them grow. And that means not throwing overboard those initiatives that proactive members have. Learn to channel those concerns, to help ensure they're not seeds without fruit.

I wish we could promote all designers; but the structures in companies where there are design teams rarely work that way. The career path implies the person's growth in the company, but on the other side; you need to help the designer grow toward the direction they're seeking. You can help them obtain a hard or soft skill, without the promise of promoting them—that's the help real leaders offer. You need to be open to hearing that your collaborators might leave, but it's very different for them to leave because they're not happy versus leaving because they have an opportunity they're ready for and you helped them in their learning (we know that if it were up to you, you would have promoted them already).

These are some of the things I can recap. Personally, I recommend Julie Zhuo's book The Making of a Manager and Liz Wiseman's Multipliers.

`To Read or Watch 👀

Is design your love or addiction?

An interesting reflection on the danger of obsessing over design processes and not over outcomes.

To Listen… 🎧

The UX Rebels ep. 36 (24 temp 02): Liderazgo y Management

In this chat I had with Benjamin Garcia, Viviana tells us about her definition of leadership and how she has developed it. You'll see we have points in common—it pairs well with what I wrote in this edition.

item.title: What to Do When You Have a 1:1?
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: 1:1 meetings (read One to one) are among the most enriching things, in every sense of the word, that I've experienced.
item.content:encoded:
I'm not talking about when you organize them; I'm talking about when you're part of one.

Ed 08

1:1 meetings (read One to one) are among the most enriching things, in every sense of the word, that I've experienced.

My topic isn't about how to run them. It's about what you should do when your boss calls you to one. It might be your first time or maybe you've always wondered as an individual contributor: What do I have to do? Coming prepared will give you advantages.

Defining 1:1s for individual contributors

First let's define (from my experience and opinion) these solo meetings with your boss, which range from weekly, biweekly, monthly and in some cases, not the most recommended from my point of view, quarterly or even annual frequency (if you have people reporting to you and you do this, please stop and meet more often with your collaborators). The idea is to follow up and avoid snowball problems. If we can prevent something or solve it when it's small we won't have monumental fires at work taking over our lives.

The idea is that you can use these meetings to understand your growth, be more conscious of it and in a secondary way, understand your boss's and your company's motivations and align them reciprocally.

It's about you, so be conscious.

A 1:1 conversation is not for your boss, they surely have one with theirs. It's about you, so always try to come prepared, do a review: What have I achieved? What do I need to achieve? But do it seriously before your session. Take notes of the agreements and use them as a thread, because you need to follow up. Your boss is the vehicle, however, you are the gasoline that moves the machinery.

If being eloquent isn't your thing, bring notes of the ideas you want to discuss and their order, write them down, clarity in writing will reflect clarity in thinking.

It's not just about work

And although it might always be related; the truth is that in a 1:1 you can discuss personal topics. If your boss already took the leap to do a 1:1, let yourself flow with radical transparency, how you feel and how you feel affects your work, but it also sketches paths, understanding a problem always gives us guidelines to understand possible solutions.

Basically, the 1:1 is not the moment to receive feedback, but the moment to give it, that's why your boss will be the one doing active listening, and it's a moment to have that openness. Having them often will make it easier to follow up and change things when they're not working.

A boss once told me, Ulises what is said in a 1:1 stays in this 1:1, so feel free it's a safe space, let's talk about you.

Why did this topic come up in this edition? As social beings we see the end of cycles as a way to reflect and have retrospective; although being honest you can start at any moment. Check where you want to go, and check what steps are necessary to get where you want, and if in your case there aren't many 1:1s you could propose them, as a way to touch base and stay communicated.

To Read or Watch 👀

Defining objectives you can actually achieve: What is an OKR?

Humans are good at many things, but something we're really not good at is making plans. Every time we set an objective it's like a sentence for us not to achieve it, but it doesn't need to be that way. Understanding a bit about how our brain works and the tools we have to face it can help you establish plans that can actually be achieved. That's what we'll talk about in this program.

To Listen... and a bit more 🎧

How to do behavioral design

This episode came out a while ago, I actually listened to it on Spotify, but it's a basic to approach behavioral economics, explained simply and in Spanish, this is simply unmissable.

item.title: On design, tasks and outcomes
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: A few weeks ago I was thinking about my process and its scope. When working on a design systems team, one of the deliverables is the documentation of the designed component.
item.content:encoded:
The difference between what you design and your deliverables.

Ed. 09

A few weeks ago I was thinking about my process and its scope. When working on a design systems team, one of the deliverables is the documentation of the designed component.

But recently I stopped to reflect: What is the objective of my design process? A deliverable like a document? My first thoughts pointed to this deliverable serving as the link that allows the next phase to work (development). The designed object is this document. But I spend more time thinking about the solution than documenting it. The documentation is just instructions for my solution. My solution is the interesting part. However, are they the same thing? Or are they different things…

Then, like a revelation, the following idea emerged while I was in the shower. In that moment where you're not thinking about anything, but you can think about everything…

Imagine I'm a designer. I have a workshop and I've designed a chair. Think Herman Miller but in a custom version, cheaper, with a touch of DIY so customers can assemble it at home.

I've spent months in the understanding phase, where I understand the problems with current chairs. I've talked to users and understand the opportunity areas in the market. Also, I'm clear that the chair needs to be comfortable, with a relatively accessible price and manufacturing (we're not for everyone, but not for just a few either). But yes—the best in terms of design and materials. Now I've spent months in ideation, validating ideas, understanding which ones are more feasible to make without losing sight of the limitations and requirements I outlined earlier.

Done! After several tests I found the design I need, and I like it. So I'll do an initial production run. One of the objectives is to allow users to assemble it in their own home. Therefore, we need to include instructions on how to do it. The chair is designed to be assembled easily by the customer/user themselves, so I need an instruction manual, a printed paper, even a PDF that will tell the user how to assemble their chair and allow them to use it. That instruction manual enables the tangibility of the chair, since without it, the chair is nothing more than a box with disassembled pieces.

So what is the instruction manual? The chair is what allows the user to sit, but the instruction manual is the link that allows the user to assemble the chair (implement the design in a certain way) and use it. Does this sound familiar to the documentation I mentioned at the beginning?

A "task" is not the same as what you "design"

In my analogy it's easy to understand how what is designed (the chair) and what is a task (the instruction manual) are separated. In this case, a task helps what is designed fulfill its function and objective. I didn't do a design process to create documentation, or to make an instruction manual for assembling the chair. I did a design process to understand the problem to solve: A user needs to sit for many hours and wants a comfortable chair, that's not expensive, and this allows selling more chairs at scale.

Understanding the problem, we can look for a solution, what is designed (this is what my design process is for too). This solution is only the means. Instead of a chair, think of an app, a website, or a service. All of the above was designed to achieve an objective, like the user buys something, comes back for more, uses your service more, etc. This is an Outcome.

Outcome

That's why it's important to understand the different levels: tasks help us with what we're going to design. A wireframe won't be part of the app, nor a journey. Those are tasks that will help us make decisions. They're links between micro processes, and those decisions will be reflected in our design, that is: what is designed. But the worst thing you can do is design without understanding the other level: the Outcome.

It's NOT about making the most fancy, intuitive app, web or service, one that takes the user into account, that will bring ROI to the company. No, it's not about that. It's about the app, web, service or... the chair allowing the user/client/etc. to do something and as a consequence being intuitive, easy to use and memorable. (Catch the difference).

Recap

Task: what allows us to create "what is designed". In my analogy it's "the instruction manual".
What is designed: what solves a problem, the solution. In this case "the chair".
Outcome: the ultimate end we want to achieve or impact on users. In this case it's "being able to sit comfortably for many hours".

We should never forget the Outcome we want to achieve. This will help us make decisions about the time and effort we sometimes unintentionally put into a task instead of putting it into what is designed. The task helps, it's necessary, but don't spend extra time on it, because it's just a step. What will help us achieve the Outcome is what is designed. Dedicate a lot of time to the second without losing sight of the first.

Daily work and seeing things in a fragmented way (we're part of a team in many cases) makes us focus too much on process details. It's always good to zoom out to see the complete picture and not forget the Outcome we're doing things for.

Disclaimer.

Beyond Outcome, which I take from Joshua Seiden and his book Outcomes over Outputs, what is designed and task are concepts I use for what I want to explain. I don't doubt someone else has already called them something different. If you have references on this, let me know.

To Read or Watch 👀

Managers make organizations, not products

I shared a few weeks ago on LinkedIn this brilliant post by Tanner Christensen that talks about the difference in impact between a Manager and an Individual contributor with influence. The first impacts the team and the second impacts the product.

To Listen... and a bit more 🎧

Max Cooper - Live at the Acropolis

unmissable concert, even to leave playing in the background while you work

item.title: Designing 10 Thousand Years Into the Future
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: A few months ago I watched the Dune movie, and although I knew the work by references, I had never sat down to read it (I just finished the book a few days ago). The film struck me as majestic and visually compelling—the production design is top-notch. You feel like you're in another universe, a wor…
item.content:encoded:
What never changes in design across time?

Note: If you enjoy reading Iterative Thinking, feel free to share it.
Ed 10

A few months ago I watched the Dune movie, and although I knew the work by references, I had never sat down to read it (I just finished the book a few days ago). The film struck me as majestic and visually compelling—the production design is top-notch. You feel like you're in another universe, a world that doesn't have much connection to our current one.

The movie takes place in year 10,000, in the future, and it's curious because even though we see monumental technology, interstellar travel, and humanity having conquered planets and spread across the universe, we don't see computers, robots, or artificial intelligence the way science fiction films usually show us.

While watching in the theater, I kept thinking about what it would be like to design instruments like that, 10 thousand years in the future—devices that submit entirely to their function, brutalist architecture with minimalism and feudal touches. What would it be like to design experiences in environments where technology is purely functional, not entertainment or leisure?

The problem and needs change, the process not so much...

Leaving the theater I asked myself: What would it be like to design in the next 10 years? Metaverse, NFTs, VR, AR, AI, etc. And also—you never know what might appear in 10 years, something disruptive that nobody had planned in their roadmap.

The tools can change, but the process not so much...

Five years ago I was using Photoshop to make interfaces, then I moved to Sketch and currently use Figma. I've used multiple tools, some that no longer exist like Pixate, I used Origami Studio before Meta made it friendly, I was an early adopter of Adobe XD, InVision, and UXPin, and went through the dilemma of trying to consolidate everything between design and developers with Fuse Tools or Framer. I've fallen in love with some tools momentarily, but the industry has forced me to move on and go further. Now I can use these and other tools for digital workshops, and I've forgotten about post-its—in Miro, Mural, FigJam. Presentations in PDF for stakeholders and directors who don't care if they were made in Keynote, PowerPoint, or Google Slides. And still, I keep sketching with paper and pencil. In reality, the tool is the least important thing—if there's no substance, they're just hollow artifacts.

We're not sure what the future holds, we never will be. We can play at being fortune-tellers or try to understand the course of things with foresight (Future Design). But we'll never have solid certainty about what will happen. The only certainty is that change is constant, we must adapt, and perhaps the thing that will change the least in a general sense will be our design process.

Needs change, tools change, contexts change, even the complexity of deliverables. But the mindset, the process, the framework—adapted and understood—tends to stay the same: understanding problems, solving them by delivering value (taking into account the implications and readings this may have).

So I like to think that designing 10 thousand years in the future will just mean changing the needs and tools, but our process of analyzing and understanding in order to solve will transcend. And if it can transcend 10 thousand years into the future, it can do the same for the next 5, 10, or 20 years.

The design process doesn't change—if anything, it adapts. But that's the value we have as designers, because the tools and deliverables will keep changing over time. How we arrive at those solutions (the process) is what will truly remain.

Happy holidays! See you in the next edition before 2021 ends.

To Read 👀

The ROI of Design Doesn't Exist

Sol Mesz brings us this interesting article, where she talks about how it's impossible to have a universal metric for Design ROI—this is a must-read article for those who want to justify decisions with business.

We had Sol in an interesting interview on The UX Rebels, where she told us about The Full Loop Analytics Framework.

To Listen, well and watch… 🎧

Paolo Miscia: How Close Is the Metaverse Promised by Facebook for Society and Media?

Mauricio Cabrera (creator of Story Baker) in his podcast The Coffee interviews Paolo Miscia, former Digital Research director at Discovery Latin America and creator of Al Metaverso, talking about the Metaverse and its implications. Here's the episode on Spotify if you want audio only.

item.title: Before 2021 Goes
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: 2021 is about to end, and we've reached edition 11 of this newsletter, 11 of 52 editions which is the goal I set when I decided to jump into this project. The fact that it's the last week of the year doesn't stop me from writing.
item.content:encoded:
The last newsletter of this year

Ed. 11

2021 is about to end, and we've reached edition 11 of this newsletter, 11 of 52 editions which is the goal I set when I decided to jump into this project. The fact that it's the last week of the year doesn't stop me from writing.

By nature we tend to see the end of a year as the closing of a cycle and an opportunity to start a new one in the best possible way (aspiring to do it better than the previous cycle). That's not bad, but we don't need a year to end to begin a new goal, so don't get overwhelmed if you're still not clear on what you want to achieve in 2022. What matters isn't just the goal but that you manage to improve something in your life; even working less and enjoying life more, disconnecting from social media and reading less on screens (just don't stop reading Iterative Thinking please, ha!). You're the one who decides what improvement means, so reflect on it and make it happen.

This 2021 left me with a lot of learning, especially personally. I've learned to deal with the guilt of not achieving everything I set out to do. I understood that you have to enjoy the journey even if our objective is to reach the finish line, because reaching the finish line is just a moment, one that tastes better when you enjoyed the trip. So, I've learned to let go.

Learning to let go in life is the same as letting go of certain ideas when you design. Some don't fully mature, sometimes they don't turn out as you planned. You'll have to adapt, be flexible and be open to other options that at first you didn't even imagine.

But that doesn't mean you shouldn't plan, rather that you should be ready to improvise, to adapt. You'll see other landscapes (ideas), you'll have to take other paths, and inevitably you'll reach the finish line (the solution to the problem), it's just that maybe the journey won't be as you originally planned.

It's never perfect. So swim with the wave, not against it

Before you feel frustrated, I recommend you focus on advancing little by little. Personally, I have the goal in 2022 of reading more (I mean books), and I've managed in the last two months to go from doses of 5-10 minutes to reading at least 45 minutes a day (at least 4 days out of the 5 work days, what I read on the weekend is a plus). But the first week I read nothing, I couldn't find that moment to do it. I stopped focusing on covering my reading time objective and focused only on reading, even if I only read one or two pages. A little is better than nothing, and your brain will stop fighting against that new habit you're trying to form. Without realizing it I achieved that 45-minute session I was looking for, divided into smaller sessions, but this happened around the fourth week.

The trick is to keep going with the plan even if you don't achieve it one hundred percent. 10 minutes of whatever is better than the idea of wanting to do it. And if one day you achieve nothing, it doesn't matter, keep trying.

When we design we have to zoom out from the complete picture and understand what works and what doesn't. We can apply the same to what we do every day. This is an internal dialogue you need to have, what for and why do you do things? I won't deny the influence the outside world exerts on us, the culture that praises hyper-productivity and social media idealizing the status quo: perfect people. But it's not about comparing yourself with your neighbor, but with yourself. Try to be an improved version of yourself and in 3 months, 6 months or a year, see that result.

I wish you all the best for this 2022! Today there won't be distractions (recommendations), enjoy with your loved ones and if you feel like it, write me a message, whether on Twitter or email telling me what you're hoping for in 2022.

item.title: The Service of Cancellation
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: A few days ago, I wanted to cancel an insurance policy I had through an Amex card. I called, they verified me as a client, and they simply said "very well sir, we'll proceed with your cancellation, but before we do I'd like to tell you a few things…" Less than 5 minutes and the person on the phone h…
item.content:encoded:
Two radically different experiences for a similar process.

Ed. 12

A few days ago, I wanted to cancel an insurance policy I had through an Amex card. I called, they verified me as a client, and they simply said "very well sir, we'll proceed with your cancellation, but before we do I'd like to tell you a few things…" Less than 5 minutes and the person on the phone had given me enough reason not to cancel the insurance. And at the end she said, "but if you'd like, we can cancel it right now."

Maybe I wasn't sure, maybe I didn't have all the details about the benefits this insurance gave me, but feeling that it was so easy to cancel made me say: think it over, if you still want to you can call back and cancel.

Yesterday, I got a call from Banorte, telling me I had an outstanding balance; it was true—there was a card I never cancelled, that I no longer used and had forgotten about, and they were charging me the last annual fee. I set out to do everything necessary to cancel it: I paid the amount I owed, called their Call center and after almost 45 minutes, 3 call attempts that got cut off and more than 10 department transfers (because I think it was very difficult to reach the cancellations area and they kept sending me elsewhere by mistake), the process was torture, but I got it done.

The quick answer is that they want you to give up on cancelling. Which might seem obvious and clever (well no, it's not clever), it's like saying that to get you to come back to a restaurant I'm going to make it hard to get the check. Have you ever cancelled Spotify or Netflix? Have you noticed it's just as easy to cancel the service as it is to sign up? I've done it and I've come back. Medium, Skill Share, are other examples of services that are so easy to cancel that you don't hesitate to return when you actually need them.

To prevent you from cancelling, I'll make it hard

And yes, maybe the cancellation metric will go down. But what you should be investigating is the cause of why people want to cancel. Having happy clients who transact a lot for the same reason can be better than having many clients—retaining them by making it hard to leave your service is a strategy that can bring you a bad reputation, that makes people not speak well of the service you offer, and let's remember that bad publicity can take you down.

When you design Sign up experiences, have you put the same attention into designing cancellation experiences, to make them as easy as possible?

That's my reflection for now, happy start to 2022.

To Read 👀

Bias by design

Jon Yablonski is a Senior UX Designer and author of this great site:

This website is a resource for breaking down the most common cognitive biases that designers must face during the design process. It's by no means an exhaustive list, but a deep dive into the most common ones during the design process. These are also the ones that can influence design decisions and the products and services we help build.

To Listen, well and watch… 🎧

MURCOF @ Paquimé, Chihuahua

Murcof (Fernando Corona Murillo) is a Mexican electronic music producer based in Europe. I've been following his work since he made music for the Colectivo Nortec as Terrestre. Murcof blends evocations of minimal techno, sonic experimentation, and classical music. This concert was in 2019—while you can leave it on in the background, the visuals definitely illustrate what you're hearing very well.

item.title: Time, a Key Constraint in Design
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: I'm starting the book Homo Deus, this second part (I haven't read the first one, which is Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind, hopefully that's not a problem) interested me because the author tries to at least sketch paths about the future and technology.
item.content:encoded:
Ed. 13

I'm starting the book Homo Deus, this second part (I haven't read the first one, which is Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind, hopefully that's not a problem) interested me because the author tries to at least sketch paths about the future and technology.

The author tells us that for now human efforts are focused on improving ourselves, acquiring super-human capabilities, like when you use your phone to not remember things, or like when someone without legs can walk with bionic prosthetics. It might sound a bit dystopian and very much in the style of the 1997 film Gattaca; but it's real, advances in medicine are first used to solve problems and then to improve us. Although the definition of improving us can be subjective.

On the other hand, man is seeking permanence, immortality. The survival age range is the highest in all of human history, and from a social point of view, this can bring long-term changes. If I can live 100 years, why would I retire from work at 60? It wouldn't be sustainable, and if I can live forever, what would my concept of time be?

This dilemma of immortality is drawn in an interesting way in Altered Carbon, we're not eternal, but our consciousness is digital and basically we just need a carrier body, a vehicle to keep living.

If we were immortal, what would be the meaning of life? We obsess over making the most of it (however that's understood), because it's finite, because one day it ends. We have only one opportunity to live, to produce and to consume; if we're eternal we could do a thousand things, we could change life paths, we could keep learning things or not… as Elon Musk thinks: as we grow we tend to stagnate and our way of thinking tends not to change, for him we must die, because our ideas can persist and this would prevent us from progressing.

But in Design, time is a key constraint, the concept of iteration is what helps us reconcile the idea of having an imperfect or unfinished design; but also, it's what helps us see this not as a defect, but as the idea of change as something constant. My imperfect or unfinished solution is only better than the previous version and always aspires to become the next better version. But if we lived eternally, what's the rush to get to that next version?

Time would take on a contemplative meaning, but never a factor that could pressure us. For my 5-year-old daughter a year is a lot, for me who's almost 40 it doesn't seem like much, for someone who lives eternally, what would waiting a year mean?

And I make this reflection with the idea of understanding how time is a finite resource and precisely that characteristic that seems to work against us, because there's never enough time for design, for the process, actually works in our favor, the idea that we must stop. I can't have a design better than the previous one if I don't stop to conclude, because I need the concluded design to understand its limitations and be able to understand what I did right and what I did wrong, but this only happens through time. The anxiety of knowing we're finite is what motivates us to keep seeking the best solution while we have time.

I don't know how this will be when we're immortal, but in contrast I believe that having limited time helps me design better.

To Read 👀

Design Critique Culture

Feedback is one of the most important parts in the design process. In this article, Boris Müller gives us a guide so that Design Critique becomes the trigger for a project's success.

To Listen 🎧

My Appearance on a UXBS Podcast!

Cristobal Lemoine interviewed me a few weeks ago on a UXBS podcast. I talked a bit about my experience building trust in design teams and how you can naturally align your collaborators' interests with those of the company, a win-win relationship. Hope you like it.

item.title: Design as a craft
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: I remember I was always curious about computers, I learned to use Photoshop just by clicking around, version 5.0 and on PC in case you were wondering. When I learned digital editing, what is now basically how all design in the world is done, I wanted to conquer the world.
item.content:encoded:
We can read a thousand books, but design is learned by doing.

Ed. 14

I remember I was always curious about computers, I learned to use Photoshop just by clicking around, version 5.0 and on PC in case you were wondering. When I learned digital editing, what is now basically how all design in the world is done, I wanted to conquer the world.

I thought I knew everything, I was young, as Bart Simpson would say "the future is now, old man". I watched how my professors could subtly spot the errors in my primitive compositions and illustrations. They could detect mistakes just by glancing at them for an instant. I didn't understand how they did it. Were they geniuses?

One professor made us draw horizontal lines every 5 millimeters freehand in 20 x 20 cm squares. At least 20 times throughout an entire semester. I never understood the exercise, I just know that by the middle of my degree I could distinguish very well when something was 5 millimeters apart and when it wasn't.

The act of designing, from thinking to executing, is not a cyclical and repetitive process, but rather a spiral, with similarities that can be confused with repetitions, because each time we get better, little by little.

I remember what my Design I professor told us in the first class:

To be the best designer, it's not enough to understand visual composition, Gestalt principles, or use graphic expression materials. To be a designer you need to learn about everything, every project that lands in your hands is an opportunity and a duty to learn something, if you design a menu, you'll learn about cooking, if you make a brochure for an exhibition you'll have to know the work. The more you design, the more you'll know and therefore you'll be a better designer, your work represents your last design, because that's where everything you've learned in your entire craft shows.

And the thing is, design is a craft, it's learned in practice and refined over the years. We can read thousands of books, but reality is not by the book. But the dopamine we release with every micro-interaction with our devices has conditioned us, we live craving the next big thing and I see how newcomer designers want to replicate this same consumption pattern to their career.

If you're trying to enter the UX industry, I recommend patience. Maybe the bootcamp, course or certification you took is like a Netflix series, first season in one sitting; but your professional growth will be more like a show that airs weekly and with commercials, you go little by little. The designers who know more than you, the ones who really know more than you, have years, years of practice.

Although I know you might feel like I did when I learned to use Photoshop, I'll just tell you the world isn't eaten in one bite, not when you design. The world is eaten in small bites and with each bite, as you savor it, you learn something. It took me some years to understand that technical skills aren't enough like thinking, like reflecting and trying to understand the context of why we find ourselves facing a project to which we will provide a solution.

My teachers weren't geniuses, they had simply developed a good eye for seeing the deficient compositions of their students. My eye, after a semester of making straight lines by hand separated by 5 millimeters, could see when that distance wasn't exact elsewhere.

The marketing of the education industry makes you believe that climbing the ladder is like leveling up in a video game, junior, Semi-Senior and Senior, Lead, and then you zoom out a little and you'll see that whoever was Lead in one place is barely Senior in another. Don't rush, and don't pretend to absorb someone's years of experience in months because it doesn't happen.

Design is learned by doing, and I'm not referring only to executing, because I can already see the strategists wanting to kill me. I mean you must apply and practice what you learned in school, courses and certifications, and when you apply it you'll see the difference, you'll reflect on what you learn and with that you become better.

To Read 👀

Base Components

Joey Banks is starting a very interesting newsletter about Design, UI and Design Systems and Figma. His third edition talks about how to create base components, centralizing elements can bring you benefits for making changes in the future, for Figma nerds

To Listen or Watch 🎧

Jobii

I'm a fan of Jobii's music, here's a bit about his creative process and what it means for him to make music for a service like Epidemic Sound

item.title: UI is not UX
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: A few years ago, my perspective on design led me to somewhat disdain execution. I'm a visual designer and communicator, so UI execution was my entry point into this world; however, I let myself be influenced by a perspective where execution wasn't the important thing, because, I came to think, it's …
item.content:encoded:
My problem with this kind of argument used to put oneself above others

Ed. 15

A few years ago, my perspective on design led me to somewhat disdain execution. I'm a visual designer and communicator, so UI execution was my entry point into this world; however, I let myself be influenced by a perspective where execution wasn't the important thing, because, I came to think, it's something very replaceable. The truth is I think I got confused in my intention to substantiate design and went to the extreme.

I remember very well what I learned from my friend Paola González back then. She's an industrial designer by training, but professionally she's worked as a Service Designer, she's very good at using design frameworks and facilitating multiple workshops, her work lives in the first diamond, or in Discovery, in analysis and even ideation; that is, she was undoubtedly a strategist; but, something I could observe from the beginning was her visual sensibility, her admiration for the good execution needed to make good ideas tangible. For her, execution was as fundamental as strategy.

I had a deep internal reflection: How can someone who comes from the strategic side understand the importance of execution while I, coming from that place, was seeing it as something lesser? Perhaps that level of importance I had mentally drawn was an illusion. In frameworks like the double diamond or Design Thinking we don't see one phase as more important than another; in any case, a series of steps are outlined that must be followed, all are necessary, same importance.

Since then I've tried to have a more holistic approach. It's not one against the other, it's everyone designing the experience. A good strategy is nothing if it's not executed, an execution can be hollow and superficial if it doesn't understand the why of things.

When people say UI is not UX, I agree, and don't misunderstand me, the truth is UI is not UX; UI is part of UX. We're not talking about antagonistic things but two things with an intrinsic relationship. We fragment them only to understand deliverables and in many cases to define roles; but, there's no good UI without understanding the user, and there's no good UX that doesn't lead to a UI. There are no apps out there in black and white that are just workflows, or journeys or interviews, and it's very easy to see when a UI makes no sense, isn't usable, and is only made to demonstrate how many effects I can put in a design. If we do another zoom out, what the user actually sees and uses is an interface (thanks devs) deployed on their device.

But then are UX and UI the same thing?

No. Having a relationship and not being antagonistic doesn't make them the same. We need to have a systemic approach to this:

Let me share a great and fun analogy I read from Ulises Estrada:

A taco al pastor is not a tortilla + prepared meat + cilantro + onion + pineapple + lime (optional)… A taco al pastor is the sum of all its parts. You can differentiate its parts, you can understand how they work and when they're present, but on their own none of them is a taco al pastor, only together they are.

For those not from Mexico, here's the taco al pastor

That's why I think the debate UI is not UX is a pointless debate. You don't sit there arguing: What's better, the tortilla or the salsa? Of course, they're different things, and they serve different purposes, but each one alone is not the experience of eating a taco al pastor.

My problem arises when people want to differentiate themselves to justify that only they have the voice of the user, or that they do the real intellectual work. Basically they want to put themselves above others, because they do things others don't do, only they bring the powerful insight that changes industries and they climb up on their little brick.

No. The debates shouldn't revolve around who's more important and how we differentiate ourselves. Let's zoom out to see the whole picture and collaborate in this value chain where we're just another link, sometimes catalyzers and evangelizers, but that only happens when the other links can't see us, not because we're more important. This is user-centered, not design-centered.

Now, speaking of roles: Are these carried out by different people or the same person? Well, it will depend on the team size, the budget, and the project complexity, on the profiles (generalists or specialists). Sure! Defined roles across different team members make deep work more effective, but we can't always do that. What we should aspire to is delivering the best experience possible.

The debates should be about how we collaborate better, about how we gain a greater understanding of other areas, our scope, and how we create greater impact. The user will never say: fire the person who did the information architecture because you can tell the researchers didn't listen and that's why their UI isn't intuitive. No, the client just says: I love this or this is garbage…

What do you think?

Para Leer 👀

Turn method into magic—the alchemy of prototyping

David Hoang has his blog Proof of Concept, where he has this great article about prototyping. What's it for and how should we use it in the design process? It seems obvious at first glance, but we shouldn't forget that we must clarify expectations. A prototype isn't just the pleasurable act of animating a sequence of static screens without code.

Para Escuchar o escuchar 🎧

Eight Seasons: Astor Piazzolla - Four Seasons of Buenos Aires; Vivaldi - Four Seasons

I found this album through this tiktok from Manuel Zogbi, who will give you more reasons than I can for why you should listen to this (ideal for working).

item.title: Learning from NFTs: The Culture of Future Promises
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: Disclaimer: Just as an update and explanation for why there was no newsletter for three weeks: My family and I caught COVID, after almost two years of not contracting it, luck wasn't on our side. Fortunately it just meant staying home following the doctor's instructions. We're still in that post-COV…
item.content:encoded:
What I've learned in recent weeks and need to share with you

Disclaimer: Just as an update and explanation for why there was no newsletter for three weeks: My family and I caught COVID, after almost two years of not contracting it, luck wasn't on our side. Fortunately it just meant staying home following the doctor's instructions. We're still in that post-COVID phase, where you're not sick but you're not at 100% either. Thanks to everyone who messaged me asking why there was no Iterative Thinking. I honestly didn't want to come back to this until I was calm enough to write properly.

Ed.16

November 24, 2021, in edition six of this newsletter, I touched on topics around the Metaverse and NFTs where I felt out of date, not current, but with the promise of at least catching up on these technologies and understanding them much more clearly.
