On the contrary, that helps adoption, so users who start out naturally become paying users. We could think that Adobe won't change and that Figma will end up being part of Creative Cloud, but I believe this could be a signal from Adobe to catalyze a paradigm shift. Niche applications are competing with them — ask those who use Canva (don't laugh, I'm serious) and those who edit video in apps from their phones that aren't Adobe's. They're not multi-purpose apps for professionals, but they focus on few things and do them well and simply for non-professional users.

Only time will tell what happens… What will Adobe XD users feel, those who like the application and see how Figma could end the current way they work?

The rebels will still have options

Penpot will surely start making noise — an open-source-based tool that inherits a lot from the path traveled by Sketch and Figma, cloud-based, but honestly, still in its infancy. That said, this is an interesting solution for freelancers or very small teams, which by definition don't need the level of security that an enterprise does. But I believe the alternatives will keep coming…

And if not, maybe it's time to turn back to an old love.

To read and watch 👀

How I use Figma to craft better presentations

I stopped using Google Slides, PowerPoint, and Keynote (which is my favorite) a long time ago to use Figma for presentations. Storytelling is a skill you must have to communicate your ideas effectively as a designer. This article gives you a guide to keep everything in order and take advantage of Figma's prototyping option in your presentations.

Graphic Means

Do yourselves a favor and watch this documentary — for those who like to understand where we come from, at least for those of us who make visual things in this industry.

item.title: On Success and Innovation
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: I've finished "Tim Cook's Apple" and I've had a moment of reflection about what success and innovation really mean.
item.content:encoded:
We relate innovation to success, but they're distinct concepts that can even contradict each other

Ed. 28

I've finished "Tim Cook's Apple" and I've had a moment of reflection about what success and innovation really mean.

I should say the book gives us a clear picture of how Apple has transformed since Steve Jobs' death and Tim's rise as CEO. An Apple with resounding sales successes, where the company reached a trillion dollar valuation (12 zeros) and everything points to the market valuation potentially reaching 3 trillion dollars today.

Apple with Tim Cook was able to defeat Jobs' ghost, in the sense of transcending its founder's death, with a commitment to the environment, internal support for minorities and education, and winning debates about user data privacy even against the FBI. An Apple that seeks, according to Cook himself, to do good from its position. But what has happened with innovation in its products?

Jobs always liked to emphasize that Apple had created disruption at specific moments in the industry, revolutionizing the personal computer with the original Mac in 1984, revolutionizing the music industry with the iPod in 2001, and revolutionizing the world of mobile telephony and its reach with the iPhone in 2007. For some the iPad was the next great product, but I like to think the iPad is an extension of the ecosystem created by the iPhone.

With Tim, the next device that captured a new segment was the Apple Watch. Although everything points to this wearable being the leader in the Smart Watch market and the money it represents is comparable to the luxury watch market, it hasn't had the disruptive transcendence of its predecessor devices.

An Apple that has evolved its devices with Face ID technology, and with cinematic results that give casual users the elements that DSLR cameras have. Some speculate that just as music players and cameras for casual users were eaten by mobile devices, semi-professional and professional cameras could face similar hardships in the coming years. Apple keeps evolving its products, but outside of Apple Watch it hasn't created a device capable of revolutionizing another industry.

And don't get me wrong, I think Apple's products keep evolving with each release. Apple is a successful company. But its innovation can be questioned, under the definition of creating radical disruption in the market—that's another thing, one that perhaps hasn't happened in a decisive way in Tim Cook's Apple.

Everything pointed to Apple trying to revolutionize television. Apple TV, TV+ have been responses that sought to match competitors' offerings, like Netflix and Disney, but they're only incremental improvements, no doubt, and they've given Apple profits just from the sale of services themselves.

Project Titan, which tried to revolutionize the automotive industry, hasn't fully materialized, and for now the most tangible thing has been iCar for third parties. Maybe all this is an exercise for the day Apple's autonomous vehicle sees the light. Here we'd have radical disruption again. Let's hope it happens one day.

I don't think Apple's next radical innovations will happen in the industries it currently handles. AV and VR are topics Apple has been researching and flirting with. Apple Glasses are still a myth, and it's not clear what the next innovations might lead to.

But whether this happens or not, Apple's success is beyond discussion. Financially it has broken records. But radical innovation is not necessarily a sign of it, even though the quality and evolution in its products are constant and even copyable by the competition.

Now to finish Build by Tony Fadell, another former Apple designer.

To Read 👀

Not all design opinions are created equal

When people with less experience come to you, your goal should be helping them understand better how and why they made those decisions, not how you would do it, based on your experience.

To Listen 🎧

Zofa - Nology

Technological nostalgia in Mexican minimal techno

item.title: The Tangible Nature of the Intangible
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: I must say that in a box under my desk, sealed tight to keep out the dust, there's a CD collection I built during my teenage years and college days. And also, on a hard drive, I have about 30 GBs of MP3s treasured as my music collection, all properly named and with their respective album art.
item.content:encoded:
When our obsession with hoarding shifts from objects to services and bytes.

Ed. 29

I must say that in a box under my desk, sealed tight to keep out the dust, there's a CD collection I built during my teenage years and college days. And also, on a hard drive, I have about 30 GBs of MP3s treasured as my music collection, all properly named and with their respective album art.

I don't have a large movie collection, but among what I have is the Heroes series, which by the way I first watched by renting from Blockbuster, and I liked it so much it ended up being a birthday gift from my now-wife; a live concert from the trio, at that time, Underworld, and a Kraftwerk concert, the Matrix trilogy including Animatrix, Lilo & Stitch, Wall-E, and Inception.

And this inventory comes up because I've realized how intangible these kinds of "objects" have become today. I already told you in edition 26 that I resisted switching to Sketch, well I must admit that a part of me didn't accept Spotify and later Apple Music when they launched in Mexico. It didn't make sense to me not to own the music, even if only as MP3 files. Curiously, the same thing didn't happen with Netflix… I'm complicated.

Now that I have accounts on several video services and Spotify, I've realized the immediacy of having the most complete catalog of music and video at your fingertips. What matters is no longer having it, now it's: watching it whenever you want and wherever you want. We've shifted the value from owning an object to using whatever we want immediately, in a utilitarian way, which can be very practical in many senses. This is, for me, the evolution of libraries, or a metaphor for them; because perhaps they're more similar to membership clubs, where by being a member, you can access depending on the case: audio, video, gaming and, of course, e-books, like in the libraries of old. What mattered about those places or platforms was the collection they housed, and that's exactly the service they're offering us, only the use and not obtaining that object.

To what extent is digital better? Or to what extent have we erased from our perception the difference between using a service and owning goods? What happens if one day one of those movies or series I enjoy watching so much disappears from the digital catalog and is never shown again? There may be an imminent risk that many works go unnoticed through the passage of history just because there are no available means for their reproduction and/or exhibition. Because today the algorithm of what's most popular and the licensing agreements are what makes a title stay in a catalog or not, and it changes without warning.

And even, much to my regret, an MP3 file isn't something tangible, even though it gives me that feeling of owning something. In a strict sense, it's encoded code that through a process is decoded so I can listen to it, bytes that live more in the electromagnetic spectrum than in atoms.

It may seem trivial, but in a world where most things are intangible; because they're software, they're code; like your money transfers, or they're digital files; like the photos you take to remember trips, your videos and your memories. The social interactions you have from your phone exist but they're not face to face, they're not tangible or physical, but are they real?

If we take this same idea to the world of digital art. What is an original in art that's based on digital files? Perhaps we should rethink the references that tell us when a work is a copy or is original, because in digital that doesn't make sense. Duplicating a file is closer to cloning than making a photocopy. There's no file zero, at least in a perceptible sense. Yes in a registry sense, but it's not something perceptible to the user of the digital object.

If we take it to more everyday things, your money is just a number shown in an app, but you don't need to have the money physically. In fact, the bank doesn't need it either, but it's still real money, valid for making transactions.

I'm not saying we should go back to physical things. Some things are better this way, no doubt, but others aren't. My reflection is about how perhaps we need to rethink the idea of owning and the idea of the original piece, in a place where digital hasn't established a real difference, and where even though we don't own anything, we can still evoke things, remember moments regardless of whether it's a CD, a paper photo, or bytes. The capacity that file gives me to connect moments, memories, and ideas is what makes those bytes tangible, what makes them feel so real and can awaken the desire to treasure them, whether it's a playlist, a list of favorite movies, or an NFT in a digital art collection.

In the end, we're beings equally intangible at the atomic level. There's only space surrounding us, but we're real. The meaning of what we do and the mark, good or bad, that we can leave on others is what makes us real, making tangible the intangible.

See you in the next edition

To check out 👀

Cubic.art

After the hype of the supposed burning of a Frida Kahlo artwork to mint NFTs that would only have a digital base, Daniel Gurrola shared the Cubic site with me, an interesting initiative where basically you can buy a portion of an artwork through a grid of NFTs. Imagine this for buying a portion of properties and receiving the respective proportion in money from the rent or sale of that place.

To listen to 🎧

Unspoken Words

From Max Cooper, if you have Apple Music, listen to it with Dolby Atmos, here's the Spotify playlist, here to buy the Blu-ray

item.title: Why Adobe needs to “Figmatize” and not the other way around
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: A couple of weeks ago, I watched the Briar Levit documentary titled “Graphic Means”, which talks about the history of graphic design, from linotypes to photo compositions, and finally, the Pasteup, the analog version of the current digital graphic design.
item.content:encoded:
# Why Adobe needs to “Figmatize” and not the other way around

## The evolution of user interface in digital editing tools.

Ed. 30

A couple of weeks ago, I watched the Briar Levit documentary titled “Graphic Means”, which talks about the history of graphic design, from linotypes to photo compositions, and finally, the Pasteup, the analog version of the current digital graphic design.

The image above helped me understand the metaphor used in the first digital editing apps. Brushes, markers and letter-set are examples of tools which are referenced in the toolbars from software like Adobe Photoshop or Illustrator.

A bit of history

First Illustrator, then Photoshop and Pagemaker (which finally became InDesign), these first apps used the Paste-up Desk as a metaphor in which pages and publications were designed before the computer age.

As time went on, computers were becoming a standard in the industry. The iterations and improvements in every single one of these Apps releases were more than amazing. For example, Photoshop 3.0 introduced the concept of Layers, which Gimp, Figma, Procreate, Affinity, and even Canva, have currently inherited.

Adobe began to see these apps as digital editing hubs with multiple purposes, which had overlaps between what you could do in Illustrator, Photoshop and InDesign.

It was clear that one was used for bitmaps, another one for vector editing, and another for layout publications, but the integration between apps allowed you, in many cases, to do similar things across all apps.

Photoshop has been used not only by photographers and digital illustrators, but even 3D artists who want to colorize their concepts in a dynamic way. Its multipurpose was such that it even had a timeline to edit layers like frames, and export them as GIFs and even video, and is still that way to this day.

In 2008, there was a boom in interface design, and after Apple changed the industry with the introduction of Apps and the iPhone, Web 2.0 started a more sophisticated web design, including skeuomorphism. At the same time, another web design movement started coming to life that responded much better to more standardized websites than websites made from scratch auteur design.

Flat Design, which started with Microsoft’s Metro and continued with Google and its Material Design, triggered a clean visual style, and by 2013, became a standard after iOS7 introduced its own version of flat design. Tools focused on the development and design of flat UI were needed, which didn’t require features for digital illustrators. Sketch App was launched in 2010, and by 2016, Figma released its public version. At the time, they were Adobe’s minor competitors, but they had been getting more traction since their approach centered on UI designers.

Sketch seemed like an interesting mix between Adobe Illustrator and Photoshop. It prioritized achieving the most effective way to export assets, which was a nightmare in Adobe Apps. Once Sketch removed all image editing features that you didn’t need, you were left with a tool which was centered on interface design.

If Photoshop contributed with the concept of Layers, Sketch brought Symbols back to life from the mythic Flash libraries, which Figma refined and aligned more to what developers actually used, called Components. As a result, Design Systems became part of the core of the development of digital products and services.

Adobe was late to realize that users needed something lighter and more scoped to their needs, so it was until 2015 that Adobe XD was introduced, as a response to the market loss that Sketch and Figma had presented. They made a simpler App, rather than the heavy, “all-in-one” Photoshop, which binged on your RAM.

### Niche Apps: the new paradigm

In complex Design Apps, we tend to have a more complex interface, for this reason, Adobe created Workspaces in their Apps. It was a way to customize the UI to more specific uses; for instance, workspaces for digital artists, for video producers or photographers, amongst others. An interface that is used for different audiences will require a higher level of customization.

On the other hand, when a Design App has a more narrow approach, it solves more precise problems and frees us from features we don’t need. Our Interface is simpler and our learning curve is minor.

Nowadays, we can see this last approach. Sketch, Figma, and even Framer share this same principle: Apps with specific uses. Nobody in their right mind would use these apps to edit pictures. We wouldn’t expect a digital illustrator to use them, even though they are vector based.

Procreate could be another example. In addition to focusing exclusively on tablets, it is a solution for professional digital illustrators, but at the same time, it is an App for amateur Digital illustrators.

Social Media post design was another niche taken by alternative Apps like ==Canva==, Desygner, Crello, Snappa, Stencyl or Visme; in which users need Apps to design posts for Social Media platforms or digital flyers, but rarely for print. Even Adobe introduced Spark as a mix between Photoshop and illustrator, with a simplified interface that uses templates like Canva.

This new paradigm has democratized the design and the entry-level, which created a more diverse segment where the users are not only professionals, don’t always need professional tools, and/or don’t want to pay for them==.

For this reason, I believe that the worst thing for Figma would be to become a more complex App. For instance, the last update which supports video split opinions, between some who celebrate the feature and others who don’t find a real use for that.

Advanced integration will respond to wider audiences, and will involve different niches. A robust and multipurpose app could target more people, but would mean less simplicity in the interface. Maybe in the future, we will see workspaces for Figma or perhaps Adobe is learning something and will start to pay attention to casual users, designing simpler products with more accessible prices.
Nos vemos en la siguiente edición.

Para leer 👀

Rediscovering Apple's 1987 Identity Guidelines

Una interesante revisión del la guía de diseño de marca de 1987.

Para escuchar y ver 🎧

Principal Designer o Design Lead

José Arroyo de EsteMancito.UX Podcast me invitó para hablar de mi experiencia como Principal Designer y contrastarlo con mi experiencia como UX Lead, Estreno su temporada en video conmigo, ¡Que honor!

item.title: Artificial Intelligence in the Creative Industries
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: For several months now, almost a year, the boom of Artificial Intelligences (from now on AIs) that can recreate images has been trending. Dall-E, Midjourney have made headlines with their results. Cosmopolitan became the first magazine to put one of these creations on its cover and even an AI-made a…
item.content:encoded:
We worry about losing our Status Quo as creators and creatives with Artificial Intelligence, but perhaps we should embrace change as a mindset and make that our Status Quo

Ed. 31

For several months now, almost a year, the boom of Artificial Intelligences (from now on AIs) that can recreate images has been trending. Dall-E, Midjourney have made headlines with their results. Cosmopolitan became the first magazine to put one of these creations on its cover and even an AI-made artwork has won a prize in a digital art competition; the author didn't lie, he said how he created the work, but it seems the jury either didn't understand what he meant or simply didn't care.

Let's be honest, we're only starting to see advances in tools that give us somewhat random images—yes, created from scratch—but it's far from these tools replacing creatives like photographers, 2D and 3D digital illustrators, at least in the immediate future. For me the underlying question is: In 10 years, where could these tools evolve in scope, and above all, what implications would they have?

It's curious how we can find in this discussion an attempt to safeguard the Status Quo of what the creative act implies, dismissing the new possibilities that these description-based AIs can offer us. I ask myself: How would we redefine the creative act the moment an AI can create exactly what we imagine?

Change as the constant in the Design industry and the mirage of democratization

What's interesting about these debates is that the design industry—and I can vouch for graphic design—has gone through several changes. Before computers entered publication editing, as I mentioned in the previous edition, there was a whole layer of roles and specialists dedicated to preparing files for printing. When computers became the standard, many of these roles expired, but new ones emerged. When the print world entered crisis because the internet became the platform where digital marketing campaigns now ran, people used mobile devices and applications to get services, e-commerce boomed. Roles emerged for these new tools and artifacts that users now utilized. I feel that change is something intrinsic to the act of designing.

Technological and paradigm shifts, resulting from new market needs, have historically pushed many designers to change their way of working, but above all, to change the tools they use to do it. And at its core, our activity as designers has always had changes. What doesn't change in a general sense is how we approach problems to understand and solve them. The way of executing design, which has more to do with technique, has varied according to context and era.

Mass access to mobile devices and computers in any of their forms, and the internet as a commodity, have brought great advantages for end consumers—shortening production processes, supply chains, and in many cases enabling the non-specialized user to use these tools without needing a specialist. The results will undoubtedly vary, because having access to tools is only part of the equation. Knowing how to write and having a pen enables you to express your ideas in written form, but it doesn't make you a writer. And no, I don't believe that training alone (in any of its forms) necessarily grants you that credit either, because you could have all the theory to be a writer and that doesn't guarantee you'll produce quality novels.

For me, this is achieved through the conjunction of three parts:

A) Theory (training)

B) Practice

C) The passage of time.

Only when we bring together these three parts will we stop being operators or theorists. When we practice over time (the 10,000 hours theory)—which while it may not make us experts—it will give us a quality superior to what we had at the beginning. That's where expertise reveals itself, and that can provide value in a way that tools alone only enable but don't guarantee.

So when we talk about democratization, we tend to think that with it we democratize mastery and expertise, when in any case we enable anyone to aspire to have it, and it will depend on each person to carry it out. What gets democratized are the tools and their access—again, only part of the equation.

That's why I believe the emergence of AIs will only motivate us to find ways to:

1) Use these tools as part of our workflow, as Antonio García Villarán aptly points out in his video testing AIs

2) Adapt and redefine the creative act, because precisely in this industry, we can have different solutions for the same problem, so AIs can't become a solution for everything.

Of course, every democratization brings with it a higher valuation for the specialized segment and its price grows. So in the days of digitalization, prints made "by hand," with finishes that have more to do with the art of artisanal printing than with handling InDesign, are sought after and their price is higher.

In an era like today, of Spotify, vinyl and the high-fidelity audio device industry is just a niche of specialists, hard-users, or simply people who can afford those luxuries.

Adapt or die

As the good @Ramza told us in episode 8 of our podcast The UX Rebels: the message is the same, what changes is the medium. No matter what changes in roles and tools the industry brings us, there will always be space for people who want to understand and solve problems.

That's why it's curious that designers, who are the ones always seeking change, and who in the case of user experience are the ones pushing to think differently and innovate, are the ones who least understand this equation.

We trust our tools so much that we forget the added value we bring to them and to the problem we're solving. Let's embrace change as the only constant in our career. That's where the resilience and adaptability we ask for so much is needed. The openness to what if should be our mantra and not just something that comes up in workshops or sales pitches.

I don't doubt that one day an AI will do exactly what we think, but by those days I trust that we'll know how to redefine our work, we'll find what to focus on, and change will bring with it other problems to solve. And there we designers will be, ready to face them.

To read 👀

Blending Modes

How do layer styles work? For those who want more certainty when choosing between Multiply and Darken

To listen 🎧

The History of Sound, on Convoy Networks

I've been getting a lot out of my Convoy Networks subscription. Their History of Sound series has a narrative that feels like it deserves a video with images on YouTube, or even on a streaming platform. If you can, try the service.

item.title: Creating tomorrow by executing today
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: Now that I'm in a calm work environment, it's easy to understand the importance of having moments of stillness. I tend to be pragmatic, looking for tangible and feasible solutions; however, I've come to understand again that it's also worthwhile to see everything from a more macro perspective, freei…
item.content:encoded:
As designers, we must find the balance between mapping the future and delivering short-term value

Ed. 32

Now that I'm in a calm work environment, it's easy to understand the importance of having moments of stillness. I tend to be pragmatic, looking for tangible and feasible solutions; however, I've come to understand again that it's also worthwhile to see everything from a more macro perspective, freeing yourself from the constraints—that is, thinking about solutions without the limitations of what you can do today.

It's easy to get lost in the maze of execution, I say this from personal experience. When the urgent always comes before the important and you have to win the race of putting out fires, or when we're stuck in an "agile" environment where design arrives late and there's no time for anything.

On the other hand, we can be at the other extreme, where we live drawing the future, without deadlines, priorities and clear objectives, where we lose our footing, and our high-level strategies are never reflected in tactical actions—that is, when we use foresight to avoid taking responsibility for the problems we need to solve today.

This is exactly something I've been reflecting on in recent months. In a graph of work rhythm, having peaks with stress and tasks to execute with due dates are compensated by valleys where you can do deep work, without being tied to dates, but where the objective is equally clear: understanding how and why you arrive at your design decisions through ideation.

This naturally makes it easier, or at least in a simpler way, to move from just executing to understanding the problems to solve in a more integral way, having a more general version of the projects you're involved in, and why not?, your solutions will be more complete, less limited and will invite you to push, change and optimize processes that don't work optimally today.

"Ideating the future is very easy, but executing it, and dealing with the rest of the organizations is much of what is not talked about"

Leonides delgado Epsidio 8 The UX rebels

The challenge is not understanding and mapping out how we should do things for them to work. The real challenge is understanding what we need to change in what we currently do to turn it into the future, but this process will never happen if you only live executing tactical problems, putting out fires and surviving daily tasks.

The problems can overwhelm you, and it will be easier to focus only on what you've been asked, and there's nothing wrong with delivering short-term value, understanding that today is just a decision that tomorrow will be iterated, because it's just one of several steps to reach the ideal state. There's nothing wrong with making concessions, delivering MVPs, if with that we can trace a clear bridge so as not to lose sight of the desired future.

Drawing the future is not real if we don't have a mindset to build bridges with small deliverables that take us to that future.

It sounds easy, I know, it's more complicated than writing all this in a newsletter. But the first step is to review if you're only taking requirements, if maybe you're stuck in the day-to-day execution and you need moments of stillness where you can think more creatively, have ideas outside the box and at least mentally imagine what the future of that solution looks like if everything were possible.

Our design process must help us push to change the things that don't work well. The future is built little by little, it's changing and never static.

See you in the next edition.

To Read 2x1 👀

Why Everything Looks the Same

A complete analysis of how economic globalization, generational transition and technology converge to flatten the consumer experience

Why Dall-E will not steal my job as an illustrator

Emmanuel is a digital illustrator who describes the experience of trying the AI image creation service: Dall-E, an interesting and forceful statement about how we're still far from being replaced by robots.

item.title: "Take Power and Give It to the People": Has the Promise of the Internet Been Fulfilled?
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: After watching The Playlist, the story of how Spotify was founded on Netflix, I've been reflecting on what these paradigm shifts and the digitization of services have meant. As I mentioned in edition 28: What matters is no longer owning it, now it's: watching it whenever and wherever you want.
item.content:encoded:
The Playlist: the Spotify story, advertising on Netflix, and Adam Mosseri and the creator economy.

Ed. 33

After watching The Playlist, the story of how Spotify was founded on Netflix, I've been reflecting on what these paradigm shifts and the digitization of services have meant. As I mentioned in edition 28: What matters is no longer owning it, now it's: watching it whenever and wherever you want.

The promise was that the Internet would grant us freedom, information for the masses that would empower people, because by democratizing access to information, at least you give them the option to search for it.

Going back to the final episode of The Playlist series, where in the near future (in the year 2025), Spotify would be accused of being a monopoly, because the record labels have interests in the platform since they're basically Spotify shareholders. Is the platform not really a platform but rather a fairly closed system where the least benefited are once again the artists and creators?

The reality is complex and full of nuances…

In many cases this is unfinished business that technological democratization hasn't achieved. Cases like Aaron Swartz or Alexandra Elbakyan, creator of Sci-Hub, are proof of how the system continues to be a bottleneck. Because while entertainment enjoys democratization as a commodity, specialized knowledge is only for those willing to pay for it, even when the publishing houses behind the charges invest nothing but take the profits at the expense of the paper creators.

The promise of a world with free and open access… was never real

The post-pandemic era full of inflation has put this to the test, and the tech companies that have seen their value decline, Netflix with ads and a cheaper price, show us another idea that the future painted as inevitable: Ad-free content on Demand in an omnichannel way.

Advertising, we now know, is necessary in every content platform with ambitions of scalability.

YouTube was always right: Monetizing creators means having ads, even if they're intrusive and even if people hate them and try to skip them as soon as possible.

The media agencies that coordinated big budgets for brands across different channels were devoured by Google and Facebook, who now monopolize much of the profits from Internet advertising. We became the product, and advertising and ads evolved, but they never disappeared.

This leads me to rethink what Adam Mosseri proposed in his Ted Talk: A Creator-Led Internet, Built on Blockchain, to turn platforms into just that, into true platforms where creators own their audiences. He says the change is happening, but it's not a straight line. Although the Internet is there to keep pushing, and grant more power to those who don't have it, the interoperability of platforms that are going to lose power, and according to his words; Web3 and Tokens would be the path, a path that today remains uncertain, but it seems we're seeing some ideas that have been taking shape for a while from Jack Dorsey and Blue Sky in 2022

The user has the possibility of being the shaper of the Web, or at least this is one of the possibilities that this paradigm shift offers us. A change in structure can offer us positive possibilities, but there are many challenges that must first be overcome. The decisions that the community can make shouldn't always be assumed as a response toward "improvement"; in many cases it will be a social response to where the majority points or where that majority can be influenced. Ed 21. Decentralization vs Centralization

But as I also mentioned in that same edition, decentralization is not synonymous with redistribution of power, so I believe the strategy should be not falling for techno-solutionism, thinking that technology will naturally make us better. We are the ones who shape technology and we are the ones who must be conscious of the decisions we make, and although difficult, we must aspire to anticipate scenarios that are real today, but still seem like a Black Mirror episode to us out of ignorance.

See you in the next edition.

To Read 👀

The Complete Guide to Figma Branching

Speaking more about design, this article is very complete if what you're looking for is to understand and apply branching to your Figma files.

To Listen 🎧

good kid, m.A.A.d city by Kendrick Lamar

item.title: The goal of UI is not to be pretty and pleasant
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: I've always believed that graphic designers, visual artists, and those who work in creative industries have a tendency to seek the spotlight, even indirectly. Maybe not all of us like being present when people talk about our work, but we like people talking about our work.
item.content:encoded:
UI Design is much more than just "pimping out", dressing up, or adding color to screens.

Ed. 34

I've always believed that graphic designers, visual artists, and those who work in creative industries have a tendency to seek the spotlight, even indirectly. Maybe not all of us like being present when people talk about our work, but we like people talking about our work.

It's inevitable to project something of yourself when you're in the process of creation. What you create is by definition a reflection of you, and at different levels you can feel more or less attached to what you created. The illusion emerges that if it gets modified, you get modified; that's why you defend it tooth and nail, because it feels like an extension of yourself.

One of the conclusions I reached in collaborative environments like experience design is that it's not about you, your creation is not the end in itself, your creation is only a means, a channel that will enable greater objectives to be achieved.

We don't design a login screen with easy readability, with recognizable patterns, because it's beautiful, even though it may be—we design it that way because that gives users an intuitive and frictionless experience.

In the design process of a digital product, UI (User Interface) Design makes tangible in visual form what we want to achieve as a business, incentivizing or changing user behavior (this last part is what Joshua Seiden calls Outcomes). UI that doesn't serve this is empty and purposeless UI.

If we add the context layer to this, we'll understand why we can have flows or screens that technically use the same design pattern but are visually different. It's not that one is more aesthetic than the other, and therefore more visually striking or disruptive or innovative—it's that maybe they speak to different audiences, where the visual culture is different.

That's why I believe we need to eradicate the idea that UI Design is about making things "pretty" and "pleasant." UI Design is about using different resources—use of space, color, typography, contrasts, accessibility, and consistency—to achieve business objectives and concrete behaviors in users.

Things don't work because they're beautiful, they're beautiful because they work

The aesthetic part will depend more on whether it's correctly inserted into the visual context, whether it responds to the attributes that have been given to the brand, service, or product.

We need to tear down a certain ego that visual designers have, because it's not about us, nor about what we create. We serve a greater good, a more cross-cutting objective. UI Design doesn't begin or end with designers.

A good experience is not eye-catching buttons, pixel perfect, with shadows and effects that make us feel like elements pop out of the screen. A good experience happens when a user can accomplish a task intuitively, effortlessly, and quickly; that's when a memorable experience emerges. UI is only part of it, because it's centered on people, not on UI.

So when you design UI, try to understand the why of what you do, the macro purpose you're all trying to achieve together. Having that foundation will make you more aware that your design is not about you—it's not about it being beautiful or pleasant, it's about it working and being effective according to the objectives your digital product pursues.

See you in the next edition.

To Read 👀

Good Design

A small but concise reflection on how we chase trends instead of seeking timeless design.

How To Build Expertise While Learning

You can turn on subtitles in English or Spanish. How to become an expert while learning: by sharing what you know.

item.title: Resilience — That's What Design Is About
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: 2022 has brought us surprises. The year is ending very differently from how it started. Uncertainty has become part of the landscape.
item.content:encoded:
The work stays the same, only the medium changes

Thanks to the more than 100 subscribers of Iterative Thinking. If you're one of those who subscribed in the last two weeks and/or this is the first edition you receive: Welcome and thanks for reading me.
Ed. 35

2022 has brought us surprises. The year is ending very differently from how it started. Uncertainty has become part of the landscape.

Reflection on the industry is inevitable. The end of the year naturally makes us think in retrospective. It's not easy to draw an optimistic and bright scenario. The only certainty is that nothing is certain: but that's what Design has always been about.

Mutation is a constant in Design.

Fabrixio Texeira, as every year, has published the expected trends for 2023, and was very accurate in reflecting on a situation happening globally. The economy is suffering a contraction, the tech industry is suffering cutbacks, and because of this, many design roles have been cut around the world.

Our industry is a reflection of what's happening in the world, where not only designers have lost jobs, developers have too, but also people in sales, logistics, and many other areas. The cuts respond more to a general restructuring of the tech industry than to a Design crisis — though it does get worse, yes, because of thousands of people around the world who have been thrown into the UX industry with the promise of a better future, as the recipe for career success.

Some might think that the role of the designer (in the full sense of the word) is coming to an end, when we see the proliferation of AI in multiple creative fields, when we can even make content iterations automatically with AI. Some point out that the designer's job will be more of a curator than a creator.

Design has never been static, it has always mutated like society itself. We change formats, mediums, but that's only "the how." We've talked about this throughout the last year in this newsletter, about what design would look like in 10 thousand years, and how there will always be problems to solve. Our activity will adapt according to the needs, but we will always keep solving problems.

Looking inward.

Adrian Solca, as always, has been forceful in his message; we need better professionals who positively impact organizations.

We need to understand that there are no specialists without a good generalist foundation.

This doesn't happen by magic…

"…there are no shortcuts to being an expert in a field. It requires hundreds of hours of practice, analysis, and reflection, but none of that can happen if we don't question what we know, what we do, and what we aspire to be." — The Collapse, by Adrian Solca

If you're reading this and/or you're looking for your first opportunity, you're overwhelmed and don't know where to start, I can only tell you; magic recipes don't exist, they lied to you.

The craft is forged with resilience and persistence and reflection. The coming months won't be prosperous so it's not going to get better.

If even so, you're convinced that this is your thing and where you want to build a career, welcome, patience and critical thinking will be your allies, this is not a 100-meter sprint, this is a marathon.

For those who've been at this for a while and at least know what it's about…

I'm somewhat tired of the UX is not UI speeches, which I've tried to describe why they don't do the industry any good, or arguing about What is really a Product Designer or a UX Designer? and Why doesn't the industry reach a consensus?

The industry is not going to resolve these questions, it doesn't matter what title they give us, if with it we understand the value of what we do, and with it we impact organizations and consequently people's lives. The value of design is not in the title, but in its impact.

We must reflect individually on the value of what we do, only by doing this will we understand where we can go and how we'll adapt to the challenges and paradigm shifts coming in the next years. And Design is not going to disappear, we just need to understand where it's moving to — the faster we can understand it, the easier it will be to adapt.

We never stop learning and we'll never know everything, let's assume our reality as just a fraction of what we think it is. Review what pending subjects you have and work to strengthen them, don't wait for opportunities to push you to learn other things, on the contrary, your hunger for curiosity should push you to keep searching and that will inevitably lead you to better opportunities.

If you've been part of the cuts this 2022, I can only say I'm very sorry, I hope you can find work soon, if you think I can help you with something send me a message on LinkedIn and let's see what we can do.

See you in the next edition, before 2022 ends.

To Read 👀

Design system canvas

Pavaan is a designer from London who adapted the Lean Canvas to Design Systems. Although the artifact is in testing phase, you can check it out and if applicable give him feedback on the use of the tool.

To Listen 👀

Underworld - Pearl's Girl

item.title: Before 2023 Begins
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: If I'm honest, I didn't imagine myself writing about my newsletter a year later, and here we are.
item.content:encoded:
The last edition of 2022

Ed. 36

If I'm honest, I didn't imagine myself writing about my newsletter a year later, and here we are.

The goal set last October of reaching 52 editions wasn't met; however, I gained the confidence to share my ideas in written form. That lack of confidence held me back for a long time, but I think we've tamed that demon.

Through this newsletter I made reflections (not always of the same quality and depth, it must be said) on various topics, directly and indirectly related to design, but without a doubt the triggers I had for each edition were enriched by what I read, watch, and listen to.

In 2022 I set out to understand more about Web3, Metaverse, and maybe AI. The first one I think was mission accomplished. The interesting part of the exercise was gaining an understanding that would let me explain certain aspects to you that at first glance can be confusing and overwhelming.

And although the crypto-winter (many call it an ice age) is going to last a while, plus the collapse of several massive projects this year hasn't helped confidence in protocols vs platforms.

Still, I believe it's a topic that's here to stay, though we'll probably move forward more slowly and with lead feet.

Web3 still has pending assignments with users, usability, and accessibility; that's where UX can help with everything learned in centralized environments. Not to mention that we won't see mass adoption while the incentive is being an early-adopter to buy cheap and sell high.

Tokenized communities must find real value by solving real problems, and their governance models will need behavior experts, both behavioral design and behavioral economics.

We have pending homework to understand the design possibilities of the Metaverse, not the one uncle Mark talks about, but that interoperable Metaverse that matches the protocols proposed by IDZ and BlueSky, there are two books pending on the list.

About AI, I'm concerned about the ethical and copyright implications, as well as the development of a new generation of fake news, which in a world as bipolar as today's, is an excellent breeding ground to set the world on fire.

AIs are not free from the bias of the tribe that creates them, and assuming these black boxes are reliable puts the youngest in trouble, eager with curiosity, speed of obtaining information will be prioritized over the veracity of what that information contains.

On the other hand, when you ask an AI in a chat about a topic it's easy to get small doses of many topics without going deep, but that already happens with Tiktok and Instagram. Hyper-focused and concise knowledge only lets us dig a little on the surface, but we need to switch to other formats if we want to go to another level of depth and expertise.

That's why I want to create more in-depth content in 2023.

Adrian Solca's exercise in the WhatsApp and Discord UXenSerio group (if you don't know what I'm talking about please do yourself a favor and visit the links), as well as several mentorship sessions I gave on ADPlist have shown me how many of the new members and newcomers have lots of questions about Design Systems, and that's where there might be a lot of information, but little of it in Spanish. Maybe we'll see more serialized content on Medium about this topic as a contribution, I won't deny it, this newsletter has been the seed that gave me the confidence to share what I know and learn along the way.

Thanks to everyone who read me in 2022, see you in 2023

item.title: Faster Ideation Processes with AI
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: Microsoft and OpenAI's latest moves have put pressure on Google, and it seems things haven't gone well at all with Bard.
item.content:encoded:
Because ideation doesn't just happen in design, but in any field.

Ed. 37

Microsoft and OpenAI's latest moves have put pressure on Google, and it seems things haven't gone well at all with Bard.

Creative industries have been the first to see shifts in the production paradigm. Image creation, ranging from photography, illustration, and 3D; audiovisual production like video and music. Communication in messages—we can basically make queries in written form.

Many point to the disappearance of professions and the cheapening of production costs. The hype makes us think the change is in what's new, even though we don't quite understand how it works and especially all its implications.

Don Norman has already entered the conversation, saying that AIs are much more of a collaboration

And this idea is exactly what I find interesting. While the first take is that AI will replace us, today these solutions are an alternative to be more productive or, in design terms, to do ideations that are equally or more effective in a shorter time.

Time is the Achilles' heel of every design process. While time is a constraint that pushes us to make decisions—understanding that these are the best we could make, they're not perfect and are always improvable—it's also true that many of us, looking back on the path traveled in a project, have thought that with more time we would achieve more.

AI could be an interesting tool for idea iteration: because we could draw paths of different hypothetical scenarios in less time.

A first application could be making draft visualizations in Dalle·2 or Midjourney for a magazine cover in minutes, and I'm talking about using these as drafts for the concept to illustrate. Reading a summary of two contracts understanding the differences of each one—that summary was made in minutes by an AI.

It won't be long before we have some software that can create UI ideas for us, but let's not fool ourselves, this only solves part of the problem—visual ideation, a more sophisticated ideation that isn't made by humans.

But trying to get out of the design bubble, and visual design to be more precise, there are other fields that could be improved (if we look at it optimistically) by the use of AI. We tend to think of ideation when we talk about something visual like creating an image, designing an interface, though our own thinking (something intangible) can be iterated, the process of how we do things can be iterated. If I can ask it to make an image and iterate on it until I get the desired result, why couldn't I in the near future run simulation models of a plan, understanding the constraints and context?

I'm not just talking about iterating PowerPoint slides, I'm talking about running simulations with team design, I'm talking about how this could impact disciplines like Future Design, Behavioral Design, or Strategic Design. I'm not saying AI will change behaviors—I'm saying AI could run behavior models to prospect more successful scenarios.

Getting to solutions faster, because AI would do the work of thinking, discarding, and rethinking for us. Having scenarios, concepts, or routes to test in less time.

Yes, for now, we've seen concrete things like creating images, music, voice, and video, but as Taramona says, what happens if scientists have discussions and dissertations with an AI? One that learns, analyzes, and develops patterns at a speed we can't. What if AI is used to understand protein folding, or run models to validate string theory?

Without a doubt, this naturally shifts our skills to new areas, from the use of these new tools with their new processes (like the famous prompts), to understanding these new outputs and their limitations for decision-making.

See you in the next edition… but first please help me with this poll.

To Read 👀

AI is a feature, not a product

It's not that AI devours products by replacing them, but that AI will be a feature that makes more sense to add to certain established products, expanding their capabilities.

To Listen 🎧

Dreamscape - one heart

item.title: Asking for feedback in design
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: A few days ago I had this reflection after exchanging ideas on WhatsApp in a group where UI topics are discussed (which Adrián Solca opened as part of the UX en Serio community).
item.content:encoded:
Beyond just asking open-ended questions: Which option is better?

ed. 38

A few days ago I had this reflection after exchanging ideas on WhatsApp in a group where UI topics are discussed (which Adrián Solca opened as part of the UX en Serio community).

Some find in this space a good forum to discuss topics about Design Systems, or Handoff, and in some cases many seek guides on best practices, like accessibility or the correct use of UI patterns. More than once I've come across questions like "What do you think of this proposal?" Or "Which of these two options is the best?" Followed by an image with the options to analyze.

Adrián Solca himself has responded that this isn't the way to evaluate UI, inviting us to basically test, don't assume UI works, prove that UI works.

And although I agree with the argument, I know that those who ask these questions deep down want, or think, that there's a concrete answer and they want to align their proposal to mental models that, consequently, lead to standardized UI patterns. Basically, they want to know concretely which is the best solution.

Feedback as an exercise

The exercise of asking for feedback can nourish you as a designer; and not because of the answer you receive, which helps you greatly to learn from the more experienced and expand your knowledge; but rather, because asking for feedback is a reflective exercise that can help you gain clarity on how you think and what you think.

When we do ideation in design collaboratively, it's natural that we argue with our peers why certain decisions are better than others. Arbitrariness only leads to capricious paths that will be hard to defend against objective arguments later on.

We can even have discussions and debates, but if the process is going well, we'll know why we arrived at these ideas, which paths have been discarded and which ones we've kept.

But it's curious that when we ideate alone we don't follow this parallel. We tend to simply ask openly: Which option is better?

The problem is that design cannot be evaluated without context. In design, the best solutions don't win—those that are most feasible to build win (and perhaps that makes them the best solutions) but that feasibility will depend on context.

When we throw out such an open question we're eliminating the context that led us to make certain decisions. We're leaving it in our counterpart's hands to decipher how we got there and why that's the best solution. More experienced designers will ask questions and want to know more before saying anything. Less experienced ones will just assume what little they know and risk giving their opinion.

Going back to the example of collaborative ideation, when we design alone these kinds of discussions and debates must be had by the designer with themselves. Why is this version better than the previous one? Why do I prefer this version over the others?

If you can't verbalize how you arrived at a solution, it's because you don't have clarity on the process that led you there.

If we can identify what we like, can we separate ourselves from it and become more objective? If I can understand the patterns I use to think, can I avoid using them to think outside the box? Questioning ourselves to understand ourselves grants clarity, it will let us see the pros and cons of each path. It won't necessarily give us definitive answers, but it will bring us closer to arguments to be able to decide.

This will make a big difference between asking opinions about two proposals versus giving context about the objective we're pursuing, narrating concisely (this isn't about rambling) what our process has been to arrive at these two options and why one seems better to us; even if we don't know which one to choose, at least we should understand the pros and cons of each.

This will give your counterpart a clear context of how you arrived at these options. You'll receive arguments and comments that understand and take into account the context, and from there, they'll be able to help you find the most suitable solution.

But on the other hand, you'll be more aware of your thinking process, owning the concepts, not just following them. You'll be more eloquent when sharing and socializing your process that justifies your solutions.

Because let's be honest, the challenge isn't asking other designers for feedback. The real challenge is arguing our decisions to people on our team or organization who aren't designers, and this is only achieved by being clear about the objective, the context, and our design process that led us to this solution.

See you next week (I promise).

To Read 👀

The Truth About Strategy

Most people enormously overcomplicate the concept of "strategy." As such, your strategy is what informs and connects what the company is trying to do and how it's trying to do it, with the people responsible for delivering it.

To Listen 🎧

Keep Going - Swørm

item.title: Ed. 39
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: Things don't work because they're beautiful, they're beautiful because they work
item.content:encoded:
9# Evaluating Design.

We evaluate design from our taste and not from its function.

ed. 38

Things don't work because they're beautiful, they're beautiful because they work

This is the maxim I've tried to repeat every time we want to discuss the value of design.

I'm not saying the aesthetic part of design doesn't exist, I'm saying aesthetics aren't arbitrary and serve a function, delivering a message in a specific encoding for a certain audience; it's not coincidence, it's intention.

You can appreciate design, you can like it or not, but that's different from whether it fulfills its function.

As designers with a visual background, we tend to evaluate design based on our tastes, which by the way depends on our visual culture.

We tend to think the world needs to be improved, making it more "aesthetic." We think that if things aren't like a Zen garden and are full of noise, they're not good design. We completely forget that first we must think about what its function is.

Some hate Excel because they find it an ugly product, but they're just casual-users, hard-users don't need a minimalist interface, they need as many columns and rows as possible to fit on screen. Otherwise why isn't Mac's Numbers the king of spreadsheets.

We forget it's not about how it looks, but about how it works, about whether it works…

When we evaluate design, we must do it from a more objective and less personal position, I would point to three elements

  • What it wants to communicate (message). What was fundamentally meant to be said, what was the task the user must complete, what is the objective of what we do
  • The language to communicate it (context). Who is your audience, your user, what are the communication codes you share that will allow you to connect and be heard
  • Limitations. It's not the same to have a one-year deadline and 10 million dollars, as 2 sprints and little budget, design is what happens between what you want to do and what you can do.

And of course you may or may not like the result, but that's different from evaluating. If it doesn't fulfill its purpose it will be failed design.

Not everything must be minimalist, not everything needs to be ordered or eye-catching, but neither should we limit ourselves to appealing only to functionality; but it will depend on the purpose of what we design and for whom we design.

For examples we just need to take a look at Andy.Works, eye-catching applications that seek to break with traditional models of how an App is designed.

Curious that in their distribution strategy they're only available on iPhone and iPad.

Having clarity on the purpose and function of what we design helps us be decisive with what we do, understanding that not everything is solved the same way, there are no recipes.

We must try to be objective when we evaluate design, and separate very well when our personal tastes interfere. Especially in product design, where the solution almost always responds to solving a problem, and regularly a real problem.

But what do you think? I'd love to know if you agree or not, how do you evaluate your designs?

See you in the next edition.

To Read 👀

The Cost of Craft

This week I read this essay that seemed very good to me, it gathers many points about the problems of scaling teams; above all, high-performance teams that when growing can lose that touch when changing and establishing dynamics in larger teams. The challenge is in maintaining balance and observing what needs to be adjusted.

item.title: Prompt Designer: Confusing a Possible Skill with a Nonexistent Role.
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: In this quest to understand the Next Big Thing, we want to do some "foresight" on how AI (Artificial Intelligence) will impact our work as designers. Overwhelmed by the uncertainty of not knowing how much and how what we do can be automated: we future-cast.
item.content:encoded:
We're trying to make sense of a world that's changing faster than we'd like and faster than we understand.

Ed. 40

In this quest to understand the Next Big Thing, we want to do some "foresight" on how AI (Artificial Intelligence) will impact our work as designers. Overwhelmed by the uncertainty of not knowing how much and how what we do can be automated: we future-cast.

AI caught us by surprise, using a mental model that's heavily used today—messages on our mobile devices, the chat. It's a digital analogy to conversation, which, by the way, is itself the basic way we share knowledge and learn.

The AI services available right now aren't perfect. The expectation for improvement is especially high for services dedicated to creating multimedia like images and video, since there's a gap between the prompt the AI uses as reference and the result obtained.

But what is a prompt? According to Wikipedia's definition as applied to natural language:

Instructions issued to a computer system (such as a text-to-image artificial intelligence) in the form of written or spoken language.

Language itself is a complex system, but AI is a system that learns exponentially. For now, we learn and struggle to get multimedia that's as close as possible to our prompt.

Content creators and influencers on social media, whose target audience is designers, have been showcasing the advantages of integrating these tools into our workflow—which isn't bad as a way to experiment with new tools, but which, at least for now, are insufficient to solve real problems. Coca Cola or Vanity Fear aren't using AI for their brand because of performance and cost—they're doing it for the hype.

Asset sellers have capitalized on this gap between what the main AI services use and those who want to use them. Smoothing the learning curve, they offer Prompt Packs to get defined results without wasting time.

This has led some articles to already talk about new professions to cover this gap.

Prompt: A Skill That Won't Be What It Is Today

We're trying to create a role for a hyper-specialized skill that responds to a digital divide that should shrink over time. AI grows exponentially—what we imagined would take years now takes a couple of months. AI will understand our writing better; it's the one learning to decipher the prompts we make, not the other way around.

Imagine an Emailing Messages Designer in the early 90's, but back then designers were graphic designers, the people doing HCI were psychologists and anthropologists; engineers were fully immersed in HTML and we called them web masters.

People didn't become Emailing Messages Designers—people learned to use email and wrote messages.

People aren't going to become prompt designers—people will learn to interact with AI through prompts, and AI will tend to understand our descriptions better as time goes on.

Mass adoption of these tools requires the learning curve to keep getting smaller, because we tend to use a product more when the experience is intuitive and natural. The prompt designer is a barrier between the service and the end user.

And assuming a specific case where we'd need to learn writing rules to, for example, design UIs with AI (the sophistication and specialization of a tool is very viable), this doesn't mean creating a role—it means adopting new technical skills that will make us more productive and help us solve problems.

Nobody calls themselves a Keynote Designer, Figma Designer, or Framer Designer. Let's not let our tools define our work, because tools are just tools.

…

But then again, I don't control the industry, and this is just a reflection. We still see UX/UI designers posted in job listings and we're still fighting because UI is not UX in 2023. Nothing would surprise me.

See you in the next edition.

To Read 👀

Augmented Imagination

"AI will not mean the death of artists, intellectuals, or anyone else. It will mean our rebirth […] Here's how: we must stop thinking of AI as artificial intelligence and instead think of it as augmented imagination."

item.title: Design Education: Schools, Bootcamps, and Practice.
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: For those who don't know, I studied Design and Visual Communication at what is now the Faculty of Arts and Design (UNAM) in Mexico. But the truth is that much of what I do today wasn't taught to me in my degree program. Back then, people said "Multimedia" when referring to web, people used CDs and D…
item.content:encoded:
There's no defined formula for becoming a designer, but a curious and critical attitude is a present pattern.

Ed. 41

For those who don't know, I studied Design and Visual Communication at what is now the Faculty of Arts and Design (UNAM) in Mexico. But the truth is that much of what I do today wasn't taught to me in my degree program. Back then, people said "Multimedia" when referring to web, people used CDs and DVDs to store information, listen to music, watch movies, and distribute applications. I drew, I made illustrations.

But today I want to reflect on universities, bootcamps, and practice.

Universities and design schools, that elephant taking slow steps

Studying design at a university or specialized design school is one way to acquire the skills and knowledge needed to work in the design industry. However, formal education alone is not enough to be a good designer.

Designers must be willing to stay updated, and I'm not just talking about the latest trends and/or technologies; I'm talking about understanding and assimilating the design process, which can vary by industry, but in any of these you must learn it, understand it, and assimilate it.

It's important that designers have a continuous learning attitude.

Design programs at a university or specialized school can provide more comprehensive training, this would help us have a solid foundation in design principles, theory, and critique.

In my training as a visual communicator, I spent 4 years learning about visual composition, typography, digital and analog representation techniques, photography, and I even took geometry and semiotics, not to mention 8 semesters of drawing; we also dove into reflecting on and analyzing some design methodologies.

But until now, unfortunately, at least in Mexico and Latin America there are no universities that offer a User Experience Design degree (if that's what the program should be called). Perhaps the closest things are some Industrial Design courses on ergonomics, Product Design and Service Design, or in Computer Engineering with courses focused on HCI.

Imagine what would happen if we studied UX for 4 years…

Bootcamps: instant ramen for those looking for the real thing

The demand for profiles in the industry has made UX bootcamps trendy, and for many it's even the door to making a career change.

While bootcamps can be a good option for those looking for quick and specific training, they are not a complete alternative to formal education.

Bootcamps aim to provide contextual teaching with real practices, intensively and in short periods of time (between 3 and six weeks).

The reality is that the UX bootcamp offering is about creating generalists in just months, which is impossible. There will be those who after doing a bootcamp got an opportunity and are crushing it, but they're not the rule.

If what you're looking for is developing specific knowledge, like using Figma, understanding what a Usability Test is and how it's applied, how to use the Value Proposition Canvas, etc. Then yes, a bootcamp-style format could be interesting.

Unfortunately, it's easier and more profitable (for those selling the bootcamp) to sell the idea of a generalist, a know-it-all, who will easily integrate into the tech industry where supposedly you earn very well. Though this is more of a sales pitch with few facts to back it up.

The reality about design education

You can be a university graduate, having studied 4 years of design fundamentals, but that doesn't make us designers. You can have a degree backed by an educational institution that effectively says you studied design and passed, but only that.

You can be a graduate of the most expensive bootcamp, where knowledge is specialized and limited to the current context, and no, that doesn't make you a designer either…

Do I recommend studying a Design degree? Of course, if you have the opportunity and see something interesting in the curriculum, go for it. The university experience is amazing in every sense, it goes beyond getting a design diploma.

Do I recommend taking a bootcamp? Well, I think if you're already on track, you're clear on what you like, you've researched on your own, you're not a newbie; a bootcamp could help you, but only as an introduction door, or to polish specific topics (though I insist the offering isn't designed for bootcamps to develop specialized skills).

But what I do recommend is that whatever you do, develop a habit of learning, of being curious and questioning yourself, why you think what you think and how you think. Question the value your profession has, question the objective you're pursuing.

Regardless of your background, this attitude of constant learning and critical thinking is a pattern I do see in designers, in those who practice this profession day by day.

Some of the best designers I've known didn't even study design at a university. Perhaps they started as Engineers, or come from areas that have little to do with the field they work in, but all of them have always sought more, want to learn new things, have questioned their role and are clear on their next objective; this makes them work with discipline and clarity.

Connecting the dots: education and practice.

Practical experience is fundamental for any designer. Through practical experience, designers can learn to apply the knowledge and skills acquired in the classroom and in their studies to solve real-world problems.

I had already mentioned it in edition 14, the designer is formed through practice. The education you receive, whether at a university, course, bootcamp, certificate program, or researching on your own, is only one part, because we don't want to theorize about design, we want to plan and execute design.

This is only achieved with practice, so if you're a recent graduate of whatever, practice, put the knowledge you have into practice, and keep learning.

See you in the next edition.

To Read 👀

A Short History of Artificial Intelligence

"With recent advances in machine learning, we might be entering a period of technological progression more impactful than the Scientific Revolution and the Industrial Revolution combined."

To Listen 🎧

item.title: What Tony Fadell's book taught me about product design
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: I devoured Build, Tony's book (also available in Spanish). He has this gift for telling stories, for sharing how his life has been while simultaneously guiding you and teaching you about product design and the teams that make those things possible.
item.content:encoded:
Clarity on what you want to solve, narrative, and care for the whole.

Ed. 42
Note: In this article I use "clients" and "users" interchangeably to refer to those who use our product

I devoured Build, Tony's book (also available in Spanish). He has this gift for telling stories, for sharing how his life has been while simultaneously guiding you and teaching you about product design and the teams that make those things possible.

For those who don't know, Tony Fadell is known for having worked at Apple designing the iPod, although of course, as he himself says, great products are never the work of a single person—they're the result of collaboration and teamwork. Nest, which was sold to Google for 3.2 billion dollars.

At the end of the Kindle book I had 16 pages of notes… 119 highlighted segments. There's so much to reread and keep mentally pinned. For now I want to focus on some things about the product design process.

When you start with an idea

"If you're not solving a real problem, you can't start a revolution"

Tony is relentless and pragmatic. For him, the product is born from understanding a real need and obsessing over solving it. If you're not solving a real problem, your product won't transcend.

He's not saying your project needs to cure cancer. He's saying it should come from a need or problem that users are dealing with in their day to day. Fixing that should be your obsession—letting them do it more efficiently.

"Don't tell me what's special about this object. Tell me what will change for the customer"

When we're developing a product we tend to think about the solution. Our narrative is based on describing the technology, and without meaning to, we assume that differentiation is the story we need to tell our customers.

And the truth is it barely matters how we developed our product if we can't be clear about the benefit for the customer. People don't buy drills. People don't want to make holes in the wall easily. People want to hang pictures on their wall. Everything else is just a means to achieve that.

That's where Tony invites us to research our users/clients and make sure we know them—know them in a demographic sense, but also in a qualitative sense. They're people, with problems. If you stop seeing them as statistics, it'll be easier to have empathy and solve problems more effectively.

From Zero to One

And while he invites us to have data to validate and back up our decisions, he also makes clear that sometimes it's about a leap of faith—especially when your product is a new version, when you don't have precise previous experiences that guarantee results.

"If a product is really new, there's nothing to compare it to, nothing to optimize, and nothing to test. In these moments it's your responsibility as a manager or leader to explain that the team is not a democracy, that it's a decision based on opinion and that you're not going to reach the right decision by consensus. But it's not a dictatorship either. You can't give orders without explaining yourself."

But don't misunderstand. It's about not having paralysis from lack of data or overexposure to data. Not making decisions just because the data doesn't guarantee something feasibly. Data shows what's wrong, but it doesn't always tell us how to fix it.

Tony tends to have this ambivalence and this holistic view—telling you that you have to see the complete horizon without leaving aside the details. It's not about having idealized solutions that can't be carried out, nor solutions so unsophisticated that they only appeal to what can be done now and go unnoticed. That's precisely where design happens—when we're able to understand the limitations and our objective, when we're able to understand that not everything is perfect, it never will be, but it's always, also, perfectible.

After launching a first version of your new product, everything becomes about incremental versions. It's easier to understand what we need to do because the product has finally faced users. It's easier to create a roadmap with this feedback. The design and launch of following versions doesn't stop being complex, but it's a bit more certain.

The value of a good story

Narrative or storytelling is at the core of design. There's no way to do good design if we can't communicate it clearly and simply.

Design can be intuitive and speak for itself, but Tony isn't referring to describing what our design does, nor to telling fantastic stories just to cover expectations that will never be met.

He's actually talking about storytelling as a way to approach with clarity our objective as an organization that seeks to create and develop a product.

"Every product should have a story, a narrative that explains why it needs to exist and how it will solve your customer's problems."

If we manage to create a consistent and true story about our product, it will be easier for the team to have clarity about what we're seeking when there's crisis or ambiguity.

If we've assimilated and internalized it and it's real, our product will be a reflection of that. The experience we provide will be equally consistent, and now not only our team but our customers will be the ones who assimilate this story and make it their own. When they see the value in our product, they go out and share it with their closest circle—with this same story.

The experience as a whole

"The customer doesn't differentiate between advertising, the app, and customer service. Everything is part of your company. Your brand. Everything is one."

And this is one of the positions that made the most sense to me—one that's obvious, but we forget.

We can fall into false dogmatisms, live in a bubble, assume we own the experience, when maybe we're just enablers and responsible for a fragment that's part of a larger system.

But for the customer, there's no difference. It's all the same—whether it's the salesperson who helped them get the product, whether they're opening our product, downloading our app and logging in for the first time, whether it works perfectly, or whether they need to call support to solve a problem, or even when they cancel. For the customer it's the same experience, and the complete experience is what we need to look at.

That's where it makes so much sense that he invites us to look at the whole without losing details—and even to obsess over knowing those details.
