How does the user actually do it, and how do I take care of the complete experience? These are boring details we sometimes don't want to look at, but it's necessary to do so and not just focus on the seductive part of the project.

Yes, I know—if you're reading this, you're probably nowhere near being the director of your area or the CEO where you work. But having this mindset clear will help us understand that it's centered on our clients and/or users and not centered on design. The experience happens before and after you. You're not the owner or protagonist—you're an enabler.

See you in the next edition…

Maybe we could write about what this same book taught me about teams that make products. Let me know if that would interest you—leave comments!

To Read 👀

Why do they ignore my awesome design documentation?

Why doesn't anyone read our documentation? For those who work on Design Systems and Handoff processes, this article is a great reference for best practices to keep your whole team involved and make effective documentation.

To listen and watch 🎧

item.title: Fidelity Levels in Design Artifacts
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: I've mentioned this in the previous edition, Design implies being able to communicate ideas effectively. If people can't understand what you communicate, they won't be able to understand how you made decisions.
item.content:encoded:
Understanding the audience you communicate your design progress to for effective communication.

Ed. 43

I've mentioned this in the previous edition, Design implies being able to communicate ideas effectively. If people can't understand what you communicate, they won't be able to understand how you made decisions.

This is crucial when talking to clients, if you're a freelancer, but also with stakeholders when you're at a company, with developers or product people on your team, and of course, with design peers you collaborate with and have reviews and critiques with.

Beyond articulating a narrative

It's not just about articulating discourse orally when presenting—the strategy for communicating effectively is understanding the type of audience the message is directed to.

You have to understand that beyond how you modulate your messages, oral or written, you need to understand the fidelity levels of the resources we accompany our explanations with.

Naturally, making a sketch is much simpler than making a mockup or a high-fidelity prototype. In the same way, it will be necessary to provide more context as our contact with our audience decreases. It's easier to share an idea about the project we're working on with people close to the project using a sketch, because they have shared context and the details are implied. It won't be the same if your audience is your manager, a director, or even a C-Level like a CEO.

That's why, in many cases, usability tests are done with demos or prototypes as close to reality as possible—your users have zero context, so they need the highest fidelity or the final product itself.

The goal is for your audience to focus on what you want to review, where you have doubts and need feedback. Don't make your audience waste time understanding your artifact by not having enough context, by having a lower level of fidelity than required.

From presenting in person to sending by email

This same fidelity relationship applies to whether the conversation channel is synchronous or real-time, like an in-person presentation. These types of dynamics have more reading tools like non-verbal communication, and it's easier to understand when people have doubts.

This starts to change if the communication, despite being real-time, happens on screens, like a video call. Non-verbal reading will depend on the video quality and whether your audience has their camera on—without doubt, having control of the required fidelity level will make your presentation more direct and effective.

With asynchronous channels like Slack, Teams, or even WhatsApp or email, we don't have our audience face to face. It will be necessary to have a higher level of fidelity for what we want to review. We'll even have to think about what we're going to write, so that in a single read, we can communicate the context with a clear deliverable. With higher fidelity we communicate our idea better and avoid having doubts we won't be able to answer by not being present when the message is received.

Optimize your resources; not everything needs high fidelity

As a designer and visual communicator I appeal a lot to order and quality of deliverables, but I definitely appeal more to being effective and investing time in what truly matters. Going from a hand-made wireframe, to a low-fidelity digital one, then passing it to high-fidelity and then a mockup for everything you do will only make you waste time—not everything is validated with the same audience and not everything requires going through each phase and having the same fidelity.

The last thing we want is to be making wireframes when the product is about to launch just to follow "due process."

We'll get more out of the time we're destined to spend designing deliverables if they have an objective (whatever you want to communicate and/or validate) and have clear the necessary fidelity level, because we understand who our audience will be and the type of channel we'll use to present.

This will also help us determine what type of fidelity we need based on the ideation level we're at. We're reviewing the flow—do we need mockups? We're reviewing a micro-interaction—is a low-fidelity wireframe enough?

When we're doing general ideations it doesn't suit us to do many details, because these take more time and these iterations tend to be faster, which makes us invest extra time in something that will easily change.

On the contrary, when exercises tend to focus on details like micro-interactions, it's ideal to make much more detailed artifacts and our iterations will be slower or will change only in details and not in their entirety.

I hope all this is useful to you and maybe you'll understand (it happened to me many times) why sometimes when you explained your project to a CEO or by email, the response tends to be questions about what you're showing instead of feedback, which was what you were originally looking for (it happened to me more times than I'd like to admit).

I learned this from reading Collaborative Product Design more than 2 years ago (first chapters) and I've used it every time I present at my job.

See you in the next edition.

To Watch 👀

I finished the Fundamentals of Behavior for User Experience (UX) Design and Evaluation course at Sperentia Academy—beyond saying the course seemed very interesting to me and made me interested in what the different behavior models are about. This video gives us an idea of what's behind the science of persuasion. I identified with more than one shortcut.

To Listen 🎧

Unicornio - Ramón Amezcua (Dj Tlo remix)

When the Nortec movement was just beginning, Dj Tlo made this remix of the Unicornio track, originally by Ramón Amezcua a.k.a. Bostich. A forceful 4x4 techno that blurs banda samples without losing the groove.

item.title: When Artificial Intelligence Catches Up to Us.
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: The latest Adobe Photoshop Beta is without a doubt the utopia of any designer who works in digital graphic editing. What used to be memes, impossible things that only made sense as jokes, are now our closest reality.
item.content:encoded:
Interface design will be automated; but that's not the real problem.

Ed.44

The latest Adobe Photoshop Beta is without a doubt the utopia of any designer who works in digital graphic editing. What used to be memes, impossible things that only made sense as jokes, are now our closest reality.

It won't be long before these advances are inherited by interface design applications. There are already several examples like Diagram that are starting to do it.

Subscribe and get the next edition directly in your inbox!

Feeling that AI (Artificial Intelligence) automates much of your work can be exciting, because of course, for the more experienced this takes away the load I call "grunt work" that we all find tedious. The processes we'd like to automate are clear, because that's what keeps us from focusing on high-level problems.

The problem arises for newcomers, those who confuse design execution with tools and technical skills. I myself in my first years as a recently graduated designer had that confusion. Design is what happens between having a blank page, imagining the future, understanding contexts, constraints, and making decisions.

The value of selecting a good proposal

Design is born from a strategy, from a plan, but impact is only created when that plan is taken to implementation, going from the abstract to the concrete, from the divergent to the convergent, from plan to action.

We can automate our work and that's not bad. We can become "curators" of options and paths; in the end, it's the same thing we do now, only those options and paths will be created by AI, not by us. It hurts because deep down we believe that creation and ideation has more merit than selection, but the truth is that we ideate as a vehicle to select the best solution.

Famous and renowned graphic designers like Paula Scher work this way. Her team is in charge of developing the proposals—younger designers who have technical mastery create the proposals that carry Paula's direction and intention. In the end, the best proposal is selected to validate with the client. I don't see anyone taking credit away from her or thinking she does more or less design.

The days of GUI (Graphic User Interfaces) are numbered… if we're referring to our work tending to be automated, it's very likely…

Faced with these statements, we could get angry and tear our clothes, as if our status quo were being attacked. But the reality is that we don't control this kind of change. In any case, we're only part of it. We navigate in that sea of conditions trying to control the small portion of water that's ours, but only that. I work in an industry guided by standards. Mastery of those standards has nothing to do with being a good designer, so I don't think that's where our value as designers lies.

Let's stop thinking that tools define our way of working and our status quo. We can be competitive in a market that asks for certain technical software knowledge, but our value lies in how we use these tools to ideate, select, and then yes, with that result create value in the chain.

I've realized that my best contributions happen when I understand my thinking process, sketch out paths, and can select. Of course, knowing auto layout and other hacks help me and make that path easier. I can even help other designers understand their way of thinking, understand why they're making those decisions, and how perhaps they've analyzed all possible paths.

Maybe it's time to look at the value of what we do for being that human factor that selects and makes decisions, not for the amount of software we handle.

Strategic mindset, survival resource.

In his article The Emergence of Artificial Professionals in Design: Finding the Balance Between Automation and Value, Solca tells us that facing Artificial designers is only possible when designers use the tools at their reach (AI) wisely and understand that it's about improving their skills, not replacing them. It's about understanding the black box and not expecting magic solutions.

Artificial Designers "are people who may not have the training or experience necessary to create quality designs, but rely heavily on AI to generate designs quickly." Adrian Solca

It's natural that many designers will find meaning in giving value to their process and will stop seeing tools as the holy grail that makes them designers. Understanding this, they'll seek to train themselves in those things that give them value in their process. They'll cultivate what helps them think outside the box and analyze information that helps them make better decisions.

This is a transition stage where many changes will follow, where as processes are automated they'll be shorter and less expensive. Will there be more designers with the capacity to work on more projects? Does it imply there will be less demand for these profiles?

A few decades ago there were at least five roles that today Adobe InDesign and an operator of this software do. Today we don't need an army of interface designers to change a thousand screens—a component in Figma and one click is enough to make changes. What makes us think we'll need the same number of designers if we work faster with AI ideation?

And I don't ask this as a question that predicts something bad. I ask it as an open question, to make us reflect on our capabilities and market saturation. As Omar Mendoza once told me, "we need to stop navel-gazing and see that there's design outside the Tech Industry and UX."

Tools don't define my value as a designer. Neither does the industry. People will keep having problems and, automated or not, good designers will solve those problems by designing.

See you in the next edition.

Para Ver 👀

Increasing your influence as a designer

If you want to hear in a hard and pure way how designers should focus on creating impact, you should listen to this Deep Dives episode where they interview Mia Blume, design leader at Square and Pinterest.

Para escuchar 🎧

Another Girl - Jaques Greene

item.title: From Metaverse to Spatial Computing
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: That was the only thing going through my head while watching WWDC23 when I realized what Apple was announcing was a headset…
item.content:encoded:
Apple's new bet—Will it really change the industry?

Ed.45

What must Mark Zuckerberg be thinking right now?…

That was the only thing going through my head while watching WWDC23 when I realized what Apple was announcing was a headset…

Distancing itself from the already burned-out term Metaverse, Apple creates its narrative around Spatial Computing, a concept Apple has been working on for a long time with Spatial Design (In edition no.1 we already shared information about this).

But unlike Meta, Apple talks to us not just about VR (Virtual Reality), instead opting to talk about the hybrid between AR (Augmented Reality) and VR, calling it XR (Extended Reality, understood as the combination of AR and VR) trying to mitigate very forcefully the Achilles heel for detractors of this type of technology: user alienation and by definition a technology that isolates instead of connects.

High-end technology that pays the cost of not being mass-market.

Personally, I always felt the aesthetic that Facebook's demos with the Metaverse showed us was unfinished. Apple broke with that paradigm, it has shown us the way with XR, and although the concept is not new, it does feel different. The level of detail in the headset's interface makes it integrate fully with what we see.

Since Yosemite, Apple has been implementing glassmorphism style and we can see its maximum expression in the spatial interface of Apple Vision Pro—something very appropriate because our interface consists of translucent panels that integrate better with physical space.

The details in shadows and reflections are without a doubt what take these interfaces to the next level. Now some AR examples that don't respond to this same level of detail can feel clumsy.

And, as usual, the gestures are entirely manual—there are no video game console-style controllers, just your simple hands and gestures that today feel very natural thanks to smartphones.

And it's clear that this is thanks to the technological combo these headsets have, since they are themselves a computer (Perhaps we're talking about the post-Mobile device?). Apple's move was not to bet on a mass consumer device. That lets you give free rein to technical requirements, because you don't need it to work for most segments, only for those willing to pay for it: the early-adopters, those who lined up to buy the first iPhone, or those who bought one of the first 3D printers on KickStarter in 2010.

Breaking the industry standard: true mass adoption

Apple reinvented the personal computing industry in 1984 with the presentation of the first Macintosh. The GUI is a paradigm we still find today. Microsoft ate up the market and became a monopoly using this same approach in Windows.

It reinvented the music industry with the iPod in 2001. From that moment on, earphones stopped being black (walkman era) and became white (iPod style). But above all, it changed the paradigm of what portable music was, taking it to the next level.

Years later, Apple did it again, now reinventing the mobile phone industry, again in 2007, when the iPhone was presented. This made Google rethink the Nexus design that had come out years before, removing the keyboard from the original design, leaving only a fully touch screen.

Surpassing the standards that smartphones had up to that point (which were not cheap and were niche), Apple took the risk of releasing a phone starting at 500 dollars, competing in a professional market, but that anyone wanted to have in their hand.

Apple opted for bigger specs, which allowed it to have a much more refined interface, incorporating gestures with your fingers that in other options were solved with a stylus.

And it wasn't perfect, and at a certain level this first version had limited features. It took 2 years for the copy and paste gesture to be available on iPhone.

History speaks for itself. After 2008, the mobile phone paradigm was having no keyboard, downloading apps, and being able to use it for many more things than a conventional phone.

Breaking the paradigm goes beyond just creating a mass consumer item. On the contrary, Apple has been characterized by setting the standard and then the industry moves in the same direction.

That same pattern continues with Apple Vision Pro, as I wrote a paragraph above, but updated to now:

Apple opted for bigger specs, which allow it to have a much more refined interface, incorporating gestures that in other options were solved with video game controllers.

History will tell us in 2025 whether this becomes the next thing along with Artificial Intelligence or not, and whether it remains just a memory.

Failure is possible and has happened.

23 years ago Apple launched the Mac Cube, personally a computer I find beautiful, one whose design even today remains current—it doesn't feel vintage.

An impeccable design that by price was positioned for a professional audience but with specs more oriented toward the normal user.

In a professional market, design matters, but power wins the discussion. This cube was caught in the middle. Apple learned the lesson by pulling it from the market a year later.

For me, the Mac Cube ended up evolving into two products. The first, the Mac mini, which served home users who don't need too many specs, and more recently the Mac Studio, which serves professional audiences who can't go for a Mac Pro.

Innovation is seductive, but it takes more than that to change the industry and set the next standard.

One More Thing….

The Apple Vision Pro is seductive, but it didn't show uses beyond entertainment and leisure. We know they have partnerships and the developer kit has been released so they can create applications in what has been called VisionOS, but I don't know if, despite that, it will become something similar to the Mac Cube—not justifiable no matter how much design and hardware it has.

That's why I don't dare say that Apple Vision Pro will be the new reinvention Apple will make in the industry.

What I see clearly is the pattern where it can do things where other competitors haven't focused, where other limitations, like lower costs that allow reaching more audiences, limit the technology you can put in headsets.

Maybe this will make the industry focus more on developing more experiences of this type, but without real substantial uses, this won't be possible.

I have a hard time imagining completely replacing my current computer or phone with this, and, although it's great not having screen limitations when watching movies or using applications, it's still a technology that puts a barrier to social interaction because it's something you put on your face and not something you use with your hands.

I work remotely, and maybe there face-to-face interaction doesn't matter as much, but I still don't have my toolkit that I use for day-to-day work available on Apple Vision Pro. Without that, I can't get on board, because its price justified only for leisure doesn't make sense to me.

Let's see what happens, nothing is written. See you in the next edition.

To Read and Watch 👀

Principles of spatial design

Discover the fundamentals of spatial design. Learn to design with depth, scale, windows, and immersion, and apply best practices to create comfortable, human-centered experiences that transform reality. Discover how you can use these spatial design principles to extend your existing application or bring a new idea to life.

To Listen 🎧

Sais - Floating Points

item.title: The best WWDC videos about interface design
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: Today I'll share a very personal reflection. It's not about design, but it is about my view on life and our purpose.
item.content:encoded:
6# The Legacy

Today I'll share a very personal reflection. It's not about design, but it is about my view on life and our purpose.

ed 46

When I was in high school—don't ask, it was a long time ago—the concept of transcendence captivated me. The idea that people would talk about me after I was gone felt powerful. Leaving a legacy, something to be remembered for.

Maybe that's why after finishing my degree I threw myself completely into professional growth. While I had full-time jobs, I freelanced like crazy. The goal was to achieve big things. I think that idea of legacy motivated me, but I won't deny it—my passion and love for design was the complement that turned me into a workaholic eager to learn.

My idea of legacy has evolved. I can say I live well, I love what I do, and I have the best family. I'm happy—not because I have everything I want (I don't), but because I enjoy what I have.

More than 6 years ago, one of the most important events of my life happened. It was planned, sure, but I had no idea how it would change my way of seeing things: my daughter was born.

I started to understand that what's interesting about life isn't the goal but the journey. The moments I live with my daughter are unique. I'll surely miss some, but in the ones where I'm present, I'll be fully there, because life is what happens between deciding to take on the world and actually doing it.

In this span of time between defining our objective and achieving it is where we can treasure the best, unrepeatable moments of our lives—or blow right past them by being obsessed with the future, with the goal, with the "when I finally make it."

I'm realistic. Even though I do my best, I don't think I'll make it into history books. I don't think I'll be the one who changes something transcendent directly, leaving their legacy. Staying present has become essential (and I don't always manage it because of my obsessions with the future). One day my daughter asks me for help holding a plate, and the next she's pouring cereal by herself without asking.

Also, I realized that just like a product experience will happen whether we design it consciously or not—my daughter will learn from me, whether I'm conscious of it or not. I have to be clear and deliberate in the messages I give. I have to be there, not on autopilot.

My daughter will be the protagonist of her own story. But I can be an enabler—someone who helps her understand the world. I like to think I can help shape a good citizen of the world, that I can contribute my grain of sand… my legacy.

I started looking at my professional life through this lens. What if instead of being a protagonist, I'm an enabler? What if I can help others discover the world of design when they're taking their first steps? What if when I'm with someone more junior I can accelerate their growth process—because in that process I also learn and grow? Maybe I like to think I serve something bigger than my own individual value. Contributing to the design community indirectly by helping improve it.

My time in this world is temporary and finite. I don't know if it's worth living it in pursuit of transcendence—because in the end, transcendence happens when you're not here anymore, when you no longer feel and no longer exist. Basically, to transcend you have to stop being. I won't be around to verify it.

And yet—what seduces me more is that in 10, 20, or maybe 30 years after I'm gone, my daughter will remember me. Not for the "legacy I left," but for those unforgettable moments I gave her by being present, by enjoying life.

In the same way, I like to think that instead of leaving a legacy to society as a designer, I help someone change their perspective, see things with different eyes. And who knows—maybe at some future moment, they'll also remember me for what I could offer by being present.

See you in the next edition

Para Leer y ver 👀

Anthony Hobday gives us this great list of videos—one of those links you should save.

Para escuchar 🎧

item.title: My Version of Desk Research for UI and Interaction Designers.
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: As I'm finishing up this edition, there are 208 subscribers to Iterative Thinking. Thank you so much, and to the newcomers—welcome!
item.content:encoded:
How to create an information repository to consult in the future.

As I'm finishing up this edition, there are 208 subscribers to Iterative Thinking. Thank you so much, and to the newcomers—welcome!

ed. 47

When you work in product design, you can't expect everything to come through inspiration, that you'll magically find solid solutions that work.

At least in my case it doesn't work that way…

Every time I have to design a solution, Jakob Nielsen's law comes to mind.

"Users spend most of their time on other sites. This means that users prefer your site to work the same way as all the other sites they already know."

This implies that the minimum you should ask yourself at the start of designing an interface is Has this been done before? Who did it and how did they solve it?

Naturally, any interface or interaction designer should analyze their environment, review the applications they use daily, and beyond being captivated by new micro-interactions they find in daily apps, they should question how these work and respond, how they function in interface layout, etc., etc., etc.

The problem arises when very few of us will be working on music apps, chats, or social networks—assuming what you use most is WhatsApp, Instagram, TikTok, and even Facebook. Our visual and interaction culture can't be limited to just what we use day to day.

And, being realistic, you don't start looking at banking apps until you work at one, you don't pay attention to components until you work in Design Systems, you don't become sensitive to an onboarding flow until you have to design one.

You do it this way because we have limited attention, we can't learn everything, at least not at the same time. You'll always face the situation of starting with zero knowledge, that's normal. What's interesting is how you catalyze the learning process to acquire that knowledge.

How I start an interface, flows, or interaction project.

In an ideal world you could test everything, users would be the source of truth, and their insights would become a clear direction for where to design. Design, test, and iterate—sounds nice…

But in the real world you can't test everything, due to time and budget constraints. Those limitations are part of the design process, and we must work within them. Inevitably, you'll test where there's more uncertainty, and you'll have to leave things out. And no, those things you leave out won't be resolved randomly—you can do something about it.

That's why I always suggest starting with what I call desk research, which is basically searching for articles that discuss best practices for design patterns (and learning what other people have done and validated). You can find reports, analyses, or documentation in design systems about which patterns are better and why.

This helps us understand generally what works and what doesn't, contexts of use, and even things we should avoid when designing these kinds of components—without necessarily testing them. If this isn't enough we could test them with users, but in many cases this will clear up a lot of doubts. It gives us an initial understanding to make design decisions in our first ideations. Of course, you'll need to understand your project's own context—best practices are guides, but they're not rules carved in stone, so take them as reference.

How do we start? Just use something like this in Google:

"Best practice for [Component/design pattern name] in [UX/ Interaction Design]"

You're more likely to find more information in English than Spanish. But that's what Google Translate is for (I encourage you to learn English, but these days that shouldn't be a problem for reading articles).

Ideally, don't go with the first article you find. At least look for 3 to 5 sources and check their relevance—an article from 10 years ago isn't the same as one from last year.

You can always do this exercise proactively, without waiting for your work to require this kind of research.

Sites like NN Group, Medium, Toptal, a List Apart, Smashing Magazine, Build from Mars, Case Study Club among others, do very good analysis on specific topics about components and design patterns.

And of course, if the budget allows, there are many books that can address various topics more generally, to understand the fundamentals of UI and interaction.

If we do this process proactively, we'll fill ourselves with information we're not certain when we'll use. Remember that attention is limited—we need to manage this information we find so we can access it quickly when we need it.

My system for never losing valuable information for the future

I'm a compulsive reader, but in reality, I consult and scan more articles and books than I read completely. I like to learn about reflections on current design problems, about the latest changes in design tools, productivity, leadership, and technology in general. But I never have enough time to read everything, much less at the same time. And worse still, today I might find something that will be useful to me months later. Has this happened to you?

Besides doing desk research proactively, I follow several newsletters so information comes to me and I don't always have to go looking for it. These are examples of newsletters that will bring articles to your inbox:

  • Hey Designer (daily format)
  • Sidebar
  • UX Collective

These are some of the ones that give me the most information, but as I said above, I read less than I document to consult later. For me there are three moments when I read about design:

1) Right away, when I have time and there's something very interesting

2) Days later, when I review my saved articles archive

3) When I need information I already know I saved.

Those who know me know I never tire of recommending PocketApp. I basically have it installed as an extension in my browser, on my computer and phone. I use it to save links to read later. This tool lets me categorize them with tags, so in the future I'll only have to search for a specific topic, without needing to remember where the article was published or its title.

So if I find best practices for some component, some article about a topic I'm looking to learn at a certain moment, and the information is good (I scan it first), I save it with tags in PocketApp to consult in the future.

This makes my desk research a more optimized process. Before searching Google, I check PocketApp with my collection of links and tags, catalyzing the learning process to acquire knowledge in that area where I don't have much knowledge.

You learn things, and over time you connect many reference points that will be useful for creating solid solutions with good arguments.

See you in the next edition.

To watch 👀

We Start Season 4 of The UX Rebels

After taking several months without uploading videos and preparing the pre-production of the first episodes, we kick off the fourth season of The UX Rebels with an interview with Adrián Kane where we talk about his journey in UX and his experience as a bootcamp instructor.

To listen 🎧

item.title: Tools: Biases and Enablers of Design
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: Before we begin, I want to welcome all the new subscribers and give a special thank you to UXBS from my friend Cristobal. I know many of you who subscribed to this newsletter in recent weeks did so from his recommendation. If you're not following the UXBS newsletter, I can only tell you that you're …
item.content:encoded:
Design goes beyond the tools we use.

Ed. 48

Before we begin, I want to welcome all the new subscribers and give a special thank you to UXBS from my friend Cristobal. I know many of you who subscribed to this newsletter in recent weeks did so from his recommendation. If you're not following the UXBS newsletter, I can only tell you that you're missing out on valuable content.

Tools are fundamental to building products—they define the capabilities, quality, and efficiency of the development process. Choosing the right tools should be based on a clear understanding of the team's and product's needs, as well as factors like scalability, cost, and security.

While tools enable things, they also delimit scenarios and frame us within specific mental models. These mental models can make us believe that anything outside of them is incorrect or poorly implemented.

The Bias of Tools

A few weeks ago, David Heinemeier, co-owner and CTO of 37Signals, talked about how his teams don't use Figma to design and instead jump straight into iterating in code.

Many people's first reading was that David is inviting us to skip "Design"—that ideation stage where we iterate to arrive at the best solution. Our mental model makes us assume that anything outside of Figma is, by definition, no longer design.

Design as a process is not married to a tool. Before Figma, design was done in Photoshop. Before that, design was done with mechanical originals, pencil and paper, blueprints, calculations, and projections.

Before the Web 2.0 boom, it was done in code. Steve Krug ran usability tests and didn't work with interface designers but with engineers.

At that time, tools like Figma were unthinkable. And certainly, experience design moved into a stage of specialization and sophistication—Web 1.0 has nothing to do with what we have today with mobile devices.

The ideal is not to reduce the value of what we do to how we do it. Design is a process for solving a problem, and the solution will largely be framed by the tools we use—no doubt about that. But those tools will change and depend on context, culture, and the standards of new technologies.

We must understand that tools are just that: tools.

It would be like saying designers today must necessarily draw, must necessarily use Figma, or must necessarily use the Double Diamond. Our toolkit will be conditioned by the market and current standards.

That's why we need to root the value of what we do in values and principles that aren't anchored to tools and technical processes that can change.

Of course, the richer your range of tools, the better you can adapt to different contexts. Having the skill to sketch quickly is very advantageous when you're trying to land ideas. Knowing how to use Figma definitely reduces the learning curve if that's what they use where you work.

Surely many of you have discovered that moving from one tool to another is easy because you can connect the dots of similarity and focus on what's different. The same happens with other kinds of tools—imagine your bias when you want to solve everything with just Design Thinking, Lean UX, or Double Diamond.

Tools as Enablers

If we understand this—that tools are enablers for solving problems—we'll understand that contexts are so varied that there are rarely unique or perfect approaches or solutions.

This should naturally lead us to analyze what we need to improve and what tools we need to integrate into our personal kit. At the same time, we shouldn't stop questioning whether the way we do things now is the only way or if there could be others—understanding that this means incorporating new skills or tools. But it will also lead us to new approaches and a fresh perspective on how we see what we design.

See you in the next edition.

To Read or Watch 👀

UX Research books

Victor González published this article on LinkedIn about his top books for learning about UX Research

To Listen… and a bit more 🎧

Starfall - Marc Hanania

item.title: Design Automaton
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: The practice of design is refined over time and through practice, we already discussed this in edition 14 of this newsletter. Your theoretical and academic foundation is just a starting point. Experience is forged by designing — otherwise we end up being design theorists but not designers.
item.content:encoded:
Ed. 49

The practice of design is refined over time and through practice, we already discussed this in edition 14 of this newsletter. Your theoretical and academic foundation is just a starting point. Experience is forged by designing — otherwise we end up being design theorists but not designers.

When we gain experience, when we've been at this for several years, we can fall into biases. We'll probably try to solve things with our own recipes that we've created over time and that have worked best for us. We tend to systematize our thinking process and with it the mental models we use to understand and solve problems. We're sentenced to repeat formulas, over and over again.

Learning from the new ones

From there, many designers will tend to be dogmatic about the practice, and everything outside their mental model will be evaluated through a lens of skepticism.

Subscribe to Iterative Thinking!

That's why we have designers who nostalgically proclaim past eras as better — those who, seeing their conception of what design is change, declare that design is dead.

That's why we must always stay open, and even engage with ways of thinking contrary to our own, understanding that what we perceive is only a fragment of reality.

This openness has made me aware of my evolution in design. I can still remember when I was the new one, the recent graduate, and now perhaps I'm starting to become a veteran. But if I look back and compare myself to that Ulises, I'm not the same. I've changed, my motivations have changed, and the way I understand the world has too. I call it maturing. Others call it getting old.

I've seen people who have developed their careers successfully toward management in a short time. People I was guiding or teaching years ago are design managers today.

But at specific moments I've asked myself: How can someone who's been in the industry half as long as me, with a senior position or higher than mine, tell me what I need to do, mark the path to follow?

We tend to think that the way to obtain seniority is only through years of experience, where skills are developed. But it's also influenced by developing soft skills that allow us to have a positive impact on organizations — like leadership, self-management, or emotional intelligence. These can be learned effectively and quickly, and without them your developed seniority will be little or none.

As designers we must adapt to reality in its continuous change, in a shifting industry that mutates. That won't be possible if we're not willing to unlearn and learn new things.

It's very easy to judge a design career based only on years. But in my experience, many of those who now play management roles have achieved it because they've managed to learn soft skills effectively and quickly, skills that have accelerated their growth. And also in my experience, if we listen to them, they will enrich our worldview and with it the design solutions we arrive at.

It will happen, naturally, that a designer with more years in the industry will be more experienced for some situations than one with fewer. But the learning process is always two-way, and you don't need to be in a hierarchical position for people to learn from you — they will listen.

The mirage of getting good just with the passage of time

The mistake of many designers is assuming that just by having more years in the career, skills are acquired automatically.

In Design, the development of experience happens through time, exercising the muscle of connecting dots and reflecting. But without awareness of this process you can spend years without evolution or maturity, becoming what I call a "Design Automaton."

A Design Automaton has only imitated themselves over and over in their process, assuming they can solve everything the same way. They can do their job in a regular manner, but their maturity and evolution are flat. They don't tend to reflect, so their critical sense is minimal. Design has always been this way and there's no need or option to change how things are done. They believe they know everything, or at least, they believe that few things, or people, at this point will teach them something. Assuming that just by having years of practice their place in the industry must be respected.

Seniority takes years, but not only years. It's a process of constant learning and, blinded by ego, we can think that we can no longer learn new things (because we assume we're at the top of the mountain) and we lose opportunities to learn in the most unexpected ways.

If we don't have clarity about our weaknesses, we will hardly be able to evolve. Self-knowledge and questioning ourselves is key. And this leads us to accept that we don't do things perfectly, that the top of the mountain is only a state we aspire to, which we must pursue, but it's not a final state. Learning never ends.

Design Automatons at any level

It might seem like my critique is centered on designers who have been in the industry for many years, but Design Automatons exist at any level. The challenge is learning from people who have developed through experiences different from yours, at different times, and with different levels of seniority — that is, having openness and understanding that you're always learning.

Skepticism toward people with more years is a pattern we can find in younger ones, who can be biased by thinking that the latest is the best, that what preceded them doesn't matter, or worse, they ignore it, limiting their growth.

"No matter how much experience, a designer must not only continue learning, they must keep an open mind. The new ones should keep an open mind, but be willing to learn from those with more years of experience." — Benjamín García

See you in the next edition

Who has the credentials to deliver design feedback?

Speaking of credentials and skepticism, this article discusses how giving feedback is not the same as executing Design — they're two skills that aren't necessarily linked.

To Listen… and a bit more 🎧

item.title: The trap of "common sense" and "intuition" in design.
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: This edition marks a very important milestone. Originally when I started this newsletter I wanted to write 52 editions in a year (one per week) and it took me 8 extra months to achieve it. However, 52 was a number that seemed far away and now it has been reached. Thanks to everyone who has been read…
item.content:encoded:
Demystifying the design process as something magical, and something you're born with. We connect dots faster the more we practice and learn.

ed. 50

This edition marks a very important milestone. Originally when I started this newsletter I wanted to write 52 editions in a year (one per week) and it took me 8 extra months to achieve it. However, 52 was a number that seemed far away and now it has been reached. Thanks to everyone who has been reading me since then, and for the newcomers, welcome, look through the archive, there's surely more than one post that will interest you.

When I was in college, I could see how my adjunct professors could arrive at solutions faster and better executed than I could. It seemed like they simply acted. I didn't understand how they could maneuver at that speed and have clarity about what to do and how to do it.

Being honest, at that time I saw several of them as geniuses. Individuals who could simply do things I couldn't. And in my head the idea of the creative genius became mythified — the one who in a matter of seconds can do everything.

Subscribe and receive the next edition in your inbox

Many years later I realized that what at that moment looks like some kind of genius, isn't that at all. It's about years of experience, where you learn over and over again, and with time, you start seeing the same actors with different scenarios.

Tearing down the creative genius

This is a call to tear down the idea of intuition and common sense, to avoid the myth of the creative genius — the one who was born with a certain advantage and only needs to breathe to design. This idea must be torn down by those who have been in the industry longer.

I've learned that common sense doesn't exist, or in any case it's encapsulated in contexts, but it's never universal. In the same way, intuition is nothing more than previous experiences that help us have mental shortcuts or improvise (as if it were a jazz band) without ever losing sight of what we want to achieve with our design.

Recently I came across a LinkedIn post that invited us to use more common sense and intuition when designing interfaces and interaction; rather than trying to test everything with users. I'm aware of what the author was after, because genuinely, in the design process, whether due to time or budget, we won't be able to test everything.

What I don't agree with, on a personal level, is simplifying the design process to the use of "intuition" and "common sense" — subjective concepts that are unclear about what doing design actually is.

Intuition is by definition "The ability to know, understand or perceive something clearly and immediately, without the intervention of reason."

The definition sounds a bit magical, and exclusionary. It doesn't matter how much you've learned, because reason has nothing to do with it.

That's why I prefer to talk about practice, and practice won't be the same for those recently graduated from universities in design careers, related and adjacent fields; and much less for those who wanted to pivot their career and are graduating from a bootcamp, but you can develop practice. Practice is a matter of patience and time, and of course, as I said in the previous edition, of being conscious that you're learning.

As for common sense, we have that it's "The capacity to judge reasonably the situations of everyday life and decide correctly."

I tend to feel judging reasonably is subjective, and although I believe that to some extent we can share concepts, signifiers and meanings, this is about determined contexts. Contexts are defined geographically and temporally, and many times this common sense changes, and can contradict itself if compared with the past.

What I do believe is that the designer has the obligation to assimilate the context they work for, to be able to understand reality empathetically. With practice we can assimilate new contexts more quickly.

The designer who understands realities and cultivates practice

This is also a call to become more responsible with the new generations of design, and to cultivate in them the approach of research and constant questioning. Practice is more than what Design Automata do. Let's remove the idea of intuition and instead foster retrospectives and continuous learning, helping them take advantage of the information age.

Following the school of Dr. Víctor González (Founder of Sperientia) on UX Research with organizational impact through agile processes, we will always tend to prioritize user validation where there are more doubts or uncertainty, in key steps where we must guarantee the success of the task to achieve business goals. Maximizing value delivery in short timeframes. We won't always be able to validate everything, due to simple matters of time or budget.

Techniques like Desk Research applied to interaction design are key to making better decisions, without necessarily validating with the user, complementing what we do validate with them.

The designers who, to younger ones, seem to be the ones who know the most, or who "intuitively" make the right decisions, are actually the ones who never stop learning and updating their knowledge. The best decisions are the fruit of information you can consult, validate with users, and your capacity for analysis — but not of magical intuition.

Those designers who seem to understand their audience or user better do so because they've been able to assimilate their context, and they rarely let themselves be carried by common sense. Instead they prefer to be observers — obsessive and relentless. Understanding that each case is different and in many cases unique.

Let's seek to remove magical ideas about the design process, especially in UX, to help new generations be more analytical and not act randomly.

See you in the next edition.

Para ver 👀

Passion in UX with Lis Fernandez

In our most recent episode we talked with Lis Fernandez from @todotieneux about passion and how UX isn't just about making money.

Para escuchar 🎧

item.title: 4 Things Tony Fadell's Build Taught Me About Team Design
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: Second post about Tony Fadell's "Build." You can read my first post about this book here.
item.content:encoded:
4 ideas I found brilliant when Fadell talks about team design and management in his book "Build."

ed. 51

Second post about Tony Fadell's "Build." You can read my first post about this book here.

Tony's life was undoubtedly full of challenges, from when he started his career at General Magic, then the iPod at Apple, and Nest. One thing Fadell has made clear in his book is that creating and managing teams is key to product success.

1. "So before deciding to become a manager, you have to think carefully about whether it's the right path for you. Because you don't have to be one. Especially if you don't actually want to but think taking on management is the only way to advance in your professional career."

Fadell emphasizes that team management isn't easy and you need an affinity for it, but he also points out that it's okay not to do it, that you can be a great individual contributor and don't need to be a manager to stand out.

In many companies where specialists are needed more than generalists, it's natural to see career paths for individual contributors. Senior, Principal, Senior Principal, and whatever names these levels might have depending on the company—being an IC (Individual Contributor) is an alternative for those who aren't cut out for management, understanding that their experience and leadership can positively impact organizations by catalyzing projects, but without having a team reporting to them.

Perhaps many companies that have design teams today, whether due to size or maturity (especially in LATAM), have the pending task of developing a career path for individual contributors, because in many cases moving up to a management layer is the only way to keep developing your career.

2. "Becoming a manager is a discipline. Managing is a learned skill, not a gift. You're not born with it."

On this point it's obvious that although we might have a natural inclination or affinity toward certain activities, the truth is that without consistency and discipline this wouldn't be possible.

Whether you're interested in developing a career in management or you've taken the opportunity to develop in this role now. Remember that you need to learn more about people's behavior, active listening skills, negotiation and assertive communication, and emotional intelligence—these are just some of the skills you need to develop and refine.

3. "Remember that once you become a manager, you'll stop doing what made you successful. You'll stop doing the things you do very well and dedicate yourself to watching how others do them and helping them improve."

Managing teams takes us beyond design, at least the craft of designing. Being a manager/Lead/Director means understanding people, developing skills that allow us to understand people's psychology, but not to apply it to product or service design—rather to team design. A manager's job is basically making things happen and ensuring team members can do their work with as little friction as possible.

4. "Because if someone below you does something spectacular, that shows the company you've built a great team."

And it's not about having the spotlight, at least not in terms of being a product designer. It's about being a platform, literally, for other people to have the spotlight and develop.

It can be overwhelming to deal with company politics, to maintain the complete picture to help others connect dots. There's even certain bureaucratic work I don't think many managers love, but it's part of the role. There's another kind of satisfaction when you see people's growth, your team's growth, and it's different from designing something and seeing it live, but it's not for everyone, and that's okay.

Self-knowledge is key to understanding if we're made for leading. When we have clarity, it's easy to understand what we need to work on and improve to reach leadership roles. If our conclusion is that it's not for us, it also makes us aware of our place on the team. The best thing a manager can ask for is individual contributors who are catalysts, who are ambassadors and anchor points to foster team culture and follow best practices.

See you in the next edition.

To read👀

Is the New Deal for business leading by design?

This great article from Shortcut By Figma has 5 very interesting perspectives on when companies are "design-led." 5 opinions on the mindset design should have in organizations when they have a seat at the executive table.

To listen to… and watch 🎧
Special announcement. 💎💎💎

At The UX Rebels we're going to give away tickets to the DesignOps Latam event. If you have no idea what DesignOps is or you've heard of it but don't know how it works, here's the episode we did on YouTube with Alexandra Mengoni who literally gave us a masterclass on DesignOps.

item.title: Documentary: RIP! A Remix Manifesto
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: A few days ago, I responded to a post that claimed design is no longer design, that standardization has killed the space for creativity. And here I explain better why I don't believe that…
item.content:encoded:
2# Functionality: the key to defining what design is.

Design has a functional purpose, and that helps us understand how it differs from other types of creative activities.

Ed. 52

A few days ago, I responded to a post that claimed design is no longer design, that standardization has killed the space for creativity. And here I explain better why I don't believe that…

Those of us who studied at art schools, graphic design, visual or graphic communication, etc. tend to confuse creation processes with the design process. In school, when experimenting with exercises meant to give us technical skills (from making a charcoal drawing, to digital illustration, collage, and even animation and motion exercises) these exercises were about understanding the characteristics of materials and technique, not about developing something framed as design. Students end up developing their own ideas that become a reflection of themselves, a creation process used as a pretext to develop technical skills.

And it happens that many during this formative stage begin to identify more with this process, either from a genuine impulse to express themselves, or because it's easier to make personal decisions disguised under the title of design process.

As teachers, we tend to overlook making these clarifications, either because our subject doesn't aim to use a design frame but rather to learn how to use tools and/or materials, or because like our students we don't see a difference between creating and designing.

For graphic designers, not all, but for many, there's a dilemma between prioritizing function and aesthetics. The history of graphic design in various parts of the world shows us cycles where, in trying to communicate messages more effectively, we go from pure functionalism to authorial design all the way to non-readability, where whether the message is understood matters less than how it looks.

And I find it curious because, in other disciplines like industrial design, we can see similar cycles, where we want to break away from a certain level of standardization toward authorial design; the functionality of these designs never disappears and when it does, it transforms into something else, but not into design.

In architecture we can even talk about authorial architecture, but never disconnected from functionality….

Or ask Frank Gehry about his Guggenheim in Bilbao…

Although Gehry considers architecture to be Art and that, once finished, a building should be considered a work of art. Despite this, he keeps his feet on the ground with functional designs that integrate into their environment.

I don't see architects or industrial designers complaining about standards and the limitations that making something functional and feasible implies. But I have seen many graphic designers stuck in the discourse of "this is boring, the constraints of being functional and feasible take away my creativity."

I saw those debates when designing book covers, magazines, and music CDs, and now I see them when people talk about UI, UX design patterns, and design systems. We fall again into the error of thinking that aesthetics is above function, when in reality aesthetics is an attribute that accompanies function to achieve its objective.

Design is what happens when you take constraints to obtain the best possible solution. There is no design when the page is blank and without rules.

Innovation doesn't emerge spontaneously, but is an analytical process that connects what came before us and allows us to find new forms, and that my dear readers, is also creativity. Creating more efficient processes that give us standards and design patterns that are recognizable and therefore feel intuitive is also creativity.

When you understand this you realize that being original is a myth, as Brett Gaylor says in his documentary RIP! A Remix Manifesto.

So I don't believe that web standards kill creativity on the internet. Not thinking about efficiency and profitability in 2023 is wanting to do design blindly without thinking about its function.

And I believe there's also space for authorial or experimental design on the web, but assuming that's the only path to being creative is another bias.

When design has no function, it stops being design and can become anything but design.

See you in the next edition

Para ver 👀
Para escuchar 🎧

Complete- Xander and Søren Søstrom

item.title: On When Graphic Designers Hijacked the UX Industry
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: Many old-guard UX Designers, or those who don't come from a visual background, have expressed the idea that when visual and graphic designers entered the industry, it was hijacked by designers focused only on UI, who without understanding much about the UCD and UX process jumped into designing solut…
item.content:encoded:
My explanation of why we as visual designers have a place in the UX industry and the challenges we must face.

Ed. 53

Many old-guard UX Designers, or those who don't come from a visual background, have expressed the idea that when visual and graphic designers entered the industry, it was hijacked by designers focused only on UI, who without understanding much about the UCD and UX process jumped into designing solutions that appealed more to emotion and aesthetics than to fully solving a problem.

The more radical ones tell themselves the story that UI designers ruined the UX industry, and nostalgically, they talk about how the golden age of UX is over, because the original UX designers (those who do "pure UX," not UI, according to them) have seen their work reduced, UX design is no longer what it used to be…

While it's true there was a significant migration of graphic designers and visual artists into the UX industry, I believe this responds to the demand for this type of profile, the reduction of marketing budgets and the decline in using creative agencies as third parties to develop products.

Also, companies started years ago to centralize design practice internally, and agencies that still provide consulting services had to change their approach and frameworks, in an environment where metrics are what counts.

Those of us who've been in UX for a while and come from graphic design and visual communication schools, which by the way also come from visual arts, could see how many colleagues went from Visual Designer or Art Director to calling themselves UX/UI Designers without understanding much about what this new area was about—there was demand for these profiles, and it paid better.

Perhaps all this led to this great exodus of visual designers (graphic designers, visual communicators, and even people from architecture and industrial design and engineering who had an affinity for the visual).

You can see this wasn't something spontaneous. Reducing it to a simple hijacking by visually trained designers oversimplifies what really happened, and this points to the risk of not understanding the true problems and challenges this entails.

Everything Changed After Aqua

In early 2000, Apple presented MacOS X with its new GUI (Graphic User Interface) called Aqua, with the idea of "incorporating color, depth, translucency, and visually appealing complex textures." Graphic systems had been using CRT monitors for years, which besides being able to cause cancer, had better definition with millions of colors and resolutions that today might seem small, but at the time were top of the line.

The goal was to squeeze value from the new capabilities of computing systems. Aqua was a complete visual system for interfaces that began doing this, incorporating textures and materials that resembled the real world—you could see a trash bin icon for deleting files with a very realistic metal texture.

Having the ability to add more details to graphical interfaces meant we needed professionals who specialized in the visual part of design. Not that they didn't exist before—just remember designer Susan Kane with her memorable icons for the original MacOS of 1984.

You can see how the way we represent GUIs goes hand in hand with the capabilities of computing systems, at least up to this point. No folks, it wasn't that Susan Kane was a fan of minimalism and using only one color to design icons—it's that the original 1984 Mac had a monochrome monitor with a resolution of 512 × 342 pixels.

With Aqua, the desktop metaphor in GUIs required specialists and more complex tools. Interfaces became more sophisticated, visually more appealing and realistic. Skeuomorphism at its peak. But let's be clear—this type of design had a clear strategy of making digital products much more approachable, and not just aesthetic purposes.

Design cycles have shown us that once an idea is familiar we can start removing what's extra and keep only what's essential. Today skeuomorphism has stopped being used in operating systems like Windows, Linux, MacOS, Android, and iOS, which have opted for a flatter visual style. But we can still see examples of skeuomorphism in music software, although the trend is toward flat. As always, it's all about the problem, objectives, and context.

Without a doubt, apps transformed communication media and the way of doing business in the digital era in many ways. The demand for more specialized profiles prompted the entry of more disciplines into the process of creating digital products and services. We have to say it—that transition in many cases meant wanting to do a cut and paste of the processes these profiles had in their previous industries (with all the bad and good habits this implied), added to the scarce offering of formal training, which made these profiles integrate clumsily into the UX industry.

Personally, I see the discussion of pure UX as sterile, where UI is seen as something external to UX. It's hard to think of processes where UX design is done by a single person in 2023, and where that UX design (as a process) doesn't contemplate some type of interface (and I don't mean just graphical) and its implementation.

In startups it's common to see teams with a single designer, but it's also well known that this is rarely sustainable long-term. And the way I see it, a UX generalist must at least have a foundation in what UI design implies—refusing that is not understanding that the industry changed. It's not 1990 anymore.

That said, it'll be nearly impossible to be a unicorn who does and knows in detail all the processes and frameworks and tools to make a digital product and service from strategy to production. The ideal will always be role specialization, but everyone is covered under UX. The debate between what a role implies and what a process implies doesn't make sense—they're different.

Now, for those coming from other areas, and especially my visual colleagues, it's a duty to understand in general terms what your place is in the process and where the raw material of your work comes from and where it goes once your part is done. If you weren't doing this in your previous way of working, that's the first thing to learn.

When I worked making magazines, I got to do it from various roles—prepress, design, and even managing the printing budget. For every designer who worked in print, it was necessary to understand how the machines you're going to print on work, how you need to send the files so they're printed in the best way. You had to understand how the machine took the sheets of paper to understand the page order, which generally wasn't linear—meaning, you needed to understand in general terms the process you were immersed in to avoid rework and predictable errors. To break the rules, push, and innovate, you must know what precedes you.

We tend to quickly see the similarities when we design UI with what we did before, but also, it's harder for us to understand the differences that are crucial to delivering value effectively. That's the part you'll have to learn—where you must stay alert and open to unlearning and learning again.

As visual designers we've perpetuated myths about creativity and intuition, and along the way we forgot that as designers we've always had a process as the backbone of our work—and we've forgotten because in some cases design merges with the author's emotionality (this for me is no longer design as such, but it does happen).

UX as a discipline has a different process. When you understand those differences and its approach, you'll understand that at its core, it's about users and solving their problems. Design is just a means that converges with other specialties to deliver value, but it's not the center of the equation nor is it about what you want to express.

The Challenges for UIs of Us in the Industry

We need, as visual designers, to make design a more rational and conscious labor and less magical. Design in silos and with random results makes no sense in a hyperconnected world, full of data and where geographic borders are now almost invisible.

Only then will we leave behind stories where hard-core pure UX has been hijacked by the UI designer who doesn't understand UX and just rides on making aesthetic things.

Because…

Genuine UI is inserted in a UX process. Without this, UI is not UI—it's anything but UI. Because UI appeals to being intuitive by understanding and knowing users' contexts. It's not spontaneous nor is it a consequence of what the designer wants to express. It's preceded by continuous analysis of information and reflection. There's no blank canvas when doing UI—there's a toolkit that will allow us to make decisions to create.

The industry changed and transformed, and along the way demanded another class of skills, more aligned with visual "producers." But entering this industry obliges us to understand and apply the UX process, understanding our role and impact in the production chain.

See you in the next edition.

Before we finish, a small announcement:

I'm planning the launch of a design systems course focused more on the strategic and foundational part. It would help me a lot if you're interested to fill out this survey (https://gforms.app/r/jCiumgO) and help me understand the needs of potential participants.

To read 👀

4 design principles I use every day to avoid bad UX and create products that work for everyone

To listen 🎧

Sab Hop - Sabino

For my purists….

item.title: For 2024
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: It's been a little over two months since the last edition of this newsletter. Several health problems in my family in November made December a month for catching up on pending things, and aside from video editing for the podcast and some interactions on social media, the truth is I preferred to rech…
item.content:encoded:
The questions I'm carrying with me to be resolved in the coming year.

Ed. 54

It's been a little over two months since the last edition of this newsletter. Several health problems in my family in November made December a month for catching up on pending things, and aside from video editing for the podcast and some interactions on social media, the truth is I preferred to recharge and disconnect as much as possible from digital life.

It's true that the worries of the previous months didn't leave much space for reflection and writing either, but now, with those issues resolved, my ideas are coming back—though with open questions more than concrete positions about the future, about the meaning of design, about the confirmation biases that can amplify the mirage of feeling like we see the whole picture when we're really just staring at our own navels.

The challenges our work might face from technology that's disrupting how we conceive the creative process, and how we interact with "machines," makes me, for now, force myself to stay informed and try to preview the next step.

My own nature makes me confront paradigm shifts where we now write what we want to do instead of manipulating elements in our interface more precisely. For me it's easier to manipulate a digital knob or slider to precisely edit a selected element with my hands (fingers, pen, or mouse pointer) than to write what I want AI (Artificial Intelligence) to do. I'm not saying one is better than the other, it's simply a paradigm shift—perhaps the new reality we need to get used to and one that will keep evolving, and this is maybe what we'll talk about in 2024.

On the other hand, another part of me wants to see where these technologies evolve in 2024, AIs for images and video, that through prompts today create things. People aren't becoming Prompt Designers, but prompts are becoming sophisticated, pointing toward the birth of a discipline called Prompt Engineer, to the point that companies are starting to create guides and recommendations for writing them. The most interesting thing I'd like to see, as Taramona points out, would be to see the first BCIs (Brain Computer Interface) as gadgets, an AI Pin you don't talk to but communicate with through thoughts—I want to stay on the lookout for all of this.

Understanding this I think will help me understand where interface design, interaction, and UX are heading, and even if we need to rethink definitions, scopes, and applications, as well as our work as designers.

I told you there are more questions than certainties, but answering them gives me a certain excitement… Let's see where all this points…

Best for 2024.

PS. Send your recommendations for Design, Technology, and Science Fiction books I should read for 2024, send messages, the worst that can happen is I already know the book but need to put it on the list because I haven't read it yet thanks.

To read 👀

On Persuasion. Good design requires persuasion

To listen 🎧

The Resistance (The Creator)

If you have Star Plus, watch this movie, really good.

item.title: InVision: The End of an Era of Design Audiovisual Content.
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: A few days ago we learned that InVision is shutting down and for many nostalgics (myself included), memories came flooding back; because many of us used that tool at some point.
item.content:encoded:
Its closure brings nostalgia, not for the product, but for all the content that surrounded it.

Ed 55

A few days ago we learned that InVision is shutting down and for many nostalgics (myself included), memories came flooding back; because many of us used that tool at some point.

I would say without doubt it was used by many "Top Companies." High-quality prototypes, cloud-based, not too far from what Figma can do today. The perfect combo, at least in my case, when paired with Sketch.

Almost 7 years ago, there seemed to be a bright future when InVision launched its desktop app called InVision Studio, offering great quality and prototype settings with incredible motions.

It seemed like InVision was challenging the status quo of what Adobe XD and Sketch couldn't do, an all-in-one solution. Unfortunately, many of the features announced would come in future versions that never arrived.

The lack of updates, combined with the fact that in the same year Figma in its version 2.0 integrated prototyping and handoff functionalities. These made InVision lose relevance. The market and competitors were changing and evolving in the services they delivered. InVision's closure was a death foretold.

Curiously, it seems their marketing machine was going full force, and that was largely what made them grow. What started as an inbound marketing strategy with a blog or resources like UI kits, later led them to create documentaries like "Design Disruptors"

A documentary about the role of design that was announced with trailers, released with screenings in some theaters in the United States. In Mexico City, 23Design (now Frog) organized a couple of screenings at Cine Tonalá, and in the design industry it was quite an event.

This was followed by "The Loop" about how IBM adopted Design Thinking. "Squads" about collaboration and product design team topics, and "Transformation by Design" to talk about digital transformation.

The miniseries Design Systems: Mastering Design at Scale with Brad Frost, Josh Clark, and Dan Mall has a great production level. (Dan Mall uploaded the series to his YouTube channel)

But they didn't stop there. They founded DesignBetter.co (Now the domain redirects to the podcast hosted on Substack) led by Aaron Walter with reports like The New Design Frontier about the "State of Art" of design in the tech industry. They edited several free ebooks about Design Systems, Handoff, Leadership, Business Thinking, Product Design Principles, among others.

One of the things that keeps me curious and perhaps worried is that all these materials might get lost. The books stopped being available and the DesignBetter site only redirects to the podcast on Substack. And except for Design Disruptors, for now, the other documentaries are no longer available.

Although the knowledge in these pieces may become outdated, I believe that as pieces, they encapsulate a lot of what has happened in the design industry and especially in UX, for better or worse.

InVision marked an era, not because of the tool itself, but because of what surrounded it. Yes, they weren't the only ones with a similar strategy, UXPin has a hundred free books, but they definitely stood out with multimedia materials of exceptional quality.

In the end, as a product it stopped being what it promised. InVision is closing for not adapting to market needs at the pace required. Perhaps creating so many materials around it led them to lose focus on designing a better product.

The best marketing is the one that doesn't make you sell, but show. The one where the product speaks for itself. Many designers remember the references that InVision's marketing gave us, but many of them long, long ago stopped using it when they found more efficient tools.

In the history of tools (not just design tools), the best tools don't win. We don't always use the tools we'd like to. We use the tools that are industry standard and allow us to collaborate with others.

I'm not saying InVision is the great tool that lost a battle, but in many cases we started using it because it became a kind of standard in many workplaces, like Photoshop, Sketch, or Figma have been. In the end they're tools, which at their core are a business. Let's not fall into the bias of defining ourselves by the tools we use and the statements they want to attribute to themselves.

History has taught us that the interests behind these tools can turn them, without hesitation, into what they promised to destroy…

So we should always keep an openness to the idea that things can be done differently, and to some extent remain tool-agnostic (it's hard, though).

Anyway, it's always sad to learn a company is closing, because it's about people losing their jobs.

See you in the next edition….

To read 👀

The Creative Act: A Way Of Being.

I haven't read many articles during the holidays. Actually I've tried to stay as disconnected as possible. I'd been wanting to read this book since it came out. The truth is they're short chapters where Rick Rubin reflects on creativity, in all its breadth. I recommend it.

item.title: Design Systems and the Challenges of Collaboration
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: The advances in visual creation that artificial intelligence is offering pose a paradigm shift for the designer as craftsman, changing their way of working in a very radical way. Perhaps it's much more appropriate to talk about the designer as curator or as a decision factor facing an Artificial Int…
item.content:encoded:
Building and maintaining a design system means knowing how to transfer its value to the organization, not just to designers.

Ed.56

The advances in visual creation that artificial intelligence is offering pose a paradigm shift for the designer as craftsman, changing their way of working in a very radical way.
