Perhaps it's much more appropriate to talk about the designer as curator or as a decision factor facing an Artificial Intelligence that still lacks its own judgment and limits itself to making multiple variations in response to the user's prompt.

These scenarios make us understand that we need to redefine the role of the designer who sits in the second part of the double diamond, those who implement the strategy. But also, in many cases, to look back and rediscover that the intellectual work of designing can never be condensed into just the hours we spend in front of the computer pushing pixels.

Subscribe and don't miss the next edition in your inbox.

If the creation of components for a Design System becomes automated and has less human intervention, this shift pushes us to have or develop strategic tools that allow us to carry out the operation of a design system.

Promoting and selling a Design System solely for visual consistency is insufficient. We need to be clear that visual consistency or component reuse are just a means to impact more concrete organizational objectives; for example, budget savings by creating more efficient processes; this includes delivering features to market faster, increasing market fit and with it the organization's profitability.

We must understand that a Design System is not a design strategy or initiative, but one for the entire organization.

In scenarios where there's already a live product, stopping everything to build a Design System that will then be used by our product teams is unviable. That's why we need to understand how we can create spaces where we can prove the effectiveness of a design system, gaining the organization's trust, and progressively get our Design System adopted—not just by designers, but by product teams that include developers.

Avoid vanity metrics, because it's not about how many teams adopt the design system, but about connecting how adoption results in a much more efficient process in time, and therefore, in costs.

I'm a bit worried about the tool-itis in the Design Systems spectrum—not because I think it's not an important topic. Tools like Figma have democratized access to processes that were previously only possible in development environments, encouraging collaboration between PMs, Stakeholders, Developers and Designers, becoming a tool that in many cases functions as a source of truth.

But this very point tends to bias us, especially the younger and more fanatical among us. Being a source of truth, we think that design activity starts and ends in the tool, forgetting that our job is to deliver value where design is a means and not an end. Design artifacts should be evaluated by their contribution to the design process and not by their quality; we treat our work as if it were the product itself, when in reality it's an enabler whose objective is to communicate the how of building "the real product"—which is not our Figma file.

If artificial intelligence automates the technical part of Design Systems, then we need to focus less on "how to do it" and more on "doing the right thing," and this is the part that's talked about least in Design Systems.

If what you read makes sense to you, these are exactly the things I aim to cover in my course Design Systems: fundamentals, strategies and management to create positive impact in organizations. After several mentorship sessions, the questions that come up have more to do with how to negotiate and convince, plan and organize our work in Design Systems with other people, than how to make components in Figma.

The course starts February 26, it's 4 sessions (two weeks). LinkedIn

See you in the next edition.

To read 👀

Design system myths

by Nathan Curtis
To listen 🎧

Tiempo - Alba y Tiempo

item.title: Sharing what you know makes you a better designer
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: Hi, I've been a bit absent because 2024 is a year where I'll be teaching several courses, so because of the planning involved I've prioritized reading and rereading a lot of material related to Design Systems, Interface Design, Interaction, and Prototyping. I must confess I've had to put my urge to …
item.content:encoded:
Because when one teaches, two learn.

Ed.57

Hi, I've been a bit absent because 2024 is a year where I'll be teaching several courses, so because of the planning involved I've prioritized reading and rereading a lot of material related to Design Systems, Interface Design, Interaction, and Prototyping. I must confess I've had to put my urge to write on pause. But here we are.

In 2012 I started as a university professor. I learned the difference between knowing something and knowing how to explain it. For about 4 years I ventured into teaching, and I discovered I really enjoyed sharing what I know.

I remember very clearly those moments in class when looks of astonishment appeared on my students' faces. I think it's similar to reading a book and suddenly the author connects dots you never connected, and the feeling is: of course! It was always there and I never saw it.

Subscribe so you don't miss the next edition.

After discovering how much I enjoyed sharing what I know, I realized that when a topic wasn't coming easy, it was simpler if during the learning process I asked myself: if I had to explain this, how would I do it?

When I do this exercise, I do a mental review of what I know and naturally structure the information. But I also get to see the gaps—the things that don't connect, where I basically need more information to define them and connect them with everything else.

So yes! Sharing what I know helps me better understand what I'm studying and learning. Of course, you can't always count on having someone willing to listen or interested in helping you review your ideas. That's why writing is another method that lets us understand the logic of our thinking. Writing to share something is an act of empathy in itself. You don't write for yourself, you write so the potential reader can have a transfer of what you know. But at the same time, you do it putting yourself in their shoes, so that even if only by a small percentage, they can understand the world the way you do.

The way to positively impact our design industry is understanding that we're on an infinite staircase. We can always learn more and climb one step. There will always be people who know more than us because they're steps ahead. But there's always people behind you, who just started this learning journey. Share what you know not for the steps above but for the steps that follow you. No matter your level, if you have something to share—do it!

We need more people willing to share what they know, and not from the step of power and authority, but from humility and with a lifelong learner approach. Because another thing I learned from teaching is that one person teaches, but two people learn.

Of course, I'm not saying you need to become professors now, because you even need to learn about didactics to plan a class. What I'm trying to say is be aware that you can share with your close circle, and this will help you reinforce what you know, and you'll positively impact others by making things clearer for them.

See you in the next edition. I promise not to take too long.

To watch 👀

How to start a personal journal?

Speaking of sharing, my great friend Benjamín García just opened a YouTube channel called Vista360, where he wants to talk about things he likes—because there are many—that don't make as much sense in The UX Rebels.

In this video he talks about his experience keeping a journal, and I'm a faithful witness that he's deep into this topic and quite advanced. I learned a lot from the video.

Without a doubt, this level of production raises the bar for what we want to do for the podcast in season 5.

To listen 🎧

Default by Keinseier

item.title: Design needs a premise
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: And there we are, in that moment of initiation, where we only have the blank page, we close our eyes as if our interior could give us solutions, we breathe and contemplate the blank page, and we freeze… the blank page moment paralyzes us.
item.content:encoded:
Design execution is never spontaneous or arbitrary.

Ed. 58

And there we are, in that moment of initiation, where we only have the blank page, we close our eyes as if our interior could give us solutions, we breathe and contemplate the blank page, and we freeze… the blank page moment paralyzes us.

Without a doubt this happened to me in my youth, the moment I called the blank page caused me a lot of stress, many times I was able to overcome that creative block, but in those years I was looking for ways to make ideas arrive quickly and spontaneously, as if suddenly my brain would connect with the imaginary and throw out a solution that, by the way, I hoped would be surprising.

Years later, I discovered that creative block has more to do with not having clarity on the constraints of what we're trying to solve and at the same time little clarity on what we have as an objective with what we design. I'm not saying that arriving at a solution is easier, because it never has been. I'm talking about no longer feeling that stress of not knowing what to do, or at least what strategy I should take.

The idea is simple: design execution starts from a premise, that's why in the first part of the double diamond, the objective is not to create a solution but to understand the problem precisely, that is the premise we need to execute design, if this premise is not clear, if it's not assimilated by us we'll tend to go in circles, either because we're not focusing on the real problem, because we don't understand the magnitude of the problem or because we don't know what to focus on.

It's a matter of looking at the premise and trying to answer some of the following questions,

  • What do I want to achieve with what I design?
  • Who is it intended for?
  • Am I achieving it in the best way?
  • Are there other ways to achieve it?

It may seem obvious, but we can feel overwhelmed, forgetting that we can start defining things from defining what they are not, the questions above won't give us concrete solutions, but they are a framework that helps us understand what our solutions cannot be, we shouldn't explore in the "undefined" and we must concentrate on the limited universe of what remains

And of course, constraints like time and budget help even more to shape the solutions, to filter them, we must understand that the best solution doesn't win, but the most feasible one. The one that happens between what we aspire to and what we can build.

Answering the previous questions gives us the premise we use to outline solutions, it doesn't make the path shorter, but precisely makes us see a path; and not just an open field of infinite possibilities that overwhelms and freezes us.

So now we can say that creative block is a non-comprehension of the premise that design execution needs, the explorations will be more fortuitous, better understood and clearer. The solution is there, it has always been there, we just have to reach it.

See you in the next edition

Para leer 👀

How to Scope Work.

This article by Dan Mall gives us an idea of how to understand and plan scope when designing, breaking tasks into smaller tasks that let us easily see if the whole plan aligns with the scope.

Para escuchar 🎧

Cardano Circles - Max Cooper

item.title: Passion and a taste for UX.
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: I remember that day very well. I was filling out the paperwork to study at university, and my first choice was Design and Visual Communication. My brother, 10 years older, told me Design wasn't that hard because it didn't have courses with many credits. He's an actuary, and at the time he was tellin…
item.content:encoded:
If you're only here because you bought into the story that it pays well, you can turn back — it takes more than just a bootcamp to build a career

Ed. 59

I remember that day very well. I was filling out the paperwork to study at university, and my first choice was Design and Visual Communication. My brother, 10 years older, told me Design wasn't that hard because it didn't have courses with many credits. He's an actuary, and at the time he was telling me he had courses worth 18 credits. What I discovered later is that unlike actuarial science, where the average is 6 courses, in Design and Visual Communication you take 12 courses in some semesters.

Thanks to my brother's comment that hit my teenage ego, I picked Applied Mathematics and Computing as my second choice, confident I'd get into my first option, and if not, I'd surely like it — math wasn't hard for me and I liked technology...

I studied Design and Visual Communication and the rest is history. Sometimes I wonder: What would have become of me if I'd ended up in my second choice? Would I have continued or dropped out? Would I have found a way to connect what I know about Design with something visual?

Although university was a great time, the reality is that at least the first two years weren't easy. I could see how some people had a technical advantage over me. They had experience with painting materials I was trying for the first time, a command of human anatomy in drawing classes, and some had already dabbled in Photoshop.

I was aware of this disadvantage, but it was never an obstacle. On the contrary, many of my classmates inspired me to be clear about what skills I needed to develop and surpass. It was just about having patience and discipline.

Everything was clear because Design was my passion. I equally enjoyed courses where we drew with pastels, where we learned about typography, where we understood visual composition or projected geometric plans — and don't even get me started on when I turned on a Mac G3 to use PageMaker, Illustrator, or Photoshop.

This was between 2001 and 2005, of course, when the idea of UX was nowhere to be found. We used Macromedia Flash and our designs were distributed on interactive CDs. The only clear thing is that I wanted to do design, not because it paid well, not because it was trendy, simply because I was passionate about it. It was easy to understand that when something truly engages you, you become obsessed, you breathe it, you study and practice much more and you go beyond the standard.

I'm writing this because many people are or have migrated to the UX industry, biased only by the goal that it pays well and is relatively easy. It seems like it's more about what can I train in and succeed, rather than what do I like, what am I good at, or what interests me — and from there, understanding how I can build a career.

If we don't know ourselves, it will be very hard to understand what we're passionate about, what we're good at, or what we aspire to

When I had my first encounters with UX, I was seduced by the idea of being part of designing tools that people use in their daily lives. I fell in love with the practical and utilitarian perspective of UI design, because it wasn't about what I wanted to express — but about how to express what the user expected. By 2010 it was easy to see that this was fertile ground where Visual Design was just starting to get involved, but what motivated me to get closer and learn more about UX was that I liked it, that it made me curious, and that was always the drive.

I find it hard to understand that people select what to dedicate themselves to only based on the idea that they'll do better in that field. I find it very hard to separate my work from something I like and enjoy doing every day. Maybe that's where the resilience you need comes from — to keep going without doubting, without getting desperate, and to look for how it can be done.

So if you're a recent bootcamp graduate, first, discover if this is what you're passionate about, and you weren't just prey to misleading Marketing, because it's not going to be easy.

If you are passionate about this, understand that you need to learn a lot. Have a self-taught approach. Today there's more information than there was 15 years ago. Learn English (in the meantime use Google Translate for the articles you need to devour). There's a world of UX outside LATAM and Hispanic America, and English is a springboard to those opportunities.

Practice, create fictional cases, but where you try to apply what you're learning in a real way. Look for mentors, but understand it's not just about asking questions — it's about asking after doing prior research and having specific doubts. And if you can, keep training (do good research to understand the best cost-benefit options).

Develop soft skills. Half the job is doing design, the other half is communicating it. Be eloquent, and always reflect on the clarity of your thinking, your process, and how you arrive at solutions. If you can't explain it, it's because you haven't fully understood it.

And if you're not good at public speaking, practice! I've experienced firsthand how a good idea can be shot down by a poor way of communicating it.

Scott Berkun says product designers spend more time socializing their ideas and convincing people than executing them. He lists the following skills as essential:

Persuasion: the ability to convince decision makers. Negotiation: how to make deals and compromises. Facilitation: leading conversations between different roles. Source

It's not an easy path. Now this can give you an idea of what you're missing and why some people earn the salaries they earn. It's not just about using Figma or saying you know UX or Design Thinking — it goes beyond that.

Add to this the idea that artificial intelligence will change the way we work in ways we don't yet fully understand, but it will. So you need this to be your passion and something you enjoy, so you truly want to adapt to the continuous changes.

See you in the next edition.

To read 👀

What is a Product?

Sol Mesz brings us this interesting article that attempts to define what a product is: "Ultimately, the point of this strategic definition of product is that we can't think about product without thinking about the business, in the same way we can't think about the business without thinking about users."

To listen 🎧

Icarus - Tony Ann

item.title: Demystifying UX Industry Facts.
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: In the design world, you often hear the phrase "UX is not UI." I've already written my position on this. The distinction works well for understanding roles, delimiting responsibilities, and even skills. But it's no longer convenient in a strict sense to view UI as something external to the UX proces…
item.content:encoded:
Our discipline is constantly changing — our concept definitions should be too.

Ed. 60.

In the design world, you often hear the phrase "UX is not UI." I've already written my position on this. The distinction works well for understanding roles, delimiting responsibilities, and even skills. But it's no longer convenient in a strict sense to view UI as something external to the UX process itself.

Good UI is embedded in a UX process — there are clear premises and we're focused on users. I don't think UI can be designed without thinking about concepts like usability, consistency, system response. In the same way, we can't claim we're doing UX without taking users into account.

But then, this leads me to reflect on the definitions of the concepts we currently have. We live in an industry and discipline of constant change. We should have the same openness when it comes to updating definitions and adapting them to current realities — not just assume them as facts and immovable definitions.

The industry tends to over-simplify concepts and use them interchangeably. So we have GUI, a specific type of interface regularly used in software. This means people lose sight of other types of UI that exist — voice interfaces, natural language interfaces, and even interfaces for systems that don't have digital support. Basically, a UI is what allows us to interact with machines of any kind.

The industry swallowed the term UX to refer mostly to products or services that ended with a digital deliverable. But experience is also designed in physical products and environments, like in industrial design and architecture.

And yes, with this we can see very simply that not all UX ends in a UI. If we appropriately extend the concept of UI, in many non-digital cases there can be UI — but not in all cases. We shouldn't fall into generalizations.

UI is part of UX, but it's not a condition for UX to be UX. There can be UX without UI, but there cannot be UI without UX.

The "UX is not UI" dichotomy oversimplifies a complex and nuanced relationship.

We must recognize the evolution of these concepts and their relationship to design better solutions. We shouldn't have UI designers who have no idea about UX (who then shouldn't even call themselves UI designers), nor strategic designers who, by separating UI from UX, have no notion or understanding of what it means to create solutions that contain UI.

Again, having notions and understanding doesn't necessarily mean we'll do it ourselves or that our role should involve those activities. Having notions means we understand our place in the value chain — we understand where things come from before they reach us and where they go after we finish our part. This creates a much more effective collaboration environment and allows us to be more successful in service design.

By recognizing the evolution of these concepts and their interdependence, we can design better products and services that meet user needs at all levels. The next time you think about UX and UI, remember that both are essential components of the same successful user experience.

See you in the next edition.

To read 👀

Defensible Visual Design

An interesting list of points for defending our design. Understanding the contexts of places, we can use this to strategically defend our designs better.

To listen 🎧

Amuse - Notre Dame

item.title: Figma and AI: What Are We Really Looking For?
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: A little over a week since Figma's Config Event took place. It's curious that although it's a forum for reflecting on the state of the industry, best practices in digital product design, and new paradigms in design like artificial intelligence or design system scaling, the Hype centered on the openi…
item.content:encoded:
Artificial Intelligence can catalyze the Design process, but it seems what we're looking for are "magic" solutions

Ed. 61.

A little over a week since Figma's Config Event took place. It's curious that although it's a forum for reflecting on the state of the industry, best practices in digital product design, and new paradigms in design like artificial intelligence or design system scaling, the Hype centered on the opening Keynote with the tool's new features. Figma finally adds Artificial Intelligence (AI) functionalities for interface creation.

Some point out that the UI Designer role is going to disappear, the more radical ones talk about designers now being forced to leave the industry and become strategic. The more optimistic see opportunities to catalyze faster or deeper ideation processes.

As if Figma fell prey to hype and FOMO. The collapse of the Adobe acquisition meant Dylan Field's company would have to find a way to jump on the AI bandwagon somewhat on its own, unable to leverage the arm Adobe has built. The new AI functionality is presented as a magic solution we can ask to design things for us.

Now the statement centers on we can create websites and digital products in minutes, AI is sold as what delivers final digital products, and not as the arm that puts steroids into designers' processes: for me, that's where the error lies.

AI is what catalyzes the Design process, not what executes the Design process.

I've said it in other editions, picking up the idea that Omar Mendoza told us in the first episode of the Third season of the UX Rebels: knowing how to use design software, without understanding the design process, inevitably leads us to become software operators; not designers.

If we transfer design execution to what AI delivers as a final deliverable, without filtering, we'll become less than operators and our role won't be necessary. If the only thing I do is press a button labeled "Made by AI" then we can be absorbed by process automation.

I say this after reading the post from Andy Allen, creator of Notboring.software, who comments this on X, about what Figma produces when asking for a weather app, an obvious reference to the iOS weather app

Dylan Field explains that for now they're working on improving the functionality, they've decided to remove it from beta, and although he's stated they use language models available in the market, and that the training of said models has been with work they themselves have commissioned, the obvious result right now shows very little variation.

Beyond legal issues, and having certainty about whether Figma would use user permission in the future to train their models, which isn't far-fetched, for me the reflection goes a different way.

What kind of response do we expect from a prompt asking for a weather application? Would Dribbble-style designs be enough to feel this AI is intelligent and thinks outside the box? Is visual differentiation that might impact interaction itself an innovative idea? What process is the AI following and what context is it taking into account to reach these solutions?

The design process isn't magic and it's not a recipe that lets us replicate the same result in different contexts. Why do we want to put a black box between understanding the problem and the solution? Just because we have FOMO and AI sells?

The advantages of making a more complete ideation process, in less time, are clear, but in the ideation process, visual explorations are not the solution themselves, but the path to finding it. When having a convergence process in ideation, we seek to contrast explorations with technical limitations, costs, timelines, and even cognitive load so users can adopt and learn new patterns. Understanding what things we can challenge and what things should be part of the solution itself.

And I'm not saying current LLMs don't work like this right now, the prompt itself is the medium to integrate context and limitations of what we ask artificial intelligences. What bothers me is that Figma used the statement of just asking for design solutions magically, oversimplifying what's taken into account when executing design.

Can we create an abyss of people without craft and without expertise?

The problem with using the magic solutions approach, at its core, is assuming AI will only give correct answers, which directly leads to the designer's role as executor disappearing.

Even with this premise, at the end of the road I find it hard to see humans ceasing to be a decision factor, perhaps not the executor, but the one who decides, which can put strategic designers without design execution skills in check. Without craft, there's no way to develop expertise that allows us to make decisions from what AI offers us. If the executor role disappears, the most natural thing is for strategic designers to have to develop these skills, and the question is Are all of them willing to do it?

And although I know authors like Jakob Nielsen bet on models like generative UI, or David Hoang and his idea of Dynamic UI, where AI itself has extreme levels of personalization and iterates and improves automatically. Cost-wise it sounds super efficient, but it doesn't clarify the scenario and role the designer would take in these kinds of scenarios.

But let's see what happens. We don't need to know the future to build it, we need to understand the present with its pros and cons to build the paths toward the future we want and use past experience in favor of making the best long-term decisions.

See you in the next edition. I promise I'll start writing more often.

Para ver pt. 1 👀

The Fascinating History of the AI Game of Thrones

Gustavo Entrala narrates like no other how the battle to develop current AI models has unfolded. An unmissable video to understand the commercial context around this topic in the West

Para ver pt. 2 🎧

The broken promises of design system

My friend Jay Lopez shared on LinkedIn this talk from Config 2024 that left me with a great reflection on what speed, consistency, and quality in a design system implies. Sometimes we're not aware of the costs of taking this radically and without self-criticism.

item.title: 2024 Retrospective
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: The truth is I've asked myself many times: What do I write about in the next Iterative Thinking edition? I haven't read something that connects the dots to create an aha moment or a eureka to write about, and if we add that I've been reading less, things get worse.
item.content:encoded:
This edition is to express what's happening to me on a personal level, and why perhaps I haven't been writing as much as I'd like.

Ed. 62

The truth is I've asked myself many times: What do I write about in the next Iterative Thinking edition? I haven't read something that connects the dots to create an aha moment or a eureka to write about, and if we add that I've been reading less, things get worse.

So, humbly and with no other intention than using this written resource called a newsletter to write my thoughts — which, even though this time they're not about design, are always present. Sorry, I can't stop thinking. Even if it doesn't look like it!

Subscribe, it's free!

So I'm going to tell you how my 2024 has been…

Since around February-March, work at the office has become intense, full of learning, challenging — I won't deny it — but at certain moments it's exhausting. Sometimes the last thing I want to know about is design. Yes, it happens to me too that I need to see other things to come back recharged and tell you "My Adventures in Design."

I'd love to tell you that during this period I have a new hobby, that I've learned something new that connects with design, or with my philosophy of life, but the truth is it hasn't been like that.

Most of the time I've just consciously decided to spend time with my family, take a breather, without having a clear objective. I try to live in the present without tormenting myself with guilt from the past and anxiety about the future. The intention is always there, but I don't always achieve my goal. Sometimes I do, and even though they're almost just instants, it's great.

The latest editions of this newsletter are invariably revelations I have while reading other things that clearly trace themselves mentally and I can put into writing without much effort, but it hasn't been an exercise that has repeated itself too many times.

Also, courses are something that have kept me busy in 2024. The course with Adrian Solca at Universidad Anáhuac de Mérida on UX/UI was wonderful and the results were more than interesting, making us want to have a second edition.

My immersion as a teacher at Colectivo23 in the Digital Product Design program for two consecutive editions, and my Design Systems Course which, by the way, already has its second edition, have also kept me busy.

Teaching is a noble activity where one is the student, but two are the ones who learn.

On the other hand, we're looking for dates for a second in-person meeting of the Design System Community - Chapter Ciudad de México. The first meeting in June was small but very motivating. The younger ones are interested in learning, and although that makes me feel older, it also fills me with enthusiasm — being able to contribute so that those who want to start in the industry can do so more easily (although it's never really easy). Subscribe here so you don't miss the next meeting, if you're in Mexico City and interested in Design Systems.

On top of this, I continue with my sporadic but consistent mentorship sessions on my ADPList. Well… now I understand why I haven't read as much 😂. I've prioritized other things and being honest, I don't regret it. I've learned a ton. Maybe the only thing I need to find is a way for this newsletter to continue its weekly editions, even if my reflections aren't always so loaded with "Design."

As the cherry on top, we have season 5 of The UX Rebels, and we're certain there will be a sixth.

That's all, thanks for reading me. See you in the next edition.

To read 👀

Shapeof .AI

Ximena Cuenca shared this great resource with me — a collection of UX patterns for products that implement artificial intelligence. Save this page in your bookmarks, because if you're on AI projects you'll use it as a reference.

To listen 🎧

Doubt - Keinseier

I discovered Keinser on Instagram randomly while scrolling through the reels the social network recommends. The only thing I know is that he's a German musician with a YouTube channel with interesting ambient sessions, and this track is on Spotify.

Bonus 🎧

Second edition of my Design Systems Course

The course has a strategic focus 😎. This will help you convince teams of stakeholders, POs/PMs, and developers to collaborate and achieve adoption of a Design System. Don't miss out, reserve your spot now and if you have any questions, don't hesitate to contact me to make sure you'll get value from the course.

item.title: AI designer. The market's obsession with labels
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: More than a year ago I predicted that the "Prompt Designer" role wouldn't have much of a future as a specialization within design. Since then, we haven't seen this role flourish.
item.content:encoded:
First prompt designer, now AI designer.

ed. 63

More than a year ago I predicted that the "Prompt Designer" role wouldn't have much of a future as a specialization within design. Since then, we haven't seen this role flourish.

What we have seen is how, little by little, designers and other roles have started adopting artificial intelligence tools. Designers know how to use prompts, some of them have done explorations with more than interesting results, but they're not known as Prompt Designers.

Beyond the development of techniques and basic rules for creating prompts, the tools keep improving rapidly. This has led many, out of fear of being left behind (FOMO), to try to keep up, and a market has emerged offering courses to turn designers into supposed "Artificial Intelligence (AI) Designers".

However, the title "AI Designer" is ambiguous. Shape of AI classifies Artificial Intelligence (AI) designers into three types:

  • Designers who incorporate AI into their workflow. They experiment with tools like ChatGPT or MidJourney to enhance their work.
  • Designers who create AI tools. They develop applications based on technologies like large language models (LLMs) in conversations, or generative adversarial networks (GAN) for image generation, ensuring they're accessible through intuitive interfaces.
  • Designers of foundational AI interfaces. They redefine interaction paradigms to facilitate user adoption of AI tools, including conversational experiences and spatial computing environments.

This classification suggests that there will probably be more designers who simply integrate AI into their work than those who develop AI tools or new paradigms.

Many designers won't shift exclusively to AI, but will incorporate AI-powered tools into their work. Although processes will change and we don't know exactly how yet, design has always been a resilient discipline that takes advantage of technological advances to be more efficient. But this isn't "AI Design"; it's simply Design. Design that evolves.

Developing artificial intelligence tools isn't very different from designing other products. The main difference lies in understanding the possibilities and limitations of this new technology and in avoiding techno-solutionism, that is, adding AI just because it's trending, without focusing on solving real problems.

It's essential to understand the ethical implications of these technologies, especially considering current problems like device addiction and the social impact of the applications we interact with. We must learn from past mistakes and prevent scenarios where things get out of control.

On the other hand, we'll need to understand other forms of interaction that users can use. Failed experiments like the AI Pin or Rabbit R are transitional steps toward the true applications and paradigm shifts in the use of these technologies. Designers must do a deep analysis of the real capabilities of the models, and as happened with the shift from desktop to mobile paradigm, they'll have to integrate other disciplines to connect dots that are fuzzy today. At its core, it's not about how to sell this technology, but about finding problems where this technology is a better or radically more efficient solution. Only perhaps in this layer could we talk about an "AI Designer".

This leads me to think that "AI Designer" may be more of a trendy label, a market response to the demand for professionals familiar with AI tools. But in the end, it's about designers who fully understand the context of what they're designing, who are constantly learning and moving in scenarios full of uncertainty. Putting AI before the designer title is just a signal that we're focusing on the solution as a differentiator and not the problem to solve.

In summary, although we're adding more tools to the design repertoire, I don't think the title of "AI Designer" sounds justified yet — it still feels distant. The future is not yet written, and I'm sure there will be more advances in the way we interact with AI-powered tools, and tools created from their inception with AI. I also think there's a latent bubble that could burst. AIs still promise a lot and deliver little, and the hype has produced a generation of companies and/or startups that will see their true validation when seeking long-term profitability. Perhaps in the near future, the scope will be clearer and we'll understand how our way of working will change.

See you in the next edition.

Para Ver 👀

Sentient Design: AI and the next Chapter of UX

Josh Clark is working on a book called Sentient Design, which will be published in 2025. Here's an introduction about it.

Para escuchar 🎧

Perspective- Klsr

item.title: Figma with UI3, and designers showing their resistance to change
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: I hate by the book. For years I've understood that what theory says is more of a framework than something to take as recipes. Sure, it's valid to learn from recipes at the beginning, but with time and as we develop more experience, we realize that reality has the bad habit of having nuances—we'll ne…
item.content:encoded:
Change is so present in design, and it should be in the tools we use.

ed. 64

I hate by the book. For years I've understood that what theory says is more of a framework than something to take as recipes. Sure, it's valid to learn from recipes at the beginning, but with time and as we develop more experience, we realize that reality has the bad habit of having nuances—we'll never find a case exactly as a book describes.

This makes us realize that change, or uncertainty, is very present in design. Having unique cases, it's on us to be more than critical—to use our toolkit, maintain an open attitude, and be willing to make mistakes quickly and learn.

We'll probably have to deal with teams that don't clearly understand the value of design, and we'll have to evangelize. It's not easy to convince teams to be open, to try new ways. As creatures of habit, we like doing things the way we're used to, because we believe it's more painful to learn a new way, even when it's more efficient—we prefer to keep inefficient processes.

It strikes me as very strange that as designers we tend to show this tendency toward habits. We can defend design styles and even frameworks tooth and nail, we project our identity onto the tools we use, as if the tool were an extension of the result.

Figma just announced that their UI3 version will be available to everyone starting October 10, and in every post about it there are designers trying to stay on the previous version. This really catches my attention. I jumped on the beta as soon as it was available and although there is a small curve to get used to the change, the truth is there's no major problem after a week of using it.

I don't judge anyone who prefers the previous version. My reflection is that if you work in UX, a part of you should be a kind of explorer by nature, an early adopter, willing to try new paradigms to maintain openness and freshness in your solutions. We talk about doing things differently, but we want to keep the way our tools work unchanged forever.

UXers, as Omar Tosca says, should be the first to understand that rejection is the first response to change, regardless of its quality (that could be another debate). It's curious how we ask users to face that rejection to change, but we're incapable of leading by example when it comes to changing our tools.

I've been through Photoshop, Illustrator, Tumult Hype, Sketch, InVision, Pixate, Principles, Origami Studio and Figma or Penpot. I've noticed changes in their evolution and in many cases their disappearance. I'm always optimistic about it. Are design tools a reflection of how we think, of how that thinking evolves? I'll leave it as an open question…

And yes, my current interests are no longer in trying the new tool still in beta. Maybe now I'm more interested in understanding problems and more conceptual topics around Design. But whenever I can, I like to try new versions. Actually, seeing Figma's redesign excited me, because in the end it's about subjecting your mental model to new forms, and that kind of mental exercise is what in many cases allows us to think outside the box.

UX has advanced so much that we'll hardly encounter software in interface design that doesn't appeal to previous mental models—see Jakob's Law. We complain about the boring internet, which in many cases will tend by rule toward the functional, the established, but we long for design processes and tools without change that work the same way forever.

Anyway, if you have no choice and have to switch to UI3, here's a great resource from Figma that will help you understand what changed.

See you in the next edition.

To Watch 👀

The crisis in Design and UX: What does it mean to be a UXer in 2024?

Adrian Solca is a design director and gives an interesting perspective on the crisis or not in UX design. As always, very much in his style, he gives us context on what has happened in recent years.

To Listen 🎧

Hier- Keinseier

item.title: "Good Taste" in Design Doesn't Exist
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: A few weeks ago I read a LinkedIn post saying that designers need to develop good taste, that knowing how to use Figma isn't enough, you need to spend hours developing your eye and with it the good taste to create quality designs.
item.content:encoded:
Not in any universal sense — context is always the key to evaluating design.

ed. 65

A few weeks ago I read a LinkedIn post saying that designers need to develop good taste, that knowing how to use Figma isn't enough, you need to spend hours developing your eye and with it the good taste to create quality designs.

The idea has been bouncing around in my head: is there a universal "good taste" that designers acquire through experience?

Experience in design as craft and as understanding contexts.

We need to understand that design as a craft, as I've said before, improves over time. I'm pretty sure I design better now than I did in my early years out of school. Practice (and I mean both the technical-manual process and the intellectual one) is a process through which, over time, you develop a kind of toolkit to arrive at solutions more efficiently in less time.

I've always held the perspective that as designers, our work is based on understanding the context where we're going to act — we need to know our audience/users and how they move through their reality. We assimilate that reality to understand how to encode messages that can be correctly decoded by our receiver.

Clarification: I want to refer in a general way to message as "information that is transmitted" which in many cases will trigger behaviors, like using the designed thing or achieving something with the designed thing.

We can approach different types of contexts in each project we develop, since as designers, we're rarely the audience/users of what we design. Part of the problem to solve when designing is understanding if this context is foreign to us; so that, maintaining openness, we can begin the process of assimilating or learning the codes of that context, which will allow us to craft effective messages.

So we have these two axes: one where our structure of thinking and analysis gets reinforced through the practice of design, and another where the projects we've worked on have led us to learn from very diverse contexts, and with that our experience in understanding differences and similarities also gets reinforced.

We shouldn't confuse these two axes, since we can find designers who have done great work in the craft of design, but perhaps their experiences have been limited to certain types of products or specific audiences/users. This doesn't necessarily represent a disadvantage, since we're talking about profiles that can be specialists with deep knowledge in specific areas. Contrary to those who might be called generalists, who have had experiences in much more varied contexts, but where their depth doesn't reach the same levels.

The key of context in design

Until a few months ago I had a very clear idea about design, at least in the UX space and with UI Roles: designers should leave their personal tastes out, and focus on objectively understanding the codes to create efficient messages; it's not about the designer, but about the designed thing, my idea dictated. The problem that my teacher (and friend) Omar Mendoza pointed out to me, is that we cannot detach ourselves from the bias that our own context gives us.

Now I believe we cannot set aside our context and biases. Being aware of them is important so they don't influence us randomly; but avoiding them is only an aspiration, not a reality. We cannot design and be outside the context because we are always immersed in it.

"Good taste" as social consensus

Under the premise that designers work by assimilating contexts, we must understand that these contexts are social consensuses, not universal ones. In many cases these realities will have values that oppose each other, where what is well-regarded in one won't be so in another.

For the designer, looking at these differences will force them to understand cultural relativism and avoid ethnocentrism. This leads us to the conclusion stated earlier that design must be evaluated in its context and any absolutist exercise to evaluate it will be inefficient.

Conclusion

So, is there "good taste" in design? The short answer is no. What there are are consensuses that work only at a cultural level and never universally. Even within the same social group that consensus might not be homogenized due to different factors, such as generational differences among its members.

That's why it's important that when we're designing we question whether our experience has led us to fully understand the context we're designing for, and whether we're not simply replicating the codes of what constitutes good design in certain contexts, assuming these codes always work regardless of where we apply them.

And, on the other hand, understand whether, when we evaluate design, we have sufficient tools to understand the context, its codes and values that allow us to understand the consensus under which the design is good or not.

See you in the next edition.

Avisos🎙️

Third edition of my Design Systems course.

The third edition of the Design Systems: Strategy and practical fundamentals course is now open, so if you want to know about everything involved in convincing, starting, and pushing this initiative, this is the course you need. We won't talk about Figma, but about what happens outside your component library.

Any questions: contact me by replying to this email.
Para leer 📚

The "Global Design System" is nothing but a pipe dream

Henrik Ståhl gives us a good explanation of why Brad Frost's idea of a Global Design System has many challenges to face.

Para escuchar

Aurea-Arkitekto

item.title: Reevaluating Success in the Social Media Era
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: As we approach the end of the year, it's natural to do a retrospective on our goals. However, I firmly believe that any moment is valid to look back and reflect on what we like and what we want to change.
item.content:encoded:
Or how to apply a design approach to get to the root cause of our dissatisfactions.

Ed. 66

As we approach the end of the year, it's natural to do a retrospective on our goals. However, I firmly believe that any moment is valid to look back and reflect on what we like and what we want to change.

Exchanging messages with someone who is the author of THIS IS FINE where she talks about burnout and anxiety, I arrived at the following idea

In the social media era, we tend to constantly compare ourselves with others, which can generate a lot of anxiety. Personally, I've also fallen into this trap, comparing myself with friends who seem to have achieved more success, or be happier than me. It's easy to feel frustration and anxiety in those moments.

Exposing ourselves massively to others' triumphs can awaken our deepest insecurities: I'm not happy, I feel dissatisfied, I have a horrible job, or worse, I'm not valued. This only mentioning professional aspects, but if we go into personal territory, things can get worse.

Since my daughter's birth, I've had greater awareness of those fears, and I like to say I'm in chronic processes of discovery, reflection, and healing. The truth is that insecurities will come out, I'm an imperfect human, I'm going to screw up.

But above all, what I've thought about most is the concepts we socially learn and that often hurt us. Many of the attitudes we have toward money, social roles, standing out, doing your job well or following certain values, you learn from home, or sometimes you don't learn them; which, as we say in UX, the experience is going to happen, whether we design it or not.

One of the reflections that comes with this is: What do I want to teach my daughter about what it means to be happy? And that in turn leads me to reflect on understanding: What is happiness for me? Do I want to see her dissatisfied comparing herself like I have at many moments in my life?

The exercise should seek a genuine idea of what this or any concept will mean for us in our lives. Instead of measuring it according to social standards, we need to focus on an internal perspective.

If I don't have clarity about the meaning of these concepts in my life, social pressures will do their thing, making us believe that what everyone else seeks is what applies to us.

Applying the 5 WHYs of design to redefining success and happiness in life

As designers, our work is constantly under external scrutiny, being valued by clients, stakeholders and managers and even the end users of our products. The value of what we do is transferred to our value as professionals and in more extreme cases to our value as people.

Although this is just a mirage, and we must understand that what we produce is only a limited and biased reflection of what we are.

But what if we use a design approach and use the 5 WHYs framework to try to understand the origin of our conflict or dissatisfaction about success, happiness or simply not being where we want to be?

`
An example of a problem is: A designer feels professionally dissatisfied.

  1. Why? – They feel their ideas are not heard or valued.
  2. Why? – They have difficulty clearly communicating their concepts and proposals.
  3. Why? – They lack confidence and structure when presenting their ideas.
  4. Why? – They haven't received training or tools to improve their communication skills.
  5. Why? – Their team hasn't prioritized communication skills development as part of professional growth.

Root cause: The designer needs support to develop effective communication tools that allow them to express their ideas with clarity and confidence.`

Obviously, this example is quite generic, and the answer isn't always the organization or improving something in design. Maybe the root cause is a problem or difficulty we suffer from that affects us indirectly.

The point is to be honest, there are no good or bad answers, and this won't give us the solution. Understanding the why is only the first part, it's understanding the problem that then yes, we can seek to solve.

Reevaluating these concepts from a personal perspective, without letting yourself get carried away by external comparison, can be liberating. Ultimately, success and happiness are defined from within, driven by our own motivations.

So, this is an open invitation so that, every time you feel anxiety at other people's external successes, you channel that energy toward personal reflection. What does success mean for you? Or is it time to redefine that concept?

For me, something clear is that these concepts can evolve and change. At twenty years old, my way of seeing the world was very different from today. Why then shouldn't my concept of success, for example, evolve and change the same way I do? Even, perhaps in 1, 3 or 5 years, these same concepts should be reviewed and reevaluated, updated or changed.

If we assume a biological fact, none of the cells that make us up are the same as 10 years ago. Why hold on and want to forge unalterable concepts when in reality they are temporary and will evolve?

See you in the next edition.

P.S. You can always ask for help, so if these reflections taken to the personal realm overwhelm you, seek professional help, it's okay!

Announcements🎙️

Third edition of my Design Systems course.

The third edition of the Design Systems: Strategy and practical fundamentals course is now open, so if you want to know about everything involved in convincing, starting and pushing this initiative, this is the course you need. We won't talk about Figma, but about what happens outside your component library.

Any questions: contact me by replying to this email.
To read. 📚

Obsidian Vs. Apple Notes: Which Note-Taking App Is Better?

Currently, I'm venturing, for the second time, into creating my second brain, or in other words, seeking to create a system and flow that allows me to better remember what I read and make connections between ideas. I've discussed the topic with my friend Benjamín García, who is enthusiastic about simply using Apple Notes, and I, for my part, have ventured into using Obsidian.

If you want to get an idea of what Obsidian is, watch the following video

item.title: Not Everything Fits in a Design System: Not Even If You Know How to Make It Fit, Trust Me!
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: This week I just finished the third edition of my Edison course — and by the way, the cohort was really great, and I had a blast with the kinds of questions they had, bringing freshness and dynamism to each session. Some of the recurring questions inspired this post.
item.content:encoded:
The trap of wanting to over-systematize.

Ed. 67

This week I just finished the third edition of my Edison course — and by the way, the cohort was really great, and I had a blast with the kinds of questions they had, bringing freshness and dynamism to each session. Some of the recurring questions inspired this post.

The obsession with systematizing

Many of us designers who focus on Design Systems understand how efficiency and consistency are key metrics for measuring the success of this initiative.

Systematizing, unconsciously, gives us a certain peace that counterbalances the uncertainty and constant change that creating digital services and products brings. Design decisions that are made once, well thought out, and reused from then on.

It's tempting to systematize down to the smallest detail in our design system. The illusion is that if everything is systematized, it's controllable, manageable, and predictable.

But reality has a habit of being complex. While a design system can have foundations that allow us to make atomic components, then build composites, molecules, organisms, and templates — there will be elements we use to build interfaces that won't necessarily be part of our system and will tend to be more customized, and therefore less generic and reusable.

Photographs and Illustrations

Illustration and photography collections are proof of this. Although generically we can create backgrounds or memorable moments with customized details in components, and even include logic to make them dynamic depending on, for example, the weather, the season, or brand timing — the fact is that in many other cases illustrations and photographs serve more specific and unique purposes, where a generic illustration would fall short.

The same happens with photographs, where even reusing them can work against us, reducing dynamism, freshness, and spontaneity in memorable moments.

An alternative is to define principles that allow for consistent construction, but not always systematic, granting creative freedom to focus on specific moments that won't always be reused, but that let us extend the consistency we already have in our design system.

Understanding velocity: experiment in products, not in your Design System.

When we work on digital products, change is constant and iterativity is what allows us to validate quickly to find business models.

In the same eagerness to systematize, we can fall into the error of trying to include experiments in our design system. This can cause the Design System to become a bottleneck, because the required changes will always exceed the design team's capacity.

In this case, as Josh Clark mentions in his article Ship Faster by Building Design Systems Slower, design systems moving slower can be an advantage, since they can learn from the changes and evolutions that the product has, and when these become stable, they can be taken, evaluated, and systematized by the design system.

"This also applies to components or patterns that the design system team plans to include in the library in the future, but cannot commit to developing immediately without compromising the system's quality standards or considering all contexts beyond the product's immediate need"

When a product team needs a solution that the design system doesn't yet offer, this option exists:

  • The product team creates its own solution, using elements like atoms from the design system to create it.
  • The design systems team adds it to their backlog, ensuring its review and inclusion in the library.
  • The product team maintains responsibility for the component until the design systems team includes it in the library.

For this reason, experiments to validate hypotheses are not systematized. Design systems advance at a slower pace because they serve as the source of truth for long-term solutions.

So far, these are some examples of elements that impact our design but remain outside the system. Some foundations can guide the visual direction, but that doesn't mean these elements should be included in the design system.

When it comes to experiments and iterations in digital products, design systems teams must understand their capacity and recognize that their primary goal is to build and ship digital products that solve user needs. The iterative process moves faster because its goal is to reach market fit. The design system can leverage that evolution to systematize only the proven solutions.

Also, I encourage design system designers to understand their organizations, assess their needs, and find the right balance in their process. Decisions should be based more on specific needs and context than on recommendations from articles. These can serve as guidance, but should never be taken as strict rules.

In your opinion, what other elements do you think can stay outside a design system but are still commonly used to build interfaces?

See you in the next edition…

To read 📖

Simplify your product design — borrow familiar patterns

Ami Vora explores how simplicity in product design is not only desirable but a competitive advantage. A product is considered "simple" when it's familiar and intuitive, allowing users to interact with it effortlessly.

To listen 📻

Tatooine - Jeremy Messersmith

item.title: When Design Gets Automated: What's Left for Designers?
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: It's evident that, as designers, our way of working has experienced significant changes. The pandemic put many services to the test and showed us it was possible to run processes remotely. Collaborative tools gained new value by letting us work in parallel and optimize our workflows.
item.content:encoded:
A reflection on AI's impact, and the important questions we should be asking.

Ed. 68

The shift in how we design

It's evident that, as designers, our way of working has experienced significant changes. The pandemic put many services to the test and showed us it was possible to run processes remotely. Collaborative tools gained new value by letting us work in parallel and optimize our workflows.

Now, with the arrival of tools like ChatGPT, technological evolution opens up a range of possibilities that could transform how we design in unimaginable ways. This has sparked debates about whether Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools could replace us or, on the contrary, if they can help us expand our capabilities and improve our efficiency.

But the design process is complex—you need to understand context, evaluate needs and constraints. You can get inputs very quickly, but that doesn't always mean outcomes will arrive fast and furiously. An efficient design process can't always be measured by focusing only on time reduction, because there's no point in being fast if our solution ends up being wrong.

Beyond the prompt: the mirage of automation

For many, the prompt has become a kind of magic solution. We fall into the illusion that the tool, by itself, is enough to get exceptional results. The immediacy with which attractive images and elaborate responses are generated seduces us, making us believe that the time invested in getting good results is less or, worse, that talent can be replaced by efficiency and experimentation.

The paradigm shift: redefining the designer's role

The change in how we design has already begun, and there's no doubt that AI tools let us reach unprecedented levels of ideation in record time.

Beyond their technical capabilities and evolution, there are fundamental questions that keep me alert:

  • • If the designer becomes a kind of curator, how do we ensure future generations develop good taste and the necessary experience without constant practice?
  • • How will our conception of design change when AI is deeply integrated into our processes?
  • • If efficiency increases, will we need fewer designers, or will demand for design keep growing?

Every transformation in production processes has brought a redefinition of roles. It happened to graphic design with the arrival of computers and editing software: certain manual steps disappeared, tasks were automated, and new specializations emerged.

The photography industry is another example. Today, finding film rolls is complicated and in some cases impossible. Professional cameras increasingly adopt the mirrorless approach, and mobile devices and digital photography changed how we consume and take photos. Today it's easier to show a screen to view a photograph than to have a bookshelf with printed photo albums.

Where mastery with the X-Acto knife and airbrush was once fundamental, today it's unthinkable to work without specialized software. Maybe, in the near future, prompts and AI agents become essential tools for design ideation and execution.

AI and design: Integration or replacement?

"If artificial intelligence automates the technical part (...), then we should focus less on 'how to do it' and more on 'doing the right thing.'"

The problem is even more complex due to the diversity of contexts. While some organizations have matured in their approach to design, others are just beginning to recognize its value. These differences will make the way AI is integrated into processes radically different.

In some places with low design maturity, it will be more convenient (in an economic sense) to use AI tools and, in any case, hire a technical operator, or someone from the team itself could develop that technical skill. Needs in these organizations tend to be utilitarian and rarely strategic, and therefore the designer runs the risk of being replaced.

In other cases, there will be places with budget and design maturation, with already formed teams and clear processes for execution, as is the case with enterprise companies. However, changing design processes requires integrations with security and privacy—here AI integrations won't happen until stable solutions exist and the design cycle is complete. Changes in process always mean an investment for the organization, and these must guarantee cost reduction in the medium and long term.

It's possible that in certain environments we'll see a wave of designer layoffs due to the use of automated services, followed years later by a market correction, where it becomes understood that absolute solutions rarely work.

We'll also find contexts where the designer isn't replaced but enhanced by AI, playing a more strategic role.

The business behind AI: ethics vs profitability

Lastly, there's another factor we shouldn't forget: companies developing AI are seeking profitability. The history of computing has shown us that the best solutions or standards don't always prevail; often, the market favors less efficient but more profitable options.

While the debate about AI in design usually revolves around its impact on professional roles, there's another level of discussion we can't ignore: the design of the artificial intelligences themselves. There are approaches like Human-Centered AI (HCAI), which seek to ensure these technologies are accessible, equitable, and free of bias. In theory, this should guide the creation of more inclusive and transparent products. However, in practice, many decisions in AI development are conditioned by the need to obtain short-term profits, which pushes these ethical priorities to the background.

This already happened in other areas, like the design of algorithms for social networks, where optimization for engagement ended up prioritizing content that generates polarization and misinformation, because these generate more interaction and engagement for platforms than daily journals.

With generative AI we could see a similar pattern: the promise of accessibility and democratization of creativity could be overshadowed by models designed to favor commercial interests over user wellbeing.

As designers, we shouldn't only think about how AI affects us in our processes, but how we influence its development. If we know that economic incentives can compromise the ethical design of these tools, what role should we play in building an AI that truly helps people?

Conclusion: AI and the future of design

The impact AI will have on design isn't a phenomenon that can be tackled with simple answers. That's why my best suggestion is to not fall for extreme statements or sensationalist discourse that only seeks clickbait and immediate reactions. I myself have fallen into that temptation.

The discussion shouldn't focus on whether tools will incorporate AI, because eventually they will. Nor on debates about titles like AI Designer. The real question is: what impact will AI have on our profession and how can we leverage it consciously?

That's why it's fundamental to analyze our context, stay informed and, above all, keep being reflective and critical about the changes that are coming.

See you in the next edition.

Para Ver 👀

Do All AI Models Need To Be Assistants?

While most AI models default to a "helpful assistant" mode, different dialogue frameworks could enable new types of AI interactions or capabilities. An agent that challenges you, or pushes you to improve your arguments by debating with you?

Para escuchar 🎧

Where you are- Halal beats

item.title: Design is not what you see, it's how you get there
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: Design is an activity in which expertise develops through practice over time.
item.content:encoded:
A reflection on what design implies beyond deliverables...

Ed. 69

Design is an activity in which expertise develops through practice over time.

This expertise isn't just about mastering technique, but about incorporating broader perspectives when tackling problems. We design better because we can connect patterns and ideas where we previously saw no relationship, because we understand faster what we don't know, to ask better questions, because we question the rules, because, basically, we strengthen our thinking muscle.

On the other hand, the technical expertise we develop with tools helps us make our work processes more efficient; that is, our way of designing.

Technological democratization grants access to tools that can be easily learned, but we regularly confuse this access to tools as synonymous with access to practice—in this case, design. Tools are only one part of the equation, and they can change and vary over time.

Having carpentry tools doesn't make you a carpenter. Knowing how to hammer nails, use a saw, or plane wood doesn't make you a carpenter either. What makes you a carpenter lies in understanding how to use those tools and selecting the most appropriate ones for a specific purpose.

Not everything is solved with a hammer or a component 😂 (hope you get the joke).

If we remove the protagonist role from our tools, we'll understand that what's truly valuable as designers is our way of thinking and our way of seeing the world. Tools (I'm not referring only to software) are just extensions of our capacity to imagine and structure solutions, but they're not the center of our process.

I believe design is a social activity, but I also believe that design as an activity and as a product (the designed thing) is not explicit. You can't expect people to understand the multiple decisions you made to get there just by looking at your design.

That's why I believe we must be explicit when talking about our design process. The designer must be able to articulate a discourse that reflects their mental process, and as their expertise grows, their clarity of thought will allow them to be more decisive—even to modulate these discourses depending on the audience.

This includes everything from stages where we want to understand the problem to solve, to when we implement our solution. At all times, the designer must be an active interlocutor, who helps with questions, but also helps us have clarity about explored options in their ideation processes.

When a designer is embedded in a team, their activities are probably limited in scope. But even if we only see a part of the process, we must understand the complete flow to design with purpose, to have resources that help build these discourses that make explicit the part of the design process they were responsible for.

"In design, if you can't explain it, it's because you don't fully understand it."

Ulises Arvizu

So, beyond the ability to use our tools—which undoubtedly impacts the quality of our deliverables—part of our process of making design should be having mental clarity at all times about what we're doing and why we're doing it. With that, articulating discourses to explain our process, asking the right questions, or presenting possible solutions or final solutions will be a simpler task. Our job as designers always includes saying what we did and why we did it. If we do it eloquently and make an effort to do it clearly, our impact and visibility on the team will be evident.

See you in the next edition.

To read

A system for Writing - Bob Doto

I've been diving into this very interesting book about a writing process. I'm looking to approach essay writing, so I started reading and applying the Zettelkasten method to collect my ideas in a more structured way. This book by Bob Doto rounds out many concepts and provides a very simple guide on how to take notes and turn them into broader artifacts like articles or essays.

To listen 🎧

Spun day (Jamie Stevens Y2K Remix) - Spencer Brown

item.title: Vibe Designing: when we let AI decide for us.
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: Automating design processes with Artificial Intelligence (AI) is no longer a future possibility—it's already happening. Jakob Nielsen anticipates that designers of the future will be more orchestrators than implementers: we'll think more about what needs to be done than how to do it. In essence, we'…
item.content:encoded:
Designing isn't just about generating ideas—it's having the judgment to know which ones to discard.

Ed. 70

Automating design processes with Artificial Intelligence (AI) is no longer a future possibility—it's already happening. Jakob Nielsen anticipates that designers of the future will be more orchestrators than implementers: we'll think more about what needs to be done than how to do it. In essence, we'll leave many manual tasks to automated systems.

While I agree with that vision, I think there's a dangerous simplification in assuming that no longer implementing manually means we can completely forget about the "how." The mistake is not understanding that implementing design is also making decisions.

When we design, we don't just produce deliverables; we map out multiple paths, explore ideas through sketches, concepts, and prototypes. This divergent phase—generating many ideas—is fundamental. Because, as they say, you need to have many ideas to have good ideas.

Then comes convergence: deciding what stays out, which options can evolve, which are viable. That's the part of the process that can't be fully automated, because it requires judgment, experience, and contextual sensitivity.

AI can accelerate divergent processes, generate dozens of options in seconds, but deciding which ones are useful or feasible remains a deeply human task. And that judgment, until now, has largely developed through direct experience: trial and error, creation, adjustments, observation.

The problem is, if we let younger designers skip that experimentation stage, how will they develop the judgment needed to distinguish between a functional option and a decorative one? How will they know when the model is overtrained and generating solutions that only appear to be useful?

That's where a new phenomenon emerges: Vibe Designing. Designing by going with what "feels right" without foundational backing, without really understanding what you're doing. Assuming that using AI models means you no longer need to know about design, or theory, or fundamentals. But if you don't have the judgment to discard, design becomes an accumulation of possibilities without direction.

"Designing isn't just knowing how to use Figma, just as programming isn't just copying and pasting snippets from Stack Overflow."

Fundamentals are still part of the designer's toolkit. They're what allow you to select, adjust, prioritize.

Those who today focus only on craft will also need to develop new skills: socializing decisions, having ownership, and understanding the impact of their work on the organization.

(function() {
const iframe = document.currentScript && document.currentScript.previousElementSibling;
if (!(iframe instanceof HTMLIFrameElement)) {
return;
}

window.addEventListener('message', function(event) {
  if (event.source !== iframe.contentWindow) {
    return;
  }

  const data = event.data;
  if (!data || data.type !== 'substack-instagram-embed-resize' || typeof data.height !== 'number') {
    return;
  }

  if (data.height > 0) {
    iframe.style.height = data.height + 'px';
  }
});

})();

On the other hand, it's not enough to say that strategic designers are safe. If there's no one implementing design with judgment, strategists will also have to take on the role of curating the options generated by AI. And not everyone is prepared. Being strategic isn't enough if you don't have a deep understanding of design theory.

If strategic designers also delegate that responsibility to the models, they too will end up doing Vibe Designing.

In a world where systems offer multiple possible paths in record time, the true value of design lies in choosing with intention, not generating by volume. Automating doesn't mean abdicating responsibility. The future of design won't be just technical or strategic: it will be, above all, profoundly human.

See you in the next edition

To read 📖

Practicing CRAFT: Keeping Ahead of the Machine

As designers face the uncertainty of AI-driven change, the question isn't whether machines will take over, but whether we'll remember what it means to be human. John Maeda brings us his definition of CRAFT: a set of principles that keep design anchored in human intelligence.

To listen 🎧

Twist - Underworld

item.title: Automating is not the same as understanding
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: The promise of automation has been with us long before artificial intelligence existed. From washing machines to Excel macros, we've trusted technology to reduce effort on repetitive tasks and free up human energy for more important things.
item.content:encoded:
Toward a shared intelligence that strengthens human collaboration

Ed. 71

The promise of automation has been with us long before artificial intelligence existed. From washing machines to Excel macros, we've trusted technology to reduce effort on repetitive tasks and free up human energy for more important things.

Automating the mechanical, the predictable, what doesn't require thinking twice—it makes sense. It's a way of protecting our cognitive resources. Delegating to a machine what wears us down without adding deep value lets us focus on what truly matters.

But in the age of generative AI, the question is no longer whether we want to automate the repetitive… The question is: do we also want to automate what we don't understand?

Note: when I talk about automating in this context, I mean using AI

More and more AI tools promise to help us with tasks we don't master: writing code, visual design, or proposing creative solutions. Everything seems possible… even when we have no idea where to start.

But there's a risk hiding behind this delegation without criteria:
