If you don't have the experience to judge a decision, you also don't have the experience to judge whether AI is making a good decision for you.

Would you let a doctor who has never operated in their life operate on you? One who has only worked alongside colleagues who know how to do it and claims to have a general idea, but now trusts AI to guide them step by step.

AI can assist. It can amplify. But it cannot replace judgment when there isn't even judgment to supervise.

Do we want efficiency by avoiding collaboration?

When designers want to avoid developers, when devs dodge design, or when a PM wants to build a product without their complementary roles… there's something deeper than a preference for efficiency.

What we're seeing is a disguised version of an old aspiration: avoiding the friction of working with others, the dream of absolute independence. But that vision starts from a flawed premise: that collaboration is a burden.

Collaboration, properly understood, is a multiplier of intelligence and perspective.

Using AI to avoid that collaboration is like covering your ears and saying: "I'll just do it myself with my magic tool." But products aren't just functional deliverables. They're agreements. They're context. They're shared decisions that live in the seams between roles.

Human-centered AI: designing to amplify, not replace

The Human-Centered AI (HCAI) approach starts from a truth as simple as it is powerful: people don't want to be replaced by technology, they want to be empowered by it.

"Humans have always been toolmakers and now they are super-toolmakers, whose inventions can improve our health, family life, education, business, environment, and much more."—Ben Shneiderman, Human Centered AI

Shneiderman proposes that the true value of AI is not in its autonomy, but in its ability to become supertools: technologies that amplify our human capabilities—our creativity, judgment, social connection, and responsibility.

The HCAI approach doesn't seek to substitute human thinking, but to expand it. To design systems that don't make the user invisible, but keep them informed, in control, and with a voice. The key is finding the right balance between advanced automation and meaningful human control.

This means:

  • Using human-centered methods: observation, iteration, evaluation of human performance.
  • Designing understandable interfaces, with clear feedback, where the user can anticipate what the system will do.
  • Creating tools that don't impose themselves, but accompany human intention.

The Human-Centered AI framework reminds us that behind every intelligent system there are people: designers, developers, leaders, and above all, users. And that all of them deserve to participate in design that:

  • Recognizes human complexity,
  • Makes space for diversity,
  • Maintains dignity as a design principle.

The goal is not to build machines that think like us, but tools that help us think better, decide better, create better.

Toward a shared intelligence

The future is not about choosing between humans or machines. It's about creating forms of shared intelligence that help us move forward together.

We don't want systems that act on their own, without giving us explanations. We want tools that help us collaborate better. That make the connection between people more fluid. We want AI that, instead of replacing collective work, strengthens it.

Because automating is not the same as understanding. And building technology without understanding what makes us human… is not progress. It's omission.

Postscript for designers, leaders, and technologists: The real challenge isn't what AI can do. It's what we decide to do with it. And that, still, is a human decision.

See you in the next edition…

Para ver

Meet liquid Glass

This video is very interesting—whether we like Apple's new idea to unify their operating systems and feel less distant from Apple Vision Pro is another discussion: Liquid Glass. I've noticed that all the visual contrast failures happening in OS 26 occur for one simple reason: Apple isn't following their own Liquid Glass rules.

Para escuchar 🎧

Lemongrass - Lofi Cafe

item.title: Delegating Tasks with AI in Experience Design
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: It's undeniable how the collaboration paradigm has been changing. Integrating AI into our workflow can be exciting, but we also need to be transparent: in many areas, AI still promises more than it delivers, expectations create hype that in many cases is just noise.
item.content:encoded:
A framework proposal for understanding how and when to use AI

Ed. 72

It's undeniable how the collaboration paradigm has been changing. Integrating AI into our workflow can be exciting, but we also need to be transparent: in many areas, AI still promises more than it delivers, expectations create hype that in many cases is just noise.

In experience design we're seeing the first iterations of this collaboration: from tools like Figma AI, Lovable, or Replit, to Maze AI. However, most work well in small teams but face significant barriers for mass adoption and even enterprise adoption, especially data privacy and inconsistency in complex results.

For designers, the challenge isn't whether to adopt AI, but how to do it strategically. Understanding which tasks to delegate to AI models, and which to keep under human control, is the difference between productive collaboration and a dependency that limits our professional value. The key question isn't "Can AI do my job?" but rather "How do we redefine collaboration to enhance both capabilities?"

Solo Tasks, Delegated Tasks, and Automated Tasks in the experience design process.

Ethan Mollick in his book Co-Intelligence makes a simple but effective distinction for understanding what kind of tasks we should or shouldn't delegate to AI.

Solo tasks. Tasks where there's no added value, either because we don't want to lose the personal touch, or because the mental exercise that task involves is fruitful and skipping it could make us lose experiences that help us keep growing our skills.

Delegated tasks. Those tasks where despite being executed by AI they need human supervision, but this means we invest less time compared to doing it ourselves.

Automated tasks. Tasks you leave completely in charge of AI and don't need review.

With that context, I've decided to apply these same principles to tasks in the experience design process. This is the exercise.

Solo Tasks: Creative and strategic interpretation/decision

In some cases, this type of task needs human-to-human collaboration and exchange of ideas as an essential element. The answers can't be evaluated as correct or incorrect in a binary way and we probably can't replicate previous solutions directly.

That's why we talk about creativity to connect and interpret specific contexts, offering novel alternatives, and about strategy to understand how this can influence our process and/or result in the medium and long term.

That human synthesis is required to make decisions understanding the implications and responsibilities.

Also this kind of tasks allow us to grow and develop expertise in our work, which is why I'd like to emphasize understanding that many times skipping this task can make us lose control and not have all the necessary inputs for decision making and we don't become more expert.

Some examples:

  • Context understanding
  • Insight declaration
  • Problem definition
  • User archetype creation
  • Visual ideation (layouts, palettes, typography)
  • KPI and success metrics definition
  • Co-creation workshop facilitation
  • Scope and resource negotiation
  • Team design critiques.

Delegated Tasks: AI processes + Human interprets/decides

We're talking about those tasks where AI processes information efficiently. It's key to understand how to structure the data we want to process, assigning parameters that help AI model the result according to our needs. Prompting skills allow us to structure instructions that will help us get the expected result.

Processing and analyzing information is only one part that's complemented by human interpretation and decision-making, which acts at a more tactical and operational level. Human supervision can find errors in the processing and structure of information, can connect and relate data, by understanding much better the specific attributes of context, like the organization's culture and the project.

Some Examples:

  • Creating interview script drafts
  • Collecting samples for competitive benchmark
  • Creating wireframes
  • Creating components
  • Flow definition
  • Creating design documentation
  • Defining usability testing goals
  • Creating prototypes
  • Preparing research reports
  • Technical accessibility testing

It's worth noting that human supervision always makes the final decision, and their job is more about connecting and deciding, that's why it's necessary to have the required expertise for making these decisions, we can't decide on something we don't master.

Automated Tasks: AI applies objective criteria

As AI models get better, we'll be able to delegate more tasks without supervision. The challenge in this kind of task is providing AI with objective criteria it can use to make autonomous decisions.

Design contexts tend to be complex so sometimes it will simply be impossible to automate tasks, and AI inclusion will only be at the delegated task level, because of the ingredient of context singularity, culture and politics and implicit information that the data itself doesn't offer.

In other cases, being routine operations, these criteria are binary and easily reproducible. This allows documenting them clearly so the AI model uses them as consistent reference, eliminating human variability in repetitive tasks.

Some Examples:

  • Data collection and clustering
  • Documentation error review
  • Generating UI component variations (from style definitions)
  • Image and asset optimization
  • Test data generation
  • Design system consistency monitoring

My first idea when writing this post was to create a list of artifacts and place them according to our task division. However, due to each context's singularities, I see more value in offering a path for each designer to do a reflection exercise, about what type of tasks they have at hand and if they can integrate AI.

This is my proposal:

How do you know what type of task you have at hand?

To make it easier to decide, you can follow these questions:

1. Does this task need your creativity and will it impact strategy?

If the answer is YES → it's a solo task

Basically ask yourself:

  • Do I need to talk with others to arrive at the best idea?
  • Is there no obvious "correct" answer?
  • Will this decision affect the product or organization in the future?

If it's NO, go to the next step

2. Do you need to understand the context or make decisions that aren't automatic?

If the answer is YES → it's a Delegated task (but supervise it)

In other words:

  • Can AI do the heavy lifting but I have to interpret what it means?
  • Are there several good options and I need my judgment to choose?
  • Is my project's specific context super important for deciding well?

If it's NO, go to the last step

3. Can you clearly explain how this task is done and is its success verifiable?

If the answer is YES → Automate it completely

That is:

  • Can I write clear instructions for what to do in each case?
  • Is it obvious when it's done well and when it's not?
  • Is it something repetitive that doesn't need interpretation?

What if you're not sure?

If you're not clear which category it goes in, think about whether you can:

  • Define more specific criteria to automate it
  • Or if it really needs your interpretation (delegated)
  • Or your creative touch (solo)

I hope this proposal helps you make more conscious decisions about when and how to incorporate AI in your design process, maximizing efficiency without compromising the strategic quality of your work.

The real context matters

It's important to recognize that although this framework proposal helps us categorize tasks, the real context can limit our decisions. Budget constraints, technical capacity, or organizational security policies can force us to keep tasks as 'solo' even though technically they could be delegated, or even automated.

Also, the progressive integration of AI will keep redefining not just individual tasks, but complete processes. Tools that reduce the gap between design and development, like Vibe Coding and even Vibe Designing, promise paradigm changes that will require new reflections about our roles and activities.

What do you think? It would be interesting to know if you can apply this framework proposal, what other questions come up for you when identifying each category? Your feedback on this would help me a lot, maybe if it solidifies and gets grounded we could go deep into this framework idea in detail.

See you in the next edition

To read 🤓

When Figma Starts Designing Us

A reflection on how design tools shape us, indirectly conditioning how we think and build interfaces.

To listen 🎧

Parallel - Hiroyuki Oda

item.title: Expertise: How AI Reorganizes Which Types of Work Become Valuable
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: In our previous edition we explored practical strategies for delegating tasks to artificial intelligence. We talked about how to identify which activities to hand off to automated tools and which to keep under human control to maximize productivity without sacrificing quality.
item.content:encoded:
Why automation doesn't just change what we do, but how valuable we become

Ed. 73

In our previous edition we explored practical strategies for delegating tasks to artificial intelligence. We talked about how to identify which activities to hand off to automated tools and which to keep under human control to maximize productivity without sacrificing quality.

However, I recently came across research from MIT that completely changed my perspective on this topic.

The paper "Expertise" (2025) by David Autor and Neil Thompson presents a framework that goes far beyond the simple question "what tasks should I automate?" What I find revolutionary is that it reveals something we rarely consider: how automation fundamentally reorganizes which types of knowledge become valuable.

And this seems crucial to me for those of us integrating AI into our workflows, because the implications go far beyond immediate productivity.

The Expertise Framework: A New Way of Seeing Automation

Autor and Thompson's central idea is simple but revealing: automation doesn't affect all workers uniformly—it simultaneously replaces experts in some occupations while augmenting expertise in others.

Their framework is built on three concepts:

  • Expertise Hierarchy: Tasks can be ordered from least to most expert. Workers with greater expertise can perform lower-expertise tasks, but not the reverse.
  • Task Bundling: Jobs combine multiple tasks at different expertise levels. To be productive in an occupation, you need enough expertise for the most difficult task.
  • Expertise-Displacing Automation: When a task is automated, it doesn't just save human labor—it eliminates the need for that specific expertise.

The Example That Changes Everything: Accountants vs Inventory Clerks

To understand how this works, let's consider two occupations transformed by computerization: bookkeeping clerks and inventory clerks.

Bookkeeping clerks saw computers automate their most routine tasks (recording transactions, reconciling balances), but they were left with complex activities: resolving discrepancies, analyzing patterns, making decisions about classifications. Result: higher salaries because the remaining work requires greater expertise, but fewer jobs because not just anyone can do it.

Inventory clerks saw some of their most specialized tasks get automated (price calculations, complex verifications), leaving them primarily with physical work of counting and storing. Result: lower salaries because the remaining work is more generic, but more jobs because more people can do it.

The same technology, opposite impacts. Why? Because in each case, automation altered in different ways the average level of expertise required for the remaining tasks.

Demystifying Automation in UX

This framework dismantles narratives we constantly hear in our industry. Like those visions of startups running with just a CEO directing specialized AI agents.

These ideas reveal a superficial understanding of how automation actually works. The "CEO with agents" myth assumes that supervising is simpler than executing, when the framework suggests exactly the opposite: effectively supervising specialized tools requires exponentially more complex expertise than executing individual tasks.

If we completely automate the creative process, the only remaining human role would be "selector among options"—a position that requires very little specific expertise and would therefore command low salaries.

The Question That Defines Our Future

But consider the opposite. What happens if we specifically automate the technical creation of wireframes, flows, and UI components, but keep strategic decision-making in human hands?

Here the tasks that remain require a significantly higher level of expertise:

  • Strategic connection: Translating complex business objectives into specific experience requirements
  • Systemic evaluation: Determining whether generated solutions maintain visual and interactive coherence across the entire system
  • Technical constraints: Understanding implementation limitations and making intelligent trade-offs
  • Quality judgment: Evaluating whether proposals actually solve identified user problems

Suddenly, the UX designer role transforms into something that requires a convergence of knowledge that could previously exist separately: product strategy, visual psychology, technical architecture, research methodology.

This transformation isn't automatic or inevitable. It depends entirely on how we decide to integrate AI into our processes. And that choice will determine whether we become more valuable or more interchangeable.

In the next edition we'll explore how to apply this framework strategically and how organizations can navigate this transformation to elevate their teams instead of commoditizing them.

See you next week.

Para ver 👀

B2B-agents available on Github

Alysson Franklyn has an interesting project that he himself defines as "A comprehensive list with 40+ agents (and growing) to scale your B2B startup or power enterprise teams". Follow Alysson on LinkedIn so you can stay up to date with each of his updates.

Para escuchar

Medasin - before ir begins

item.title: How to Make AI Elevate Your Value (Instead of Making You Replaceable)
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: In our previous edition we explored the Expertise framework from MIT that reveals how automation can simultaneously elevate or degrade professional value, depending on which tasks we decide to automate.
item.content:encoded:
The "Expertise" paper framework applied to experience design

Ed. 74

In our previous edition we explored the Expertise framework from MIT that reveals how automation can simultaneously elevate or degrade professional value, depending on which tasks we decide to automate.

We saw how accountants and inventory clerks had opposite fates with the same technology, and how this applies directly to our industry. Today we get practical: How do we apply this framework strategically to position ourselves on the right side of transformation?

The Forced Convergence Revelation

This is where the framework reveals something counterintuitive. Many UX professionals aspire to a future where we focus "only on strategy" while AI handles "technical execution."

But this scenario hides a trap.

To make effective strategic decisions about AI-generated outputs, we need to be able to evaluate them competently. This requires deep expertise in tactical and implementation knowledge—not to execute it, but to evaluate what the AI is touching.

You can't evaluate whether an automatically generated screen design is strategically correct without deeply understanding both visual design principles and technical implementation constraints. You can't supervise an agent creating flows without mastering information architecture and cognitive psychology.

The same can happen for UX Research—if we're going to create User Personas with AI's help, we need to understand the context, know about trends, principles of anthropology, etc.

These are just examples, but they illustrate a clear pattern: to supervise AI we need to master the fundamentals that underpin its outputs.

Repetitive tasks will tend to disappear. Roles that largely perform this type of task will disappear or transform. Roles focused on delegated tasks that need human supervision won't be safe either. Having only the judgment to decide probably can't justify a role; the solution is: that role must also acquire strategic tasks, or alternatively, the task of supervising AIs will be absorbed by strategic roles.

This is what the Expertise paper calls "forced convergence"—automation eliminates the possibility of being competitive by mastering a single domain, forcing us to develop deep competence in multiple domains simultaneously. Cognitive complexity grows exponentially because you need to make evaluations that require the intersection of multiple domains.

Being More Intentional About Automating

Understanding this allows us to be much more strategic about how we integrate AI into our work. Instead of randomly automating tasks that seem "automatable," we can be intentional about what to delegate to position ourselves in the right direction.

The key is to always maintain our presence as the ingredient that makes divergent decisions, evaluates systemic quality, and connects multiple knowledge domains.

Analyze your context: What are the competencies that would complement the equation? If your role today tends to be more on the implementation side, you need to incorporate strategic tools into your toolkit that allow you to go beyond your current role; if your role right now is strategic, maybe you need to incorporate tactical and technical knowledge (I don't mean software operation) that allows you to evaluate the options AI will offer you and detect anomalies and/or hallucinations.

Although in my example I play with strategic and/or operational roles, this convergence can apply to complementary roles: a PM who knows how to understand research reports, Developers who do UX, or Designers who can deliver prototypes in code.

For Organizations, Elevation Strategies

Organizations have the opportunity to consciously model where and how they integrate automated tools to influence the direction of their teams' expertise. If you're a design director, a manager, or a decision-maker in your organization, you can't just go with the flow—you need to be intentional about how your team or organization integrates AI and have a clear strategy.

Key strategic question: Do we want AI integration to lead our team toward greater expertise or toward commoditization?

If the goal is expertise elevation, you need:

Investment in convergent training: It's not enough to use AI tools that give us artifacts more efficiently. You need to develop expertise in complementary topics that supervising AI models requires.

Role restructuring: Create positions that explicitly reward convergent expertise instead of maintaining specialized roles.

Culture of informed experimentation: Encourage teams to try AI integrations with the specific goal of elevating skill levels—for example, strategic skills—not just accelerating existing processes.

The Other Side of the Coin

We've already reviewed a scenario where designers develop deep convergent expertise, become strategic supervisors, see significant increases in salaries and responsibility. The same tools can also lead to commoditization, depending on how they're implemented.

Scenario B - Commoditization: Designers become tool operators, select from pre-generated options without deep evaluation capacity, see salary stagnation.

The difference isn't in the available tools, but in how we choose to integrate them and what types of skills we decide to develop in response.

What This Means for Us

The expertise framework offers us something invaluable: a way to anticipate and influence how automation will transform our work, instead of simply reacting.

For UX professionals, the strategic question isn't "will AI replace me?" but "how can I integrate AI in a way that elevates my expertise instead of degrading it?"

For organizations, you have more control than you thought over how automation affects your team structure.

Automation isn't an external force that happens to us. It's a tool we can use intentionally to reorganize work toward directions we prefer. But only if we understand the deep mechanisms of how technology reorganizes expertise.

The future of experience design won't be determined by AI's capabilities, but by how intelligently we integrate those capabilities to amplify our human skills.

And that choice, largely, is still in our hands.

See you in the next edition.

To read

Outcomes Over Output: Design Systems and AI

Ben Callahan brings us this interesting article as part of his bimonthly research program, The Question, where he gathered a group of very thoughtful Design Systems professionals to talk about the role of AI.

To listen 📻

Enceladus- Sunny Lax

item.title: Vibe Coding: How Building an App in a Weekend Taught Me Why AI Won't Replace Us
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: I had a CSV file with over 800 links saved across almost eight years.
item.content:encoded:
Between maker enthusiasm and technical debt: why collaboration keeps winning.

ed. 75

It all started when Pocket disappeared.

I had a CSV file with over 800 links saved across almost eight years.

They weren't just URLs: it was my personal catalog, full of tags I used to search articles by topic, ideas, or moods.

In my close circle I was often known as the guy who always had articles on hand about Design and UX topics. My digital life in Pocket was my trick, but now that superpower was fading.

Over the last 3 years I've immersed myself in Personal Knowledge Management (PKM) and using Obsidian. Not long ago I discovered Obsidian Web Clipper, an extension that lets me save links as notes and add metadata with tags in a single click, just like Pocket.

The idea of creating notes from links I found interesting was very seductive. That way I wouldn't depend on any external service and would have everything backed up locally in my Vault. This could become my digital library, because just like before I could use tags to catalog information.

But what would happen with my digital life from before Web Clipper? The idea of losing my digital life encapsulated in those links, now just a file in CSV format, didn't sit well with me. At the same time, opening each URL, using Web Clipper, and saving notes one by one seemed unthinkable.

After researching and not finding any solution, free or paid, that would let me upload a CSV and convert each URL into notes with a format similar to Web Clipper, I wondered if with an LLM's help I could automate the task.

After a couple of hours, with Claude's help I concluded that the simplest approach would be to create a Python script.

My journey was just beginning. I'm a designer and although I can understand CSS, HTML, I don't program, I don't understand the details of making scripts or the environment to run them. That's when I discovered Google Colab and after going back and forth between Claude and Colab, I achieved my goal.

As it processed the links, many failed. After refining the code with Claude, adding validation mechanisms and error reports, the script successfully processed 788 links. Only 12 URLs required manual review.

Triumphant, the natural question now was: What if someone else could benefit from this script? I set out (under Claude's guidance) to upload everything to GitHub in a repository, so anyone who wanted could run the script.

But the barrier was too technical. This meant assuming that whoever used the script would need to know how to use the terminal, understand Python and how to run a script. I was asking too much of someone whose problem I was trying to solve.

I spent last weekend—intermittently, partly due to family activities and partly because I hit Claude Pro's maximum usage limit several times—developing a solution that would work via web.

The idea was simple: the user just had to upload their CSV and receive back a ZIP with their notes and their report. That's how RIL2Obsidian (Read-It-Later to Obsidian) was born. I deployed it through GitHub on Railway, and got a functional proof of concept.

But something bothered me throughout this triumphant process...

The duality of vibe coding

The first sensation of being a maker happened to me years ago, when I managed to prototype my first mobile app. It was before the InVision explosion and the emotion of seeing something functional, beyond static pixels, was indescribable.

Now that sensation comes with steroids, because you feel like you're solving problems. It's no longer just about the intention to do it but about making it possible.

But what really happens with vibe coding is that, when we're not developers, we fool ourselves.

I didn't program the script: I asked the AI to write the code that would do what I imagined.

I don't fully understand the decisions it made, I don't know if the code quality is good or if it could be optimized, scaled—and that could make the difference if we're talking about stages beyond validating a concept.

Here's the silent problem with vibe coding: technical debt. That code that works today but is built on decisions I don't completely understand. If tomorrow I need to add a feature, can I do it without breaking everything? Or will I have to ask the AI to rewrite entire sections because I don't understand how the pieces connect?

For a personal project like RIL2Obsidian, it's an acceptable risk. But imagine this in a product with real users, with sensitive data, with performance expectations. That debt collects with interest: bugs that are hard to trace, code impossible to maintain, architectural decisions that trap you in a corner.

I can't imagine infrastructure built on decisions that an AI makes where the owners of that infrastructure don't understand the nature of those decisions.

That said, the process was exciting. In fact, some people celebrate that we've entered the era of creating products solo over a weekend, like Derick David in his article Calendly is Doomed or Sherry Jiang in her post on X

It's not about blindly trusting AI. If you can't understand the decisions, you have two options:

  1. You fill that knowledge gap so you can supervise.

  2. You approach someone with the expertise to supervise that AI.

I have a hard time imagining replacing humans with expertise who use their judgment with AI models, however sophisticated they may be.

In my opinion, vibe-coding is an interesting resource for ideating and prototyping in code, even for doing what Lee Robinson calls Personal Software.

But one thing is making personal solutions and proofs of concept; another is scaling massive products. I firmly believe that the best products emerge from collaboration with teams that enriches vision and expertise, not from working solo.

The problem arises when we use AI as a guide instead of as support and think we can do everything alone.

As I mentioned in edition 74

"Using AI to avoid that collaboration is like covering your ears and saying: 'I'll do it myself with my magic tool.' But products aren't just functional deliverables. They're agreements. They're context. They're shared decisions that live in the seams between roles."

For me, this exercise left me with something positive: removing mental blocks to understand and put into practice development concepts I was always brushing against but always felt alien to.

Now I want to understand more about Python, understand much more about the structure and orchestration of a project. I'm even thinking about whether this solution might work better as a direct plugin in Obsidian, which means understanding TypeScript.

This exercise worked for me to learn new things, and to understand that if any of this scales I can always find human partners who can complement me where I lack knowledge.

Even using AI to automate. Because we can automate code generation but not the intention behind it.

See you in the next edition.

To watch

The Elements of UX in the Age of AI by Jesse James Garret

To listen 🎧

Particles - Keinseier

item.title: The problem isn't AI, it's how we simplify reality
item.author: ulises@iterativethinking.com (Ulises Arvizu)
item.description: Apologies for the months without writing. Between taking a good break at the end of the year and 2026 starting with many changes—moving and other news—I couldn't find the time to put certain ideas in order. But here we are, back again.
item.content:encoded:
Design requires context and nuance

Apologies for the months without writing. Between taking a good break at the end of the year and 2026 starting with many changes—moving and other news—I couldn't find the time to put certain ideas in order. But here we are, back again.

Ed. 76

One of the things that worries me most about the current AI hype isn't the technology itself, but how we talk about it.

I constantly see reflections, opinions, and experiences that are born in very specific contexts, but quickly become universal truths. As if what works in one team, one company, or one type of product automatically applies to everyone.

And from there, we build conclusions.

Conclusions about the future of design.

About the tools.

About whether they'll replace us or not.

About how we should work.

The problem isn't questioning. The problem is generalizing.

Today, most of the conversation lives on social media, videos, and content designed to be consumed fast… and shared even faster.

But virality doesn't reward precision.

It rewards the controversial.

And in that process, nuance gets lost.

It becomes easy to say:

"The design process is dead."

"AI will replace designers."

"You don't need research anymore."

"You just need to move fast."

These are phrases that sound definitive, but rarely come with context.

And without context, design stops being a discipline… and becomes an opinion.

There's something else on top of this: the need to pick a side.

We don't want to understand complexity.

We want to take a position.

So we defend opposing stances as if they were incompatible:

Process versus Intuition,

Rigor versus Speed,

Craft versus Automation.

And in that polarization, each side takes the most convenient version of the opposing argument… to dismiss it.

But design has never worked that way.

Design lives in the nuances.

The process isn't dead, and intuition isn't enough.

Saying the design process is broken is as simplistic as saying it must always be followed to the letter.

The reality is more uncomfortable.

Many processes have become checklists.

Rituals we follow without questioning.

Steps we execute more out of inertia than intention.

But on the other side, there's also a romanticization of intuition: moving fast, "solving," iterating without necessarily understanding the problem. That only makes us move fast toward wrong solutions.

And there design loses something essential: its capacity to make informed decisions.

Here's something that is a fact: artificial intelligence is changing the way we work.

It automates tasks.

Reduces time.

Eliminates friction in many parts of the process.

But that shouldn't automatically lead us to one conclusion: that now we "must" deliver faster.

Maybe the right question is different.

What do we do with the time we're recovering?

Because if time pressure stops being the main obstacle, then the value is no longer in speed.

It's in judgment.

We don't need designers who produce more. We need designers who understand better.

Judgment isn't knowing all the answers. It's knowing what questions to ask when no one else is asking them. It's the designer who, in the middle of a meeting where everyone's celebrating delivery speed, raises their hand and asks: but are we solving the right problem? No tool automates that. And in a context where everything moves faster, that pause is worth more than before.

See you in the next edition.

Reading list

Ux fo AI I'm rereading this great book that aims to give us an idea of how to create products that integrate AI capabilities. If you can, take a look (you can download the digital excerpt). Greg Nudelman has been running several workshops by the same name and this book collects his framework with everything learned during that time.

Estrategas de la intención by Adrian Solca this ebook will require a post in Iterative Thinking, so it's a must.

To watch
